name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads quick navigation tiles, current announcements and alerts, and audience-based entry points, and routes users into detailed pages (programs, lap lanes, memberships, parties, passes, pool schedule) with appropriate URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display quick navigation tiles for key tasks using getHomeQuickTiles, and allow users to navigate to task-specific pages (programs, lap lanes, memberships, rentals, passes, pool schedule).
Load and display announcements and alerts using getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts, with alerts surfaced in a high-visibility strip and announcements in a list or feed.
Load and display audience-based entry points (e.g., kids, adults, families) using getHomeAudienceEntryPoints, and route users into pre-filtered views of the Programs and related pages.

Handle navigation from tiles, audience entries, and alerts by constructing target URLs with query parameters (e.g., categoryId, subcategory, membershipType, duration, passType, format, desiredTotalVisits).
Reflect and control which Home section is emphasized (tiles, announcements, audience entry points) using URL parameters, without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Use a quick tile to find a specific program or task
1. User lands on index.html (optionally with a section or tileId parameter in the URL).
2. The home-page-controller component initializes and calls getHomeQuickTiles, getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts, and getHomeAudienceEntryPoints in parallel.
3. Once getHomeQuickTiles returns, the home-quick-tiles-grid component renders the tiles from the tiles array.
4. User scans the tiles and activates one (click or keyboard activation on a focused tile).
5. The home-quick-tiles-grid onTileClick handler reads the tile.targetPage and tile.id, looks up the routing configuration for that tile, and constructs the appropriate URL (e.g., programs.html with categoryId or subcategory parameters, lap_lanes.html, memberships.html with membershipType/duration, rentals.html with category, passes.html with passType/format/desiredTotalVisits, or schedule.html).
6. The browser navigates to the target page with query parameters in the URL; the destination page uses those parameters to pre-filter its own data.

Workflow: Start from an audience-based entry point (e.g., Kids Lessons, Adult Fitness, Family Activities)
1. User lands on index.html.
2. The home-page-controller calls getHomeAudienceEntryPoints.
3. The home-audience-entry-list component renders audience entries from the audiences array.
4. User activates an audience entry.
5. The onAudienceClick handler reads the audience.targetPage and audience.defaultCategoryId.
6. If the targetPage corresponds to programs, the handler constructs programs.html?categoryId=<audience.defaultCategoryId> (and may add additional parameters if configured for that audience, such as a subcategory for a specific program type).
7. The browser navigates to the target page; the Programs page uses categoryId (and any other parameters) to show a filtered list relevant to that audience.

Workflow: Check for closures or schedule changes and go to the impacted area
1. User lands on index.html.
2. The home-page-controller calls getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts.
3. The home-alert-strip component renders alerts from the alerts array in a prominent area.
4. User reads an alert; if the alert has a relatedPage value, the alert is rendered as an actionable item.
5. User activates the alert.
6. The onAlertClick handler maps alert.relatedPage to the corresponding target URL (e.g., 'pool_schedule' → schedule.html, 'lap_lanes' → lap_lanes.html). If needed, an alertId parameter is added to the URL so the destination page can highlight the relevant information.
7. The browser navigates to the destination page.

Workflow: Focus on a specific section of the Home page via URL parameters
1. An external page or bookmark links to index.html with query parameters such as section=audiences&audienceId=<some-audience-id>.
2. On load, the home-page-controller parses the URL query string.
3. After getHomeAudienceEntryPoints resolves, the controller passes the selected audienceId to home-audience-entry-list.
4. The home-audience-entry-list component highlights the matching audience card and scrolls it into view (if applicable).
5. If section=audiences was provided, the controller also ensures the audiences section is in focus (e.g., by scrolling to that section or activating an audiences tab).

Workflow: Return to Home and re-emphasize a specific tile or message
1. A user navigates back to index.html from another page with a URL like index.html?section=tiles&tileId=<some-tile-id> or index.html?alertId=<some-alert-id>.
2. The home-page-controller parses the query parameters.
3. Once getHomeQuickTiles and getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts have resolved, the controller passes tileId to home-quick-tiles-grid and alertId to home-alert-strip.
4. home-quick-tiles-grid highlights and/or focuses the tile whose id matches the tileId parameter. home-alert-strip does the same for the alert matching alertId.
5. The user can then choose a different tile or dismiss/ignore the previous context.
interactions:
Interaction: Activate a quick tile
- Trigger: User clicks a tile or presses Enter/Space while a tile has keyboard focus.
- Handler: home-quick-tiles-grid.onTileClick or onTileKeyPress.
- Behavior: Uses the tile data (id, targetPage, highlightedTask) plus a routing configuration to construct the target URL with appropriate query parameters (categoryId, subcategory, membershipType, duration, passType, format, desiredTotalVisits). Navigates to the resulting URL.

Interaction: Activate an audience entry
- Trigger: User clicks an audience card/row or presses Enter/Space while focused on it.
- Handler: home-audience-entry-list.onAudienceClick.
- Behavior: Reads audience.targetPage and audience.defaultCategoryId; builds a URL for the target page. For programs, appends ?categoryId=<audience.defaultCategoryId> and optional additional parameters if configured. Navigates to the target URL.

Interaction: Click an alert linked to another page
- Trigger: User clicks an alert that has a relatedPage value or presses Enter/Space on it.
- Handler: home-alert-strip.onAlertClick.
- Behavior: Maps alert.relatedPage to a known target page (e.g., schedule.html for 'pool_schedule', lap_lanes.html for 'lap_lanes'). Optionally appends alertId=<alert.id> to the URL so the target page can emphasize the relevant information. Navigates to the target URL.

Interaction: Expand/collapse an announcement
- Trigger: User clicks an announcement item or a dedicated control within it.
- Handler: home-announcements-panel.onAnnouncementToggle.
- Behavior: Toggles the expanded state for that announcement (showing or hiding the full body text). This state is local to the current page session; it is not stored in localStorage and is not encoded in the URL.

Interaction: Change Home section focus (e.g., via section tabs or in-page navigation)
- Trigger: User clicks a control that focuses a section (e.g., tiles, announcements, audiences).
- Handler: home-section-navigation.onSectionSelect.
- Behavior: Scrolls to the corresponding section and updates the URL's section query parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState (e.g., index.html?section=announcements). Does not reload the page.

Interaction: Deep-link highlight of a tile, audience, or alert
- Trigger: Page load with tileId, audienceId, or alertId query parameters.
- Handler: home-page-controller.onInit and respective child component handlers.
- Behavior: After data is loaded from the interfaces, the matching tile/audience/alert is highlighted and optionally scrolled into view. No additional API requests are made; the behavior is based solely on the initial interface responses and URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is driven via URL query parameters and in-memory component state. No localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies are used for functional state.

Supported URL parameters on index.html:
- section: Indicates which main section should be emphasized on load. Expected values are determined by the implementation (e.g., 'tiles', 'announcements', 'audiences'). The home-page-controller reads this parameter on load and instructs home-section-navigation (or directly scrolls) to bring the corresponding section into view.
- tileId: If present, after getHomeQuickTiles resolves, home-page-controller passes this id to home-quick-tiles-grid. That component locates the tile where tile.id === tileId, applies a highlighted state, and may scroll it into view.
- audienceId: If present, after getHomeAudienceEntryPoints resolves, home-page-controller passes this id to home-audience-entry-list, which highlights the matching audience entry and may scroll it into view.
- alertId: If present, after getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts resolves, home-page-controller passes this id to home-alert-strip, which highlights the matching alert.

URL state updates:
- When the user changes the focused section via in-page controls, the home-section-navigation component updates the section parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState, without reloading the page.
- When navigating away from Home via tiles, audience entries, or alerts, the target URL is constructed with parameters as defined in the page architecture:
  - To programs.html for kids-focused swim lessons: categoryId=swim_lessons_kids.
  - To programs.html for aqua fitness: categoryId=fitness_classes&subcategory=aqua_fitness.
  - To programs.html for lifeguard training: categoryId=training_certifications&subcategory=lifeguard_certification.
  - To lap_lanes.html for lap lane reservations: no additional required parameters.
  - To memberships.html for family memberships: membershipType=family&duration=one_month.
  - To rentals.html for birthday parties: category=birthday_party.
  - To passes.html for guest passes: passType=guest_passes&format=digital_pass&desiredTotalVisits=5.
  - To schedule.html for pool schedule: no additional required parameters.
These parameter values are part of the routing configuration and are not derived from localStorage; they are independent of any dynamic content and rely only on the tile/audience/alert metadata and known navigation rules.

Component-level state:
- Components maintain transient UI state (such as which announcement is expanded or which tile is currently focused for keyboard navigation) in memory only. This state is reset on page reload and is not persisted across sessions.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading from Home interfaces, interprets URL query parameters, wires data into child components, and manages section focus without using localStorage.

Uses getHomeQuickTiles to render actionable tiles for key tasks (programs, lap lanes, memberships, parties, passes, pool schedule) and routes users to the corresponding pages with appropriate URL parameters based on tile metadata and routing rules.
data_binding: Program (indirectly via categoryId and subcategory passed to programs.html)
MembershipPlan (indirectly via membershipType and duration passed to memberships.html)
PartyPackage (indirectly via category passed to rentals.html)
PassProduct (indirectly via passType, format, desiredTotalVisits passed to passes.html)

components.functionality: Uses the announcements array from getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts to display current announcements and seasonal updates, supporting expansion/collapse of announcement details.
data_binding: PoolScheduleSession (indirectly, when announcements describe schedule-related sessions)
Program (indirectly, when announcements describe programs or classes)

components.functionality: Uses the alerts array from getHomeAnnouncementsAndAlerts to present time-sensitive messages (such as closures or schedule changes) and allows navigation to related pages using alert.relatedPage.
data_binding: PoolScheduleSession (indirectly via navigation to schedule.html for pool_schedule alerts)
LapLaneSlot (indirectly via navigation to lap_lanes.html for lap_lanes alerts)

components.functionality: Uses getHomeAudienceEntryPoints to display audience-based entry points (such as kids, adults, families) and routes users to appropriately filtered views of the Programs or related pages using audience.targetPage and audience.defaultCategoryId.
data_binding: Program (via categoryId passed to programs.html for audience-selected views)
MembershipPlan (indirectly when audiences target memberships.html or related membership flows)

components.functionality: Controls which main section of the Home page (tiles, announcements, audience entries) is emphasized and synchronizes this focus with the section URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly linear, top‑anchored flow that mirrors the mockup: global utility bar and header/nav (handled outside these components), then a narrow full‑width alert strip, followed by a dominant hero‑like band of quick navigation tiles. Below this hero band, the main content transitions into a centered, editorial‑style column beginning with announcements, then audience‑based entry cards, and finally a low‑key section navigation band near the bottom of the initial scroll. All components sit within a centered max‑width container aligned to an implicit 12‑column grid, with each band spanning full browser width but constraining most content to the central grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly linear, top‑anchored flow that mirrors the mockup: global utility bar and header/nav (handled outside these components), then a narrow full‑width alert strip, followed by a dominant hero‑like band of quick navigation tiles. Below this hero band, the main content transitions into a centered, editorial‑style column beginning with announcements, then audience‑based entry cards, and finally a low‑key section navigation band near the bottom of the initial scroll. All components sit within a centered max‑width container aligned to an implicit 12‑column grid, with each band spanning full browser width but constraining most content to the central grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above‑the‑fold space is weighted toward the hero quick tiles: header + utility bar + optional alert strip together occupy roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height, while the quick tiles hero band consumes around 40–50% of the viewport. Announcements and audience‑entry content fill the remainder of the first screen with generous margins and line spacing for readability. Each major section (alert, tiles, announcements, audience entry, section nav) is separated vertically by approximately 32–48px, following the 8px spacing system. Within components, padding is typically 16–24px for cards and interactive tiles, keeping them touch‑friendly and visually substantial without feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains low to medium overall density. Structural elements (alert strip, announcements area, section navigation) are clean with plenty of white space and relaxed line height, echoing the editorial feel from the mockup. The quick tiles hero band is intentionally more visually dense and colorful—akin to a row of mini promotional posters—concentrating graphical complexity into a single horizontal strip so the rest of the page can breathe. Announcements are moderately dense (stacked cards or rows) but constrained to a central width to avoid visual noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the global logo and horizontal nav (outside these components), then immediately drops to the thin, high‑contrast alert strip if present. From there, attention is drawn downward into the bright hero band of quick tiles, scanning left‑to‑right across four primary tiles that route users to core tasks (programs, lap lanes, memberships, etc.). After scanning the hero, the gaze naturally moves down into the calmer central column where announcements provide narrative context, followed by audience‑specific entry cards. The flow ends in a subtle section navigation band that offers secondary pathways without competing with the hero tiles. The entire experience follows a simple F‑pattern at the top, then a vertical scroll with isolated, clearly separated bands.

layout.overall_description: The home page presents as a series of clean, horizontal bands stacked from top to bottom, set against a bright, family‑friendly white or very light background. Under the global header, a thin, full‑width alert strip slices across the page, immediately visible yet shallow. Just below, the page opens into a bold hero band where four colorful quick‑navigation tiles sit side‑by‑side inside a centered max‑width container, each tile roughly a quarter of the content width and styled like cheerful mini posters for key aquatic offerings. This hero band dominates the upper half of the viewport on desktop, creating an energetic first impression. As the user scrolls slightly, the energy tapers into a calm central column: announcements appear first, centered and comfortably wide (about two‑thirds to three‑quarters of the content width) with ample white space and relaxed line height. Below that, audience‑entry cards—such as options for families, lap swimmers, or seniors—are grouped in a structured grid, still colorful but less intense than the hero tiles. Near the bottom of the initial scroll, a low‑contrast section navigation band spans the width of the content, offering succinct links into deeper sections without overwhelming the page. Overall, the layout feels like a modern community hub: bright and inviting at the top, then progressively more informative and structured as you move downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the invisible structural frame for the entire home page. It establishes a full‑width background that stretches edge‑to‑edge across the browser, with all core content centered inside a max‑width container of roughly 1120–1280px. Within this container, an implicit 12‑column grid organizes the main bands: the alert strip, the quick tiles hero band, the announcements section, the audience entry grid, and the section navigation. Vertical spacing between these bands follows the 8px system, typically 32–48px of margin between sections so each band feels clearly separated. The controller ensures that the hero quick‑tiles band occupies approximately the upper half of the viewport (below the header and alert), while subsequent sections stack linearly down the page. On large screens, this keeps content centered with generous left and right margins; on smaller screens, the controller allows content to expand to nearly full width while preserving consistent internal paddings (16–24px) inside each component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top‑level container that defines the centered max‑width and 12‑column grid; participates in normal document flow without fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick tiles grid occupies the main hero band directly beneath the header (and beneath the alert strip when present). It spans the full browser width as a distinct horizontal band, but its content is constrained to the central max‑width container. Within this container, four primary tiles are arranged in a single row on desktop, each tile spanning roughly 3 of the 12 grid columns (about 23–25% of the inner width) with 16–24px horizontal gaps between them. The band’s vertical height takes up roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport, allowing each tile to appear tall and poster‑like, with vivid imagery, bold titles, and clear calls to action. The background of the band is slightly tinted (e.g., a very pale aqua or blue) to separate it visually from the white body background, reinforcing its role as the main promotional area. Vertical padding inside the band is generous (24–48px), ensuring the tiles are framed with breathing room above and below. On scroll, this entire hero band moves up naturally with the page; it is not sticky. On narrower screens, the grid collapses to two tiles per row and then to a single column, but the band maintains its identity as the primary, colorful entry point into programs, lap lanes, memberships, and other key offerings.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section band in normal flow, occupying a dominant hero area below the header; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The announcements panel sits immediately below the quick tiles hero band, separated by about 32–40px of vertical space to create a distinct transition from promotional content to informative content. It is centered within the same max‑width container but constrained to a slightly narrower column—approximately 70–80% of the container width—so that there are generous left and right margins, echoing an editorial text block. At the top of the panel, a prominent header (e.g., “Announcements” or “What’s happening at the pool”) spans the width of this inner column and is followed by a vertical list of announcement items. Each announcement appears as a card or row with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between items, giving the section a medium density: more information‑rich than the hero band but still airy and legible. Text is left‑aligned, with line height relaxed enough to be easily scannable for parents and community members. The panel’s background remains white to maintain a calm feel, possibly with a subtle top border or light drop shadow to distinguish it from the hero band above. On large screens, the announcements panel typically occupies the central band of the viewport when the user scrolls past the hero; on smaller screens, it extends closer to full width but preserves consistent padding from the page edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, central column section in normal document flow; scrolls with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The alert strip is a shallow, high‑visibility band that spans the full width of the browser directly below the global header and above the quick tiles hero band. When active, it appears as a narrow horizontal bar—roughly 56–72px tall—running edge‑to‑edge with a bold, attention‑grabbing background color (e.g., bright yellow, orange, or red) to signal closures, schedule changes, or safety messages. Inside this strip, content is aligned to the same central max‑width container as the rest of the page. The alert message text is left‑aligned near the top‑left of that container, with 16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, ensuring the text remains legible and not cramped. Optional action elements (such as a “View details” link or close icon) sit to the right within the same horizontal line, respecting the 12‑column grid. The strip maintains low height to avoid overshadowing the hero band, but its color and position at the top of the content area make it the first thing seen after the header. When no alerts are present, this band simply collapses, allowing the hero tiles band to shift up directly under the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band anchored just below the header within normal flow; appears or disappears based on alert presence but does not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The audience entry list is positioned directly below the announcements panel, separated by roughly 32–40px of vertical space so it feels like the next logical step after reading what’s new. It occupies the full width of the central max‑width container but often uses a multi‑column layout inside that container to group audience‑specific cards. On desktop, the cards are arranged in a grid of three columns per row, each card spanning about 4 of the 12 grid columns, with consistent 16–24px gutters between them. Each card has 16–24px internal padding, a modest drop shadow or colored border, and a more subdued but still playful palette compared to the hero tiles—supporting a friendly tone without competing for attention. Titles such as “For Families,” “For Lap Swimmers,” or “For Seniors” are prominent at the top of each card, with short descriptive text and a primary link or button leading to pre‑filtered pages (using URL parameters) for relevant programs or schedules. The section’s background remains white (or very subtly tinted) to maintain the overall clean feel, and there is about 32–48px of bottom margin before the next section, ensuring the grid feels contained and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the main content column, flowing naturally below the announcements panel; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation appears near the bottom of the main home content, directly beneath the audience entry list and separated by about 32–40px of vertical spacing. It functions as a low‑key, secondary navigation band that helps users jump to or into key site areas such as programs, memberships, passes, parties, and pool schedules. The band spans the full width of the browser, but its contents are constrained to the central max‑width container and typically aligned horizontally. Within the container, a row of compact navigation items (e.g., pill‑shaped buttons or text links with simple underlines) runs from left to right, centered or starting at the left edge of the central column depending on the chosen style. Each item has an ample click area—approximately 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding—maintaining touch‑friendly sizing while keeping the band visually light. The background of this band can be a subtle off‑white or very light tint to differentiate it from the plain white content sections without drawing strong attention away from the hero or audience cards. This navigation feels like the final organizing layer on the page, providing quick structured routes for more advanced users once they have scanned the main tiles and audience‑specific options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal navigation row at the bottom of the main content stack; remains in normal flow and does not become sticky or floating.

name: Programs & Classes
title: Programs & Classes
description: Search, filter, and browse all available programs and classes (such as swim lessons, fitness classes, and training certifications), then open a specific program’s detail page.

core_features: Load and display top-level program categories using getProgramCategories so users can switch between different types of programs (e.g., swim lessons, fitness, training).
Load available filter options for the selected category using getProgramFilterOptions and render age, skill, schedule, price, and feature filters.

Apply filters and sorting to search and list programs using searchPrograms, honoring URL parameters like categoryId, subcategory, ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolType, sortBy, page, and pageSize.

Provide category-specific helper workflows for lifeguard certifications using searchLifeguardCertificationPrograms (e.g., weekend-only certification with CPR/First Aid and a maximum price or end date).
Provide category-specific helper workflows for aqua fitness evening classes using searchAquaFitnessEveningClasses (e.g., evening classes under a selected maximum price per class).
Display program cards with key details from Program entities and support navigation to program_detail.html by passing the selected program’s ID.
user_workflows: Workflow: Open Programs & Classes from header with a preselected category: 1) User clicks a header navigation link (e.g., Programs, Swim Lessons for Kids, Fitness Classes, Training & Certifications). 2) Browser navigates to programs.html with URL parameters such as categoryId, and optionally subcategory, ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolType, sortBy, page, and pageSize. 3) On load, the page reads URL parameters and initializes the state controller (category, filters, sorting, pagination). 4) The Category Selector component calls getProgramCategories and selects the category matching categoryId if present; if missing, it uses a default category derived from the returned list and can update the URL accordingly. 5) The Filter Panel component calls getProgramFilterOptions with the active categoryId, builds filter controls, and pre-fills them from URL parameters where applicable. 6) The Results List component calls searchPrograms with the current URL parameters and status constrained to active programs, then displays the returned programs using Program Card components with pagination.

Workflow: Filter kids swim lessons by age, skill, and schedule: 1) User arrives with categoryId corresponding to kids swim lessons in the URL or selects that category from the Category Selector. 2) Filter Panel calls getProgramFilterOptions for the selected category and shows filters such as age ranges, skill levels, day-of-week, time-of-day, price ranges, and any relevant feature flags. 3) User adjusts age range (which maps to ageMin and ageMax URL parameters), skill level (skillLevel URL parameter), and optionally dayOfWeek and time-of-day (which map to startTimeFrom and startTimeTo). 4) On each filter change, the Filter Panel updates the relevant URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchPrograms call with updated parameters. 5) Results List displays only Programs that match the ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, and startTimeFrom/startTimeTo filters. 6) User clicks a Program Card to navigate to program_detail.html with programId set to the selected Program.id.

Workflow: Sort and paginate program search results: 1) After filters are applied and searchPrograms has returned programs, the Sort & Page Size Bar reads available sortOptions from getProgramFilterOptions and highlights the current sortBy value from the URL if present. 2) User selects a different sort option; the Sort & Page Size Bar updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchPrograms call. 3) Pagination Controls read totalCount, page, and pageSize from the searchPrograms response. 4) User clicks Next or a specific page number; Pagination Controls update the page URL parameter (and optionally pageSize when changed) and trigger searchPrograms again. 5) Results List re-renders with the programs for the selected page.

Workflow: Lifeguard certification weekend course discovery using specialized search: 1) User navigates to Programs & Classes with categoryId set to a training or certification category and optionally subcategory indicating lifeguard certifications and filters such as maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, or weekendsOnly in the URL. 2) The Lifeguard Quick Search component becomes visible when the active category and/or subcategory indicate lifeguard certification is relevant (based on data from getProgramCategories and the current URL state). 3) Within this component, the user can optionally set an end-by date (held in local component state but not required to be in URL) and confirm whether to require CPR/First Aid inclusion. 4) When the user activates the quick search action, the component calls searchLifeguardCertificationPrograms using maxEndDate from the component’s input (if provided), maxPrice from the current URL maxPrice parameter (if present), and requiresCprFirstAid derived from either the URL includesCprFirstAid filter or the component toggle. 5) The component displays the returned lifeguard Programs in a focused list (reusing Program Card components) that the user can browse alongside or instead of the main searchPrograms listing. 6) Clicking any Program Card from this list still navigates to program_detail.html with programId set to the selected program’s ID.

Workflow: Aqua fitness evening class selection using specialized search: 1) User navigates to Programs & Classes with a categoryId corresponding to fitness-related or aqua fitness programs, or selects such a category. 2) The Aqua Fitness Evening Quick Search component becomes visible when featureFlags from getProgramFilterOptions indicates aqua fitness options are relevant (for example via an aquaFitnessOnly flag) or when subcategory indicates aqua_fitness. 3) User optionally enters or selects a maximum price per class (either using a dedicated input in the component or mirroring the maxPrice URL parameter). 4) When the user activates the aqua fitness quick search, the component calls searchAquaFitnessEveningClasses with maxPricePerClass derived from the relevant input or the current maxPrice filter. 5) The component renders the returned Programs that are already constrained by the interface to evening times, showing them via Program Card components. 6) User selects a class by clicking a card and is navigated to program_detail.html with the appropriate programId.

Workflow: Deep linking and sharing filtered program searches: 1) User applies various filters (category, subcategory, age, skill, schedule, price, feature flags) and sorting, and navigates to a specific page of results. 2) Each interaction updates the URL parameters (categoryId, subcategory, ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolType, sortBy, page, pageSize) to match the current state. 3) User copies the browser URL and shares it. 4) Another user opens the shared URL; on load, the page reads the URL parameters, applies them to Category Selector, Filter Panel, Sort & Page Size Bar, and Pagination Controls, then calls searchPrograms with the same filters and sort to reproduce the same result set. 5) The second user can then adjust filters further or select any Program Card to go to the program detail page.
interactions: Selecting a category tab or dropdown option triggers: update of categoryId (and possibly subcategory) in the URL, resetting of page to 1, retrieval of new filter configuration via getProgramFilterOptions for the selected category, and a new searchPrograms call using the updated categoryId.

Changing any filter in the Filter Panel (such as age, skill level, day-of-week, time-of-day, price range, pool location, or feature flags) updates the corresponding URL parameters (ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, locationPoolType, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly), resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchPrograms call.

Selecting a time-of-day option updates startTimeFrom and startTimeTo in the URL based on the selected option’s startTimeFrom and startTimeTo returned by getProgramFilterOptions, then triggers searchPrograms.

Changing the sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms, which returns programs sorted according to the selected criteria; the Sort & Page Size Bar reflects the appliedSort value from the response.
Changing page size updates the pageSize URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms so that the new number of results per page is applied.
Clicking pagination controls (Next, Previous, specific page numbers) updates the page URL parameter and triggers searchPrograms while preserving all other filter parameters.
Clicking on any Program Card navigates the browser to program_detail.html with a programId URL parameter equal to the associated Program.id.

Activating the Lifeguard Quick Search action calls searchLifeguardCertificationPrograms with parameters derived from the component’s inputs and current URL filters; this updates a dedicated lifeguard result section without altering the main searchPrograms listing unless the implementation chooses to synchronize results.

Activating the Aqua Fitness Evening Quick Search action calls searchAquaFitnessEveningClasses with maxPricePerClass derived from user input or current filters; this updates a dedicated aqua fitness result section without necessarily changing the main searchPrograms results.

Reloading the page or navigating via a shared URL causes URL State Manager to reapply all URL parameters to the UI components and repeat the necessary calls to getProgramCategories, getProgramFilterOptions, and searchPrograms.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page treats URL parameters as the single source of truth for the main search state. On initial load, the URL State Manager parses parameters such as categoryId, subcategory, ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolType, sortBy, page, and pageSize. If categoryId is present, the Category Selector initializes its active category to that value; if not, it waits for getProgramCategories to return available categories and selects a default (for example, the first category returned) and may update categoryId in the URL. The Filter Panel uses getProgramFilterOptions(categoryId) to determine which filters are applicable for the active category and aligns the current control values with URL parameters where they exist. For instance, when ageMin and ageMax are present, the age filter control is set accordingly; when skillLevel is present, the corresponding option is selected; when dayOfWeek is present, the matching option is chosen; when startTimeFrom and startTimeTo are present, the time-of-day control either selects the time-of-day option whose range matches those values or reflects a custom range if no predefined option matches. The feature flags in getProgramFilterOptions (such as includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolTypeFilter, aquaFitnessOnly) determine which URL parameters are relevant for the current category; if the URL contains parameters that are not relevant (for example, includesCprFirstAid for a category that does not use that filter), the UI can either hide the corresponding controls while leaving the parameters intact or clear them from the URL when the user interacts with filters. Whenever the user changes category, filters, sortBy, or pageSize, the URL State Manager updates the corresponding parameters in the URL query string and resets page to 1 to avoid invalid pagination; pagination interactions only modify the page parameter. After each state change, a new searchPrograms call is executed with all applicable current parameters combined and an internal status filter restricted to active programs. The response’s totalCount, page, pageSize, and appliedSort are used to keep the pagination and sort UI synchronized with server-side behavior. Specialized components that call searchLifeguardCertificationPrograms or searchAquaFitnessEveningClasses may read relevant values from URL parameters (like maxPrice or includesCprFirstAid) but manage their additional inputs (such as maximum end date or maximum price per class) in local component state, not in the URL, unless explicitly designed otherwise. Regardless of whether the user arrives via navigation links or a manually constructed URL, the same parsing and synchronization flow ensures that the main listing and filters always reflect the current URL state.

components.functionality: Parses URL parameters on load, maintains in-memory representation of the search state (categoryId, subcategory, ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolType, sortBy, page, pageSize), updates the browser URL when state changes, and coordinates calls to getProgramCategories, getProgramFilterOptions, and searchPrograms.

Loads top-level program categories using getProgramCategories and lets the user choose the active category; updates categoryId (and optionally subcategory) in the URL and triggers reloading of filter options and search results.

Displays dynamic filters for the active category using configuration from getProgramFilterOptions (age ranges, skill levels, days of week, time-of-day ranges, price ranges, and feature flags). On user input, it maps selections to URL parameters such as ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, and locationPoolType, then triggers searchPrograms via the state controller.

Renders sort options based on sortOptions returned by getProgramFilterOptions for the current category and lets users change sortBy; also provides a control to adjust pageSize. Updates sortBy and pageSize URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms.

Calls searchPrograms with the current URL-derived parameters (including categoryId, subcategory, ageMin, ageMax, skillLevel, dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, maxPrice, includesCprFirstAid, weekendsOnly, locationPoolType, sortBy, page, pageSize, and status constrained to active) and renders the returned Program entities as Program Card components. Also exposes totalCount, page, and pageSize for use by pagination controls.

Represents a single Program in the results list or in any specialized quick-search result; shows key details derived from the Program entity (such as name, category, schedule, price, and relevant flags) and allows navigation to the detailed program page.

Displays page numbers and navigation controls based on totalCount, current page, and pageSize from the searchPrograms response; updates the page URL parameter when the user selects a new page and triggers searchPrograms via the state controller.

When the current category and/or subcategory indicate lifeguard certifications, provides a focused control area for quickly finding suitable weekend certification programs. Reads current filters like maxPrice and includesCprFirstAid from the URL if present and allows the user to set or adjust inputs such as a maximum end date or CPR/First Aid requirement. Calls searchLifeguardCertificationPrograms with maxEndDate derived from its inputs, maxPrice from the URL or input, and requiresCprFirstAid derived from URL or component state, then displays the returned Programs using Program Card components.

When the filter configuration and/or subcategory indicate that aqua fitness is relevant, enables a specialized search for evening aqua fitness classes. Uses a dedicated control for maximum price per class or reuses maxPrice from the URL, calls searchAquaFitnessEveningClasses with maxPricePerClass, and renders the returned Programs in a compact list of Program Card components.

Listens to the searchPrograms response; when totalCount is zero, displays a functional message indicating that no programs match the current filters and may suggest clearing or adjusting filters. It can trigger events to reset certain URL parameters (such as removing narrow filters) via the URL State Manager.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom band structure as the mockup. Within the site shell (utility bar + header/nav handled elsewhere), this page begins with a full‑width hero‑like band dedicated to discovery: a page title (implicit), category-selector tabs, and two featured quick-search panels arranged horizontally. Below this discovery band sits the main search/browse area, split into a left filter sidebar and a right content column containing sort controls, the program results list (or empty state), and pagination. All components align to a centered max‑width container mapped to a 12‑column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom band structure as the mockup. Within the site shell (utility bar + header/nav handled elsewhere), this page begins with a full‑width hero‑like band dedicated to discovery: a page title (implicit), category-selector tabs, and two featured quick-search panels arranged horizontally. Below this discovery band sits the main search/browse area, split into a left filter sidebar and a right content column containing sort controls, the program results list (or empty state), and pagination. All components align to a centered max‑width container mapped to a 12‑column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above-the-fold, roughly the top 40–50% of the main content area is given to the discovery band (category tabs plus two colorful quick-search panels that behave visually like hero cards). The remaining space in the viewport is occupied by the filter/results layout, where the filter-panel consumes about 1/4 of the content width (≈3 of 12 columns) and the results column consumes the remaining 3/4 (≈9 of 12 columns). Vertically, generous 32–48px spacing separates the hero band from the filters/results section and between the results list and pagination, matching the mockup’s airy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium. The hero/discovery band is visually energetic with colorful quick-search panels but limited in number to avoid clutter. Category tabs and sort controls are spaced with comfortable padding and whitespace. The filter panel groups controls with clear gaps and headings. The results list uses a moderate density: cards with enough padding, distinct separation, and readable line-height, possibly in a 2-column grid to keep each card spacious. Empty-state messaging uses large, relaxed typography and extra whitespace, echoing the intro text section from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the page title area (handled in template) and then moves immediately to the category-selector tabs running horizontally near the top center. From there, attention drops slightly into the colorful quick-search hero panels, scanning left-to-right across them. The eye then flows down into the main content split: first noticing the filter panel to the left, then sweeping over to the sort-and-page-size bar above the results, and then down through the program cards in a vertical scroll. Pagination at the bottom centers the end of the main flow. The URL state behavior is invisible, while the quick-search panels and filters encourage users to jump or refine their journey without breaking the vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description: Within the site’s bright, family-friendly shell, the Programs & Classes page opens into a centered max-width content area. Just below the global header, a discovery band stretches full-width with a white background: the category tabs sit horizontally near the top, followed by two vibrant quick-search panels laid out side by side like hero cards, adding color and energy without overwhelming the space. A comfortable vertical gap separates this band from the main search results area below. The page then resolves into a classic two-column layout: a left filter sidebar occupying about one quarter of the width and a right results column occupying the rest. At the top of the right column, a slim sort-and-page-size bar spans the width, with the program results cards flowing beneath in a clean, grid-like list. Each card is well-padded, poster-like but orderly, echoing the hero’s promotional vibe. When no results exist, a large, centered empty-state message occupies this right column, replacing the list. Pagination sits at the bottom of the results area, centered and well-separated, marking the end of the main vertical flow. The whole layout feels airy, colorful at key focal points (quick-search panels and cards), and easy to scan from top-left to bottom-right in a smooth, linear scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and lives conceptually across the entire page. It governs the URL query parameters in sync with the visible filter-panel, category-selector, sort-and-page-size-bar, and pagination-controls. Spatially, you can think of it as an underlying layer attached to the main content container, spanning 100% of its width and height but not consuming any visual space or altering the layout. It does not change the flow or spacing of other components; instead, it responds to interactions in the visible components and updates the browser URL/state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered controller; behaves like a page-level service attached to the main content area without any visible footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-selector appears at the top of the page’s main content area, forming the upper edge of the discovery band. It is horizontally centered within the max-width container and spans roughly 80–90% of the content width, visually reading as a single horizontal strip of tabs. Vertically, it sits about 24–32px below the bottom of the global header and about 16–24px above the quick-search panels. Each tab behaves like a wide, pill- or chip-style button, with about 16px vertical and 24px horizontal padding to feel tap-friendly and generous. The tab row aligns with the 12-column grid, with the full row occupying about 10–12 columns depending on content; tabs may wrap onto a second line on smaller widths but remain tightly grouped at the center.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the top of the page’s main content; scrolls away with the page (not sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-panel is positioned below the discovery band, aligned to the left of the main content container. It occupies roughly 25–30% of the content width, equivalent to about 3–4 of 12 grid columns on desktop. Its top edge is aligned horizontally with the top of the sort-and-page-size-bar on the right, with a vertical gap of about 32–40px separating it from the quick-search panels above. Inside, the filter-panel is structured as stacked filter groups with clear headings and 16–24px vertical spacing between groups, maintaining low-to-medium density. The panel’s height grows with the amount of filter options and may extend beyond the viewport. On larger screens it can be slightly taller than the initial viewport, encouraging scrolling, while the right-hand results area scrolls alongside. The left edge of the panel aligns with the main container’s left margin, and its right edge forms a clear column boundary with the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: For desktop, the panel can be sticky within the viewport: its top edge pins just below any global sticky header, so when users scroll, the filters remain visible until the bottom of the panel reaches the end of the results section. On mobile or narrow layouts, it would typically collapse into a slide-over or accordion, but in the primary desktop layout it is a fixed-width sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-page-size-bar sits at the top of the right-hand content column, horizontally aligned with the upper edge of the filter-panel. It spans the full width of the results area (about 70–75% of the overall content width, or roughly 8–9 of 12 columns) and sits approximately 32–40px below the bottom of the quick-search panels. The bar itself is shallow, about 48–64px tall, with comfortable internal padding (12–16px) on all sides. Sort controls are left-aligned within this bar, while page-size controls are right-aligned, creating a balanced, utility-like header for the results list below. There is a subtle divider or bottom border separating this bar from the program-results-list, reinforcing its role as a control strip rather than content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; it scrolls off the top of the page as users move down through the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program-results-list occupies the full width of the right-hand column directly beneath the sort-and-page-size-bar, with a vertical gap of about 16–24px between them. It fills approximately 70–75% of the content width (aligned to about 8–9 columns on the 12-column grid) and runs from near the top of the viewport downwards, extending as results require. Programs are presented in a structured, card-based layout: on wider screens, cards are arranged in a 2-column grid with equal-width columns and 24–32px of horizontal gap between them; on narrower screens, the list collapses to a single column for readability. Vertically, cards are separated by about 24px, ensuring each card feels distinct yet part of a cohesive list. The list scrolls naturally, forming the main vertical content stream beneath the hero/discovery area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content area within the page; its height is driven by the number of program-card instances. When there are no results, the empty-state-message occupies this same space instead of the cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each program-card is a rectangular tile within the program-results-list grid. On desktop, each card spans roughly half of the results column width (about 4 of 12 total grid columns if the right column is conceptualized as 8 columns), creating two equal columns. The card has generous internal padding (16–24px on all sides) and a white or very light background with a soft shadow or subtle border to echo the poster-like promotional style. The card’s content is vertically stacked: a small banner area or thumbnail image near the top (spanning the full card width), program title and key details (dates, level, age group) in the upper-middle, and primary actions (e.g., ‘View Details’ or ‘Register’) anchored at the bottom, aligned to the right or centered. Vertical spacing between text elements is around 8–12px, keeping a medium density that remains scannable. When multiple cards are shown, their heights may vary slightly based on content, but they align at the top within their row, ensuring a tidy grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block elements within the list; they reflow responsively from two columns to one column on narrower widths, maintaining their internal padding and hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit directly below the program-results-list, within the same right-hand column. There is a generous vertical gap (32–40px) between the last row of program-cards and the pagination bar, giving a clear sense of conclusion to each page of results. The pagination row spans the full width of the results column (about 70–75% of the page content width) but the controls themselves are center-aligned horizontally, with page numbers and next/previous buttons laid out in a compact horizontal line. Padding around each page button is about 8–12px vertically and 12–16px horizontally, making them comfortably clickable while maintaining a tidy, unobtrusive look. A bit of extra bottom margin (24–32px) below the pagination keeps it from feeling cramped at the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the results column; it scrolls into view after the user reaches the end of the current page of program-cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lifeguard-quick-search panel is one of the two hero-like quick-search cards in the discovery band. It is positioned in the left half of that band, directly under the category-selector and aligned to the grid so that it occupies about 3 of 12 columns but is visually treated as one of two equal-width panels spanning roughly 45–50% of the max-width container each. Its top edge sits around 16–24px below the category-selector. The panel is visually prominent and colorful, likely using a bright accent color and aquatic imagery, with generous internal padding (24–32px). Content within the panel is stacked: a bold title (e.g., “Become a Lifeguard”), a brief descriptive line, and a compact search or key filters module (dropdowns, text input, and a CTA button) grouped in the lower half. This creates a self-contained, poster-like module that draws the eye on the left side of the hero band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the discovery band; it scrolls away once the user moves down into the filter/results area. It does not stick or float, but its high color contrast makes it a key focal element while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The aqua-fitness-evening-quick-search panel mirrors the lifeguard quick-search on the right side of the discovery band, creating a balanced pair of hero-style cards. It occupies the right half of the band, aligned with the grid to span a similar width (about 3 of 12 columns conceptually, forming two equal panels). Its top alignment and vertical size match the lifeguard panel, so their top and bottom edges form a clean horizontal line. It uses its own distinct but complementary color palette and imagery (e.g., evening pool scene, fitness silhouettes) to differentiate it while fitting the bright, family-friendly theme. Inside, the layout is similar: bold title, short explanatory text, and a focused search/filter control for evening aqua fitness classes, with the primary action button visually anchored near the lower-right of the panel. Horizontal spacing between the two quick-search panels is around 24–32px, forming a cohesive hero strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the discovery band; like its counterpart, it scrolls away naturally as users move down the page and has no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message appears in place of the program-results-list when no classes match the current filters or category. It occupies the same spatial footprint as the results list within the right-hand column: spanning the full width of the results area (about 70–75% of the content width) and sitting directly beneath the sort-and-page-size-bar with 24–32px of vertical gap. The message is centered both horizontally and, as much as content height allows, vertically within the visible portion of this area. It uses larger, welcoming typography for the headline (similar in size to the intro text from the mockup), with a secondary line of explanatory text beneath and possibly a prominent CTA (like “Clear Filters” or “Browse All Programs”) centered below that. Generous whitespace surrounds the copy, with at least 32–48px of padding above and below, reinforcing a calm, informative feel instead of clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional, replacing the program-results-list area. It scrolls normally within the page; if filters or category selection change to produce results, it disappears and the results list takes its place.

name: Program Detail
title: Program Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected aquatic program and supports either direct registration or adding the program to the cart, depending on configuration.

core_features: Load and display complete details for a specific program using getProgramDetail, including schedule, pricing, age/skill level, location, and included features.
Show program-specific policies and indicate whether a waiver is required, using data from getProgramDetail.
Provide a direct registration form for programs where registrationAllowed is true, using registerForProgram to submit enrollment.
Display a post-registration summary using getProgramRegistrationSummary, either immediately after registration or when revisiting with a registrationId URL parameter.
Provide an add-to-cart action for programs where canAddToCart is true, using addProgramToCart to add the program to the cart.
Offer navigation actions to return to the program list, view the cart after adding an item, or view My Schedule after successful registration.
user_workflows: View program details: 1) User navigates to program_detail.html with a programId URL parameter. 2) Page calls getProgramDetail with that programId. 3) Page displays program name, description, schedule (dates, times, meeting_days, start_date, end_date), age/skill information, locationDescription, and any special flags such as includes_cpr_first_aid. 4) Page shows pricing information (including resident/non-resident options if resident_pricing_available is true) and policies text. 5) Page conditionally displays the registration form and/or add-to-cart controls based on registrationAllowed and canAddToCart.

Direct registration workflow: 1) On a program where registrationAllowed is true, the user fills in participantName, participantDateOfBirth (if applicable), chooses a pricingOption (if resident_pricing_available is true), and enters contactPhone and contactEmail. 2) If waiverRequired from getProgramDetail is true, user must check a waiver acceptance control (acceptWaiver). 3) User submits the registration form, triggering registerForProgram with programId from URL and the entered form fields. 4) On success, the page receives a registration object and either: (a) immediately calls getProgramRegistrationSummary with registration.id to show a confirmation panel, and/or (b) updates the URL to include registrationId and uses that to show the summary state. 5) The user can then choose to navigate to my_schedule.html (e.g., via a 'View in My Schedule' action if the broader system adds registrations to My Schedule) or stay on the page.

View registration summary via URL workflow: 1) User arrives at program_detail.html with programId and registrationId URL parameters (for example from a confirmation email or My Schedule link). 2) Page first calls getProgramDetail with programId to show program info. 3) Page also calls getProgramRegistrationSummary with registrationId to retrieve the registration and associated program. 4) Page displays a registration confirmation section (e.g., showing participant_name, status, and price_paid if available), while still showing the standard program details.

Add program to cart workflow: 1) On a program where canAddToCart from getProgramDetail is true, the user optionally sets a quantity (if allowed by business rules) and clicks an 'Add to Cart' action. 2) Page calls addProgramToCart with programId from URL and the chosen quantity. 3) On success, the page receives updated cart and cartItems and displays an inline confirmation (e.g., a toast or confirmation panel). 4) User can click a 'View Cart' navigation action that routes to cart.html (without needing additional URL parameters, or with cart-identifying parameters if the broader system requires them).

Return to program list workflow: 1) From program_detail.html, user clicks a 'Back to Program List' navigation control. 2) Browser navigates to programs.html, relying on browser history or any preserved URL filters from earlier navigation (this page does not alter those filters).
interactions: On page load, read the programId (and optional registrationId) from the URL query string. If programId is missing, show an error state or redirect logic (implementation-specific) without making API calls.

Call getProgramDetail whenever programId changes in the URL. Bind the returned program, categoryName, subcategoryName, skillLevelLabel, ageRangeLabel, locationDescription, policies, registrationAllowed, canAddToCart, and waiverRequired to the corresponding components.

If a registrationId is present in the URL, call getProgramRegistrationSummary with that registrationId after getProgramDetail returns, and bind its registration and program to a registration summary component.

In the registration form, validate required fields (e.g., participantName, contactEmail). If waiverRequired is true, enforce that the waiver checkbox is selected before allowing submission. On submission, call registerForProgram with programId from URL and the form values, then handle success or error by updating the page state.

After a successful registerForProgram response, display success feedback and the registration summary (using either the registration object directly or via getProgramRegistrationSummary). Provide actions to navigate to my_schedule.html or to stay on the page.

In the add-to-cart section, allow the user to adjust quantity according to business rules. On 'Add to Cart' click, call addProgramToCart with programId and quantity, and on success show a confirmation message with an option to navigate to cart.html.

Provide navigation controls: 'Back to Program List' (to programs.html), 'View Cart' (to cart.html after a successful addProgramToCart), and 'View in My Schedule' (to my_schedule.html after successful registration).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL parameter is programId, required for all core functionality. On initial load, the page reads programId from the query string (e.g., program_detail.html?programId={Program.id}). If programId is present, the page calls getProgramDetail(programId) and uses the response to populate all program-related components. The page may also accept an optional registrationId parameter (e.g., program_detail.html?programId={Program.id}&registrationId={ProgramRegistration.id}). When registrationId is present, after getProgramDetail resolves, the page calls getProgramRegistrationSummary(registrationId) and displays the registration summary alongside the program details. Submission of the registration form uses the current programId from the URL. On successful registration, the page can update the URL to include the new registrationId (e.g., via history replacement or push), then use that registrationId for summary retrieval with getProgramRegistrationSummary. For cart operations, the page does not manage cart identifiers directly; it only passes programId and quantity to addProgramToCart, and relies on that interface to manage the underlying Cart and CartItem records. No localStorage is used for state; the URL query parameters (programId, optionally registrationId) and in-memory component state derived from the API responses control what is displayed.

components.functionality: On page initialization or whenever the programId URL parameter changes, this component reads programId (and optional registrationId) from the query string, calls getProgramDetail with programId, and exposes the returned program detail data to child components. If registrationId is present, it also triggers a call to getProgramRegistrationSummary after getProgramDetail completes.

Displays high-level program information such as name, categoryName, subcategoryName, skillLevelLabel, ageRangeLabel, description, locationDescription, schedule (start_date, end_date, meeting_days, is_weekends_only, start_time, end_time), and any indicators like includes_cpr_first_aid. Also shows status-specific messaging when program.status is not active (e.g., cancelled or inactive, using only system state).

Focuses on rendering the schedule and timing information for the program, including start_date, end_date, meeting_days, is_weekends_only, start_time, and end_time. Communicates any derived schedule notes (e.g., if meetings occur only on weekends using is_weekends_only) based solely on the Program fields.

Displays pricing information for the program, including default price and any resident/non-resident variations if resident_pricing_available is true. This section does not calculate or store prices itself; it simply surfaces Program.price, Program.price_resident, Program.price_non_resident, and resident_pricing_available for display and for use by the registration form and add-to-cart controls.

Displays program-specific policies and cancellation rules from getProgramDetail.policies. If waiverRequired is true, this component also displays the waiver requirement note, and exposes a flag indicating that the registration form must enforce waiver acceptance via acceptWaiver.

Provides direct enrollment for programs where registrationAllowed from getProgramDetail is true. Collects participantName, participantDateOfBirth (when applicable), pricingOption (if resident_pricing_available is true, mapping to the ProgramRegistration.pricing_option enum), contactPhone, contactEmail, and an acceptWaiver checkbox if waiverRequired is true. On submit, validates required fields and calls registerForProgram with programId from the URL and the entered values. On success, it passes the returned registration to the registration-confirmation component and may update the URL to include registration.id as registrationId.

Shows a confirmation summary for a successful registration. It can operate in two modes: (1) immediate mode, where it uses the ProgramRegistration object returned from registerForProgram; and (2) URL-driven mode, where it uses getProgramRegistrationSummary.registration and getProgramRegistrationSummary.program when a registrationId parameter is present. Displays key fields such as registration.status, participant_name, participant_date_of_birth, pricing_option, price_paid, and any relevant program details like name, start_date, and start_time. Provides actions to navigate to my_schedule.html or stay on this page.

Enables adding the current program to the shopping cart when canAddToCart from getProgramDetail is true. Allows the user to adjust a quantity field (if business rules allow multiple quantities) and, on submit, calls addProgramToCart with programId from the URL and the selected quantity. On success, it passes the updated cart and cartItems to a feedback component and exposes a 'View Cart' action that navigates to cart.html.

Centralizes contextual actions for the program detail page. Conditionally shows buttons to: start registration (scroll to or focus the registration-form when registrationAllowed is true), add to cart (submits add-program-to-cart-form when canAddToCart is true), navigate back to programs.html, navigate to cart.html after an add-to-cart success, and navigate to my_schedule.html after registration success. It does not perform API calls itself but orchestrates interactions between other components and navigation.

Displays non-visual status messages related to data loading and operations on this page. Examples include errors from getProgramDetail (e.g., invalid or missing programId), registerForProgram failures (e.g., validation or capacity issues signaled via the message field), and addProgramToCart failures. It reads the success and message properties from API responses and presents them to the user without hardcoding any domain data.

Provides direct navigation links from the program detail page to other pages. Includes a 'Back to Program List' link that routes to programs.html (relying on browser history or preserved URL filters), a 'View Cart' link to cart.html (primarily used after a successful addProgramToCart), and a 'View in My Schedule' link to my_schedule.html (primarily used after a successful registerForProgram).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom structure consistent with the mockup: global utility/header bands at the very top (outside this component list), followed immediately by a narrow status-and-errors banner, then a hero-like program-summary-panel acting as the primary entry point for this specific program. Just below, a horizontal navigation-links-bar provides in-page anchors to key sections (Schedule, Pricing, Policies, Registration). The main detail sections (program-schedule-section, program-pricing-section, policies-and-waiver-panel) are stacked vertically in a single centered content column. Near the lower third of the page, the registration-form or add-program-to-cart-form occupies a prominent card-like section, with the registration-confirmation replacing this card after successful completion. A program-actions-bar sits as a fixed/sticky strip at the bottom of the viewport. The program-detail-loader overlays the entire content area when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom structure consistent with the mockup: global utility/header bands at the very top (outside this component list), followed immediately by a narrow status-and-errors banner, then a hero-like program-summary-panel acting as the primary entry point for this specific program. Just below, a horizontal navigation-links-bar provides in-page anchors to key sections (Schedule, Pricing, Policies, Registration). The main detail sections (program-schedule-section, program-pricing-section, policies-and-waiver-panel) are stacked vertically in a single centered content column. Near the lower third of the page, the registration-form or add-program-to-cart-form occupies a prominent card-like section, with the registration-confirmation replacing this card after successful completion. A program-actions-bar sits as a fixed/sticky strip at the bottom of the viewport. The program-detail-loader overlays the entire content area when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top header (provided by the global layout) remains shallow, with the status-and-errors-banner sitting immediately beneath it as a slim but full-width band. The hero-like program-summary-panel claims roughly the upper 35–45% of the initial viewport height, visually echoing the mockup’s hero band while staying center-constrained within a max-width container. The navigation-links-bar is compact and sits just under the summary with about 24–32px vertical breathing room. The stacked sections (schedule, pricing, policies) share the central portion of the scrollable page, each occupying 60–80% of the viewport height depending on content. The registration-form/add-program-to-cart-form section is given generous vertical padding and height comparable to a major section, so it feels like a primary decision area. The program-actions-bar is a short (about 64–80px tall) fixed band along the bottom edge of the viewport, spanning full width but visually constrained to the same max-width as the main content. The loader, when visible, covers the full content area below the global header.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall content density is low-to-medium, mirroring the mockup’s airy structure. The program-summary-panel is visually rich but not crowded, with a two-column layout, large title text, and ample whitespace around metadata. The navigation-links-bar uses spacious, pill-like links with 8–16px internal padding and 24px spacing between items. The schedule and pricing sections use table or list content that is denser but nested inside cards with clear headings and generous line height, so they feel organized rather than cramped. The policies-and-waiver-panel reads like editorial content with wide margins, increased line height, and a narrower text measure (roughly 60–70 characters per line). The forms are denser than narrative sections but divided into clear groups/rows with 16–24px vertical spacing between fields. The fixed program-actions-bar is concise, holding only essential actions and a key summary (price, selected session) with plenty of negative space to keep it from feeling heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows the mockup’s F-pattern and vertical scan. Users first see the global logo and nav at the very top, then immediately their attention is drawn to the status-and-errors-banner if present. From there, the eye naturally drops into the colorful, hero-like program-summary-panel where the program name and key highlights are prominent on the left. The navigation-links-bar just below subtly invites horizontal scanning across section anchors. As users scroll, their gaze moves down through the schedule section, then pricing, then policies, in a clean, linear path. The registration-form/add-to-cart-form is positioned where a user would reach it after understanding the program, reinforcing a narrative of learn → confirm → act. Throughout the experience, the program-actions-bar stays fixed at the bottom edge of the viewport, sitting in peripheral vision but always available as the main action trigger. The program-detail-loader, when active, dominates the visual field and temporarily interrupts this flow, clearly signaling a loading state.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global utility bar and main header, a slim, full-width status-and-errors-banner sits immediately under the navigation, acting as a colored alert strip that slightly pushes the page content down when visible. Beneath it, the page opens into a spacious, hero-like program-summary-panel centered within a max-width container; this panel stretches nearly the full width of the screen on desktop while respecting generous side margins, with a two-column layout: a larger left area carrying the program title, age range, and badges, and a narrower right column summarizing key facts in a compact card. Under this hero block, a horizontal navigation-links-bar spans the content width with pill-shaped section links floated in the center, echoing the mockup’s horizontal nav. As users scroll down, they pass through a vertical stack of clearly separated sections—program schedule, pricing, and policies—each presented as a card or band with clear headings, generous vertical spacing, and comfortable line height over a white or very light background. Further down, a prominent, card-like registration area appears: either a registration form or an add-to-cart form filling roughly 70–80% of the content width and centered, feeling like the final decision moment in the journey. After submission, a registration-confirmation view replaces that card, offering a celebratory, readable recap. Throughout the page, a program-actions-bar remains fixed along the bottom edge of the viewport, aligned with the central content column, containing the primary CTA (Register/Add to Cart) and key summary details, much like a persistent, friendly booking bar. When data is loading or actions are processing, the program-detail-loader overlays the main content area with a semi-opaque veil and a centered spinner, dimming the page while preserving the bright, family-friendly aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the program-detail-loader appears as a full-coverage overlay on top of the entire program detail content area, starting just below the global header and spanning edge-to-edge horizontally. It extends from the top of the program-specific content (just above the status-and-errors-banner) down to the bottom of the viewport, and continues to cover the scrollable area if the user has scrolled. The overlay uses a semi-transparent white or light aqua tint so underlying content is slightly visible but clearly disabled. At the center of the viewport (both horizontally centered and vertically centered within the visible window), a spinner or progress indicator is placed inside a small card with 16–24px internal padding and subtle elevation. This central loader card occupies roughly 20–25% of the viewport width on desktop, ensuring it feels noticeable but not overwhelming. All other interactive components beneath are visually subdued and non-interactive while the loader is displayed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the scrollable content region beneath the global header; remains centered in the viewport while active, regardless of scroll.
