component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program-summary-panel is the first major content block of the page, positioned directly below the status-and-errors-banner with approximately 32px of vertical separation when both are visible. It is centered within a max-width container (e.g., 1200–1280px) and spans nearly the full width of this container, leaving 32–48px gutters on either side on large screens. Vertically, it occupies about 35–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and a soft, vibrant background (e.g., light aqua or blue gradient) that visually echoes the hero band from the mockup. Inside, the layout is a two-column grid: on desktop, the left column spans roughly 7 of 12 grid columns (about 60% width), hosting the program title in large, bold type at the top, followed by subheading text and key tags/badges spaced with 8–16px gaps. The right column spans about 5 of 12 columns (40% width) and contains a compact info card with details such as dates, location, and difficulty level; this card has its own white background, rounded corners, 16–24px internal padding, and subtle shadow, creating a poster-like but controlled visual. The panel’s bottom edge leaves about 32px of clear space before the navigation-links-bar, maintaining the airy, promotional feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the normal document flow, acting as the hero-like focal point for this page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program-schedule-section sits directly beneath the navigation-links-bar, with about 32–40px of vertical spacing separating the two. It is aligned to the same central max-width container as the summary panel and spans roughly 80–90% of that width, leaving balanced side margins. At the top of the section, a left-aligned section header (e.g., “Schedule”) spans the full content width with 16px of space below it. Below the header, the content is arranged in a mild two-column feel: on desktop, a main schedule table or list occupies approximately 8 of 12 grid columns (about two-thirds of the container) on the left, while the right third can be used for a compact callout card (e.g., next available session, waitlist status) or remain simply as breathing room if not needed. Rows within the schedule table follow a comfortable rhythm with 8–16px vertical padding per row, and the entire schedule area is wrapped in a light card surface with 16–24px padding and subtle border or shadow. Vertical spacing of about 32px separates this section from the program-pricing-section that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the vertical content flow, expanding vertically based on the number of scheduled items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program-pricing-section is positioned directly below the schedule section, with approximately 32–40px of vertical space between them to clearly separate the topics. It shares the same central max-width and horizontal alignment, occupying around 80–90% of the container width. A left-aligned section title (“Pricing & Fees”) anchors the top of the section, followed by 16–24px of whitespace. The main pricing content—such as base fee, multi-child discounts, and membership rates—is presented in a visually organized card that spans roughly 7–8 columns (about two-thirds width) and sits left-aligned within the section. To the right, a narrower column (about 4–5 columns) may hold a highlighted price summary or a promotional note, presented as a stacked card with a slightly brighter accent color to tie into the energetic theme. Internal padding of 16–24px and 16px vertical spacing between pricing lines keep the density comfortable. The bottom of this section leaves about 32–48px before the policies-and-waiver-panel, maintaining a clear visual break.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the vertical content flow, sized by the amount of pricing detail but visually grouped as a single band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-and-waiver-panel appears below the pricing section, separated by roughly 32–48px of vertical space. It adopts a more editorial, text-focused style in line with the mockup’s large intro text: the main text block is narrower than other sections, centered within the container and occupying about 60–70% of the total width to keep line lengths around 60–70 characters. The background remains white or very light, with minimal borders to reduce visual noise. At the top, a clear heading (“Policies & Waivers”) is centered or slightly left-of-center, with 16–24px of space before the body text starts. The body content is broken into paragraphs or collapsible accordions with 16–24px spacing between items and increased line height (around 1.5–1.6). Key clauses or important warnings can be highlighted in subtle callout rows spanning the full width of the text column. Vertical padding of 24–32px inside the panel and 32px of space to the next section (registration) reinforce a calm, readable feel despite the potential text length.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable text section that may extend vertically more than others but remains constrained to a narrower text column for readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the program supports direct registration, the registration-form appears as a major section below the policies-and-waiver-panel, typically with 40–56px of vertical space separating it from the previous section to signal a transition into the action phase. It is centered within the page and occupies about 70–80% of the max-width container, with a card-like appearance: white background, rounded corners, a subtle shadow, and generous internal padding (24–32px) to match the elevated importance. A clear, left-aligned heading (“Register for this Program”) sits at the top of the card, followed by a short introductory sentence with about 16px spacing before the first field group. Fields are organized in a loose 12-column grid; on desktop, simple fields can sit two per row (each occupying about 6 columns), while more complex fields or date pickers take the full width. Vertical spacing between field groups is 16–24px, and between logical sections (e.g., participant info vs. guardian info vs. payment notes) is 24–32px. The primary submit button is anchored near the bottom-right of the card, with a secondary link-style action (e.g., cancel) aligned to the left or directly beneath. The section ends with 48px of whitespace before the page footer or subsequent content. This placement visually signals that the form is the key next step after understanding details and policies. When this form is visible, the add-program-to-cart-form is hidden and the same spatial region is reserved for this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored in the lower third of the page’s content flow; occupies a consistent region that is shared conditionally with the add-program-to-cart-form and registration-confirmation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful registration, the registration-confirmation component occupies the exact same spatial footprint as the registration-form, effectively replacing the form card in-place. It remains centered and spans about 70–80% of the content width, maintaining continuity with the grid. The card’s height initially is similar to the form’s but can shrink or grow based on content; internal padding of 24–32px is preserved. At the top, a friendly, bold confirmation heading (e.g., “You’re Registered!”) is accompanied by an icon or small celebratory graphic aligned to the left, using vibrant accent colors consistent with the site’s family-friendly palette. Below, transaction and session details are presented in a clean, two-column summary (labels on the left, values on the right) with 8–16px spacing between rows and 24px between logical groups (participant info, schedule, payment summary). A prominent button for “View My Registrations” or “Print Confirmation” appears near the bottom right, while a tertiary text link might guide the user back to browsing other programs. Vertical spacing above and below the card remains consistent with the previous form section to keep the page rhythm intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline replacement for the registration-form/add-program-to-cart-form area; appears in the same location, pushing other content neither up nor down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the site is configured to use a cart-based flow instead of direct registration, the add-program-to-cart-form occupies the same location as the registration-form: a prominent, centered card-like section in the lower third of the page, appearing directly below the policies-and-waiver-panel with about 40–56px vertical separation. The card shares the same width (about 70–80% of the container), background, padding, and overall styling as the registration form to maintain consistency. Inside, the layout is more compact: the top of the card holds a heading (e.g., “Add to Cart”) and a brief summary line referencing the selected program instance. Below, key-select controls (such as date/session selector, quantity, or participant count) are arranged in 1–2 columns depending on screen width, each with 8–16px padding vertically. At the bottom, a large primary button labeled “Add to Cart” is aligned to the right, with price information either integrated into the button or displayed just to its left, mirroring the behavior of the bottom program-actions-bar. The card leaves a comfortable 48px gap before the footer or subsequent content, and when this form is shown, the registration-form is not rendered in this region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section occupying the same conditional layout slot as the registration-form and registration-confirmation; appears only for cart-driven flows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program-actions-bar is a persistent, horizontally oriented band fixed to the bottom edge of the viewport, always visible when the page content is loaded regardless of scroll position. It spans the full browser width from left to right, but its inner content is constrained to the same max-width container as the main page, centered with 24–32px side padding. The bar’s height is approximately 64–80px, and it uses a subtly elevated surface (slight shadow and/or contrasting background color) to distinguish it from the main page content while still feeling integrated. Inside, content is arranged in a three-part horizontal layout: on the left (about 4 of 12 columns) a concise text summary (e.g., selected session date/time, age group) is displayed; in the center (about 4 columns) the primary pricing summary (e.g., total price) is shown with slightly larger type; on the right (about 4 columns) the primary CTA button (e.g., “Register Now” or “Add to Cart”) is placed, sized generously with 16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. On narrower screens, these elements stack or realign but always stay inside the bar. Enough vertical padding is preserved above the bottom of the viewport so the bar doesn’t feel cramped. The presence of this bar is coordinated with the form sections: it provides a quick action path while the more detailed forms sit further down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the bottom of the viewport with slight elevation; remains visible as the user scrolls, aligned to the main content’s max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-and-errors-banner sits at the top of the program detail content stack, immediately below the global site header and utility bar. It spans the full width of the page, edge-to-edge, but its text content is constrained to the same max-width container used elsewhere, centered within the band. Vertically, it is relatively shallow—about 48–64px tall—behaving like a narrow alert strip with 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding around the message. When active (e.g., error or success), the banner uses a strong but not harsh background color (such as a warm red or bright green) with contrasting text to ensure visibility, echoing the colorful yet friendly aesthetic of the mockup. The message text is left-aligned within the container, with an optional dismiss icon aligned to the far right. When present, this banner pushes the program-summary-panel down slightly, preserving a 24–32px gap below it; when absent, the summary panel moves up to sit directly under the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band anchored directly beneath the global header; occupies space in the layout when visible and collapses when not.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links-bar is positioned directly below the program-summary-panel, with approximately 24–32px of space between their edges. It spans the full width of the page, but its primary interactive content—a row of in-page navigation links—is constrained and centered within the same max-width container. The bar itself is relatively shallow (about 56–64px tall), with a light background and perhaps a subtle bottom border to distinguish it from the content sections. Inside, the links (e.g., Overview, Schedule, Pricing, Policies, Registration) are arranged horizontally across the center, each rendered as a pill or tab with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and spaced about 16–24px apart. The active section link is highlighted with a stronger accent color and possibly an underline or bottom border indicator that aligns with the F-pattern scanning behavior from the mockup. On scroll, once the user has moved past the top of this bar, it becomes sticky to the top of the viewport (right below the global header/status banner), ensuring these anchors remain readily accessible without consuming excessive vertical space. A small amount of vertical whitespace (16–24px) separates the bottom of this bar from the top of the program-schedule-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky band that transitions from static in the normal flow to fixed at the top of the viewport once scrolled past, maintaining alignment with the main content container.

name: Lap Lane Reservations
title: Lap Lane Reservations
description: Page to search available lap swimming lanes by date and filters, view lane slot details, and complete a lane reservation with confirmation.

core_features: Initialize lap lane search filters (date, time window, pool type, maximum price, sort order) using incoming URL parameters and getLapLaneFilterOptions.
Search available lap lane slots using searchLapLaneSlots based on the current filters and sort order.
Display search results as a list of lane slot cards bound to LapLaneSlot data, with indicators for availability and key details.
Open a slot detail panel for a selected lane by calling getLapLaneSlotDetail to show pool information and complete slot details.
Provide a reservation form for the selected lane slot, allowing users to enter swimmer name, number of swimmers, and contact phone.
Submit the reservation using reserveLapLaneSlot and handle success and error outcomes (including when a slot becomes unavailable).
Fetch and display a reservation confirmation summary via getLapLaneReservationSummary after a successful reservation.
Allow navigation to the user’s schedule (my_schedule.html) via a "View My Schedule" action after confirmation.
Keep the search state, selected slot, and reservation context synchronized with URL parameters so searches can be bookmarked and revisited.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive with or without pre-filled filters and view available lanes
1) User opens lap_lanes.html via navigation or a quick link.
2) On page load, the URL is read for parameters: date, startTime, endTime, poolType, maxPrice, sortBy.
3) The page calls getLapLaneFilterOptions to obtain available pool types, time ranges, and a suggested default maximum price.
4) Filter controls (date, time, pool type, price, sort) are initialized using URL parameters where present; otherwise, defaults from getLapLaneFilterOptions are used.
5) The page calls searchLapLaneSlots with the resolved date, time range, pool type, maximum price, sortBy, and default values for other parameters (such as includeReserved).
6) Returned LapLaneSlot records are rendered as lane slot cards in the results list.
7) If no slots are returned, the user sees a functional message (e.g., no results) and can adjust filters.

Workflow 2: Adjust filters and sort order to refine search
1) User changes the date in the date picker.
2) The filter component updates the date state and updates the URL parameter date.
3) The results component calls searchLapLaneSlots again with the new date and existing time, pool type, price, and sort values.
4) Updated LapLaneSlot results replace the previous results in the list.
5) Similar behavior occurs when the user adjusts time range, pool type, maximum price, or sort order: state and URL parameters are updated, then searchLapLaneSlots is re-invoked to refresh the results.

Workflow 3: View details for a specific lane slot
1) From the results list, the user clicks a lane slot card or its "Reserve" control.
2) The selected slot ID is stored in page state, and the URL is updated with a parameter (e.g., selectedSlotId) representing the chosen LapLaneSlot.id.
3) The slot detail panel opens and calls getLapLaneSlotDetail with the selected lapLaneSlotId.
4) The panel displays detailed information from the LapLaneSlot and related Pool, including date, time, lane number, pool type, pool name, and price.
5) The user can close the detail panel (clearing selectedSlotId from state and URL) or proceed to the reservation form embedded in or attached to this panel.

Workflow 4: Complete a reservation for a lane slot
1) With a slot selected and its detail panel open, the user accesses the reservation form.
2) The user enters swimmer name, number of swimmers, and contact phone in the form.
3) On submit, the form validates required fields and logical constraints (such as swimmers_count being a positive number and, where appropriate, within bounds of max_swimmers if that field is present on the slot).
4) If validation passes, the form calls reserveLapLaneSlot with lapLaneSlotId (from the selected slot), swimmerName, swimmersCount, and contactPhone.
5) If reserveLapLaneSlot indicates success, the response LapLaneReservation and updatedSlot are stored in state; a reservationId parameter is added to the URL for deep-linking to the confirmation.
6) The page then calls getLapLaneReservationSummary with reservationId to ensure consistent confirmation details.
7) A confirmation component displays the reservation summary (LapLaneReservation, LapLaneSlot, Pool) along with functional actions such as "View My Schedule" (navigates to my_schedule.html) and an option to start a new search.
8) If reserveLapLaneSlot returns success=false or an error message (such as when the slot has just been reserved by someone else), the user sees the message, and the page may trigger a new searchLapLaneSlots call to refresh availability.

Workflow 5: Return to an existing search or reservation via bookmark or back navigation
1) User returns to lap_lanes.html via a saved link or browser history, which may include date, startTime, endTime, poolType, maxPrice, sortBy, selectedSlotId, and/or reservationId.
2) On load, the URL state manager reads these parameters.
3) Filters are initialized to match the URL parameters and searchLapLaneSlots is called to re-populate the lane slot list.
4) If selectedSlotId is present, the slot detail panel opens automatically and getLapLaneSlotDetail is invoked for that slot.
5) If reservationId is present, the confirmation component calls getLapLaneReservationSummary and shows the confirmation summary directly, allowing the user to review an existing reservation without re-submitting the form.
interactions: Changing the date input updates the date filter state, updates the URL date parameter, and triggers a new searchLapLaneSlots call.
Selecting or adjusting a time range (via dropdown or custom inputs) updates startTime and endTime state, writes them to the URL, and triggers searchLapLaneSlots.
Selecting a pool type filter updates poolType state, writes it to the URL, and triggers searchLapLaneSlots.
Adjusting the maximum price filter updates maxPrice state, writes it to the URL, and triggers searchLapLaneSlots.
Selecting a sort option (such as sorting by price or time) updates sortBy state, writes it to the URL, and triggers searchLapLaneSlots.
Clicking a lane slot card in the results list sets the selected slot ID in state, writes selectedSlotId to the URL, opens the slot detail panel, and triggers getLapLaneSlotDetail.
Clicking a "Reserve" action from within a slot card or detail panel focuses the reservation form for that LapLaneSlot.
Submitting the reservation form triggers client-side validation and, on success, calls reserveLapLaneSlot; based on the response, either shows error feedback or advances to the confirmation state.
On a successful reservation, the confirmation component calls getLapLaneReservationSummary using the reservationId returned from reserveLapLaneSlot to display authoritative reservation details.
Clicking "View My Schedule" from the confirmation state navigates to my_schedule.html without additional URL parameters.
Clicking an action like "Modify search" or closing the confirmation panel returns the user to the search/results view while preserving the current URL filter parameters.
Using browser back/forward updates the visible state (filters, selected slot panel, confirmation view) by re-reading URL parameters rather than relying on localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the primary source of truth for search filters, selection, and confirmation context. On initial load, the page reads date, startTime, endTime, poolType, maxPrice, and sortBy from the URL when present; these may be provided by index.html or schedule.html links. If any are missing, defaults are determined using getLapLaneFilterOptions (for example, by using the API's suggested defaultMaxPrice or default time ranges) or reasonable page-level defaults (such as the current date) without hardcoding specific data values. After resolving the active filter set, the page calls searchLapLaneSlots with date, startTime, endTime, poolType, maxPrice, and sortBy (and any internal defaults such as includeReserved) to populate results. The page extends URL state with additional internal parameters: selectedSlotId is added whenever a user selects a slot; its presence on load causes the slot detail panel to open and triggers getLapLaneSlotDetail. reservationId is added after a successful call to reserveLapLaneSlot; its presence on load causes getLapLaneReservationSummary to be called and the confirmation component to be shown. All user-driven filter and sort changes update both in-memory state and the corresponding URL parameters, then re-invoke searchLapLaneSlots. Closing the slot detail panel removes selectedSlotId from the URL (typically via replaceState) without affecting filter parameters. Returning from confirmation to the search view may clear reservationId from the URL while leaving filters intact. No search or booking state is stored in localStorage; all sharable or restorable state flows through the URL parameters in combination with fresh interface calls.

components.functionality: Manages all search filters and sort options for lap lane reservations, initializes from URL parameters and getLapLaneFilterOptions, and triggers lane slot searches.
Displays the list of lap lane slots returned by searchLapLaneSlots as selectable cards, handles loading and empty states, and propagates slot selection events.
Represents a single LapLaneSlot in the results, showing key slot information and offering a click/"Reserve" interaction to select the slot for detailed viewing and reservation.
Shows detailed information for the selected lap lane slot, including related pool details, and provides the context for the reservation form.
Collects user input for reserving the currently selected lap lane slot (swimmer name, swimmers count, contact phone) and submits the reservation via reserveLapLaneSlot.
Displays a confirmed reservation summary using getLapLaneReservationSummary, including lane, time, pool details, and offers navigation to the user’s schedule or back to search.

Coordinates reading and writing of URL parameters (date, startTime, endTime, poolType, maxPrice, sortBy, selectedSlotId, reservationId), ensures components react to URL changes, and avoids localStorage for persistent state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the site-wide pattern: utility bar and logo/nav header at the very top, followed by the existing full-width hero band (with bright, poster-like aquatic graphics), and then a single centered main content column for this Lap Lane Reservations experience. Within that main column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: page title and short intro copy, then a prominent search/filter band, then the results list of lane slots. Lane slot details, the reservation form, and the confirmation panel all appear inline beneath the selected slot in the same vertical flow, expanding and collapsing within the column rather than sending users to a separate page or side panel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the site-wide pattern: utility bar and logo/nav header at the very top, followed by the existing full-width hero band (with bright, poster-like aquatic graphics), and then a single centered main content column for this Lap Lane Reservations experience. Within that main column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: page title and short intro copy, then a prominent search/filter band, then the results list of lane slots. Lane slot details, the reservation form, and the confirmation panel all appear inline beneath the selected slot in the same vertical flow, expanding and collapsing within the column rather than sending users to a separate page or side panel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and hero continue to occupy roughly the top 50–60% of the first viewport, with the Lap Lane Reservations content starting just below. Within the main content area, the search filters band occupies the top 20–30% of the visible content height on initial load, with the results list taking up the majority of the remaining vertical space. Individual slot detail and reservation content expand downward, pushing later results down rather than overlaying the whole screen, keeping the main column intact. The content column is constrained to a max width (roughly 960–1140px), centered, and aligned to an implicit 12-column grid where interactive elements (filters and cards) snap to column widths. Vertical gaps of 32–48px separate major sections (intro > filters, filters > results, results > footer).
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium. The main background is predominantly white with ample margins to left and right of the central column. The search filters and results list are structured but spacious: individual filters use 8–16px padding; cards have 16–24px internal padding; and there is 16–24px vertical spacing between list items. Detail and reservation areas pack more information, but they are divided into clear blocks and columns so they never feel cramped. This mirrors the mockup: visually dense hero graphics isolated in one band, with the functional UI below kept clean and breathable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the top-left logo through the horizontal nav into the colorful hero band, then drops into the main content column. Within this column, attention first lands on the Lap Lane Reservations heading and short intro, then on the horizontally organized search filters band. From there, the gaze follows a vertical scan down the results list, card by card. When a slot is selected, the expanded detail panel and reservation form appear immediately beneath that card, drawing focus to the center of the page. The confirmation state keeps the user in that same spatial context, with a bold heading and summary centered within the expanded panel. A floating site-wide chat/help button remains near the bottom-left of the viewport as a peripheral but visible action, consistent with the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s bright, full-width hero banner, the Lap Lane Reservations page is organized as a single, centered content column on a white background, aligned to a 12-column grid. A clear page title and one to two lines of welcoming, slightly editorial intro text sit at the top of this column, with generous breathing room above and below. Directly beneath, a tinted, rounded search filters band stretches across roughly 80–90% of the column width, visually echoing the hero’s four-column structure by arranging filters in a horizontal grid. Below this, the results list fills most of the page height, presenting lane slot cards stacked vertically with consistent spacing. Selecting a slot causes a detail and booking panel to unfold right below that card, in-line, occupying the full width of the column but maintaining generous padding and a structured layout. The reservation form sits inside this panel, typically on the right half on desktop, with lane details on the left, all within the same visual band. After booking, the form area transitions into a centered confirmation panel that still lives in the same spot in the scroll, reinforcing continuity. Throughout, vertical spacing between these bands (32–48px) and clear left/right margins keep the layout light and readable, while bright accent colors and buttons provide energy and clear calls to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lap lane search filters sit at the top of the main content column, directly below the page title and brief intro text. They form a distinct horizontal band that spans about 80–90% of the content width, centered within the page and aligned with the underlying 12-column grid. The band has a subtle tinted background (e.g., a very light brand color) and softly rounded corners, visually separating it from the white page background. Inside this band, filters are laid out in a multi-column grid that echoes the hero’s four-card strip: on desktop, up to four filter groups (e.g., Date, Time of Day, Lane Type, Additional Options) sit side-by-side as four equal-width columns, each effectively spanning 3 of the 12 grid columns. Each filter block uses 16–24px internal padding with a clear label on top and the control (dropdown, date picker, toggle) beneath, with 8–12px vertical spacing. Horizontally, there is 16–24px padding on the left and right edges of the band. A prominent primary button (e.g., “Search Lanes” or auto-search indicator) sits aligned to the right edge of the band, either in its own column or anchored under the last filter group. The vertical space above and below this band is generous (32–40px), making it feel like a key, hero-like control zone rather than a cramped form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The search filters band scrolls with the page but becomes sticky once its top edge reaches the bottom of the main navigation: it remains fixed at the top of the visible content area within the scrolling main column. This keeps the filters always accessible while the user scrolls through results, without overlapping the global header. On narrower screens, the band remains full-width but the internal layout collapses to two or one filter per row while preserving vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lap lane results list is positioned immediately below the search filters band, with a clear vertical gap of 24–32px separating them. It sits within the same centered content column, spanning roughly the same max width and aligning with the same left/right margins. At the top of the list, a compact header row—containing a results count (e.g., “12 available lanes”) and optional sorting or tag filters—is aligned left, while an optional view toggle or helper text can align right, all within one row. Below this header, individual lap lane slot cards are stacked vertically, each card occupying nearly the full width of the column (leaving 16–24px internal padding to the content edge). The spacing between each card is consistent (16–24px vertical margin), creating a comfortable rhythm while allowing multiple cards (3–5) to be visible in the initial viewport below the filters. The list itself is not enclosed in a separate boxed container; instead, it feels like a continuous column of cards on the white background, anchored by the centered alignment and consistent side margins. As users scroll, the list extends downward linearly, reinforcing the top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results list scrolls naturally with the page as a single vertical column. It does not have its own internal scrollbar or fixed height; instead, the browser scroll controls movement. The sticky search filters sit above and remain visible, effectively anchoring the top of the list. When a slot card is expanded, the detail panel inserts directly into the vertical flow under that card, pushing subsequent results downward while preserving all list positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each lap lane slot card is a horizontally oriented card within the results list that spans the content column width, minus modest horizontal padding from the page margins. On desktop, the card width effectively covers about 10 of the 12 grid columns, centered, giving the impression of a full-width strip with 16–24px of white space to either side inside the main column. The card has a white background, subtle drop shadow or soft border, and 16–24px internal padding, aligning with the medium-density feel. The internal layout uses two main zones: the left-two-thirds area displays the primary slot information (time range in a larger, bold type, lane number, pool name, and key labels such as “Shallow” or “Outdoor”), stacked with 4–8px spacing between text lines. The right-one-third area is reserved for a status indicator and primary action. A colored tag or pill indicating availability (e.g., “3 lanes left”) sits near the top-right; a primary button (e.g., “Reserve”) is aligned to the right edge near the vertical center or slightly lower. This creates a clear call-to-action area on the right, matching the F-pattern scan: left for information, right for the action. When hovered or focused, the card slightly elevates and its border/accent color becomes more pronounced, consistent with the bright, family-friendly style. The vertical distortion is modest so the list stays visually calm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Slot cards themselves remain static within the list and scroll with the page. When a card is selected (clicked or tapped), it visually indicates its active state (e.g., thicker border or colored highlight) and the slot detail panel is inserted immediately below it. The card does not expand dramatically in height; instead, it acts as an anchor and trigger for the detail panel that appears beneath it in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lap lane slot detail panel appears directly beneath the corresponding slot card, occupying the full width of the content column and aligning perfectly with the card’s left and right edges. Visually, it feels like an extension or unfolding of the selected card into a deeper information band. The panel background is white or a slightly tinted variation, with a subtle border or top accent bar in a brand color to distinguish it from the surrounding list. Inside, it uses generous padding (24–32px) and a two-column layout on desktop: the left 55–60% of the panel is dedicated to expanded slot details (lane number, exact time, pool map snippet or location description, depth, lane sharing rules, accessibility notes, and any policy text). This information is arranged in grouped clusters with clear subheadings and 8–12px spacing between rows. The right 40–45% is reserved for the reservation form block, allowing the user to review details while completing the booking. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, this layout collapses to a single column: high-priority details appear first, followed by the reservation form stacked below, maintaining a comfortable 24px gap between sections. The panel’s top edge sits close under the slot card (8–12px gap), making the connection obvious, while a larger gap (24–32px) separates its bottom edge from the next slot card to keep the expanded area visually grouped. The panel is tall enough that, when opened, it typically occupies most of the visible viewport height, emphasizing focus on the selected slot while still allowing the sticky filters and the triggering card to remain within easy scroll reach.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The detail panel is collapsible and appears only when a specific slot card is active. It animates into view by sliding down or expanding vertically from under the card, then collapses back up when closed or when another slot is selected. It is anchored in the document flow—not fixed—so it pushes subsequent content downward. Only one detail panel is expanded at a time to maintain clarity and prevent the page from becoming overly tall at a single point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lap lane reservation form lives within the right-hand section of the slot detail panel on desktop and at the lower portion of the panel on smaller screens. On desktop, it occupies roughly 40–45% of the panel width, aligned to the right with consistent left padding separating it from the details column (24–32px). This form area is visually framed as a slightly more prominent block: a soft subtle background tint or light border helps differentiate it while staying harmonized with the rest of the panel. The form fields are stacked vertically, each spanning the full width of the form area with 8–12px spacing between label and input and 16px vertical spacing between fields. Typical fields (e.g., swimmer name, contact details, number of swimmers, special notes) are grouped logically, and if there is a step indicator (e.g., Step 2 of 3), it appears at the top left of this block. The primary submit button (“Complete Reservation”) is placed at the bottom of the form, full-width within the form column, with 16–24px top margin to separate it from the last field. Overall, the reservation section feels comfortably sized, not cramped, with sufficient padding (24px) around and clear alignment with the grid. On tablet and mobile, the form expands to 100% of the panel width, placed immediately after critical slot details, with each form element still respecting the 16px spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The reservation form scrolls along with the detail panel content. It does not float independently; instead, it remains anchored within the expanded panel. On small screens, if the panel content exceeds viewport height, the user scrolls the page to reach lower fields. Optional enhancements—like keeping the primary CTA button visible by placing a duplicate at the top of the form or using a sticky footer within the panel on very small devices—can be applied while still respecting the overall vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lap lane reservation confirmation panel occupies the same spatial area as the reservation form within the slot detail panel, preserving the user’s sense of location. After a successful submission, the form area transitions into a confirmation state: the right-hand section (or the full-width section on mobile) transforms into a centered confirmation block. This block features a bold, friendly heading at the top (e.g., “You’re all set!”) using slightly larger text, followed by a concise summary of the reservation: lane number, date, time, swimmer name, and any key reminders presented in a clean, two-column summary layout or a lightly bordered table. An optional success icon (e.g., a check mark in a brand-colored circle) appears above or to the left of the heading, adding visual emphasis. The confirmation content is surrounded by ample padding (24–32px) and increased top and bottom spacing within the panel (32–48px) to make the state feel calm and complete. The left side of the detail panel can still show summary slot details, or the confirmation can stretch across the full panel width, depending on the level of redundancy desired, but in either case the confirmation stays visually grouped as one coherent band. A secondary button or link for “Book another lane” returns the user’s focus to the results list, typically via a small button aligned to the bottom-right or centered under the summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The confirmation panel is static within the scroll and replaces the reservation form within the detail panel. It does not open a new page or overlay; the user remains in the same vertical location in the list where they initiated the booking. Closing the detail panel or navigating back to the search list returns the view to its previous state, with URL-state sync preserving whether the confirmation can be revisited directly via the browser’s back/forward controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the UI. Conceptually, it sits at the page level, tying together the search filters, results list, active slot selection, and reservation/confirmation states. It continuously reflects the current search criteria (date, time, lane type), the currently selected slot (if any), and the booking step (browsing, viewing details, filling the form, or confirmation) in the URL’s query parameters or hash. This enables users to share or bookmark a specific filtered view or a specific slot detail state without adding visible controls or clutter to the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The state manager is logically fixed at the page scope: it listens to interactions across the main content (filters, slot cards, reservation form) and updates the URL and back/forward history accordingly. It has no visual footprint and does not affect spacing or alignment. When a user navigates using the browser’s back or forward buttons, this component restores the visible state of the filters, results, open detail panel, and whether the confirmation view is shown, while keeping the scroll position as close as possible to the previously viewed slot.

name: Memberships
title: Memberships
description: Page for finding and comparing membership plans by applying filters (type, duration, access, price) and sorting, with navigation to detailed membership pages for purchase.

core_features: Initialize membership filter controls using data from getMembershipFilterOptions, including membership types, durations, access labels, price ranges, and sort options.
Parse incoming URL parameters (membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, sortBy) to pre-populate filters and sort order.
Execute membership plan search via searchMembershipPlans using the current filter and sort state, constrained to active plans.
Display a list/grid of membership plan cards bound to MembershipPlan data returned from searchMembershipPlans.
Include key information on each membership card (name, type, duration, price, included access, weekday hours where available).
Allow users to adjust filters (type, duration, access, price) and sort order, updating both the results and the URL parameters without using local storage.
Support sequential/manual comparison by allowing users to open membership_detail.html for any plan, with the current list state preserved via URL parameters when users navigate back.
Provide an applied-filters summary and a clear-all option that resets filters and re-runs searchMembershipPlans.
Handle empty result sets (no plans returned) with a functional message and controls to modify or clear filters.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Initial page load from navigation: 1) User clicks the 'Memberships' link in header/footer or uses a direct link without query parameters. 2) Page reads URL parameters (none or partial) and builds an internal filter state with defaults (e.g., no specific type selected if not provided). 3) Page calls getMembershipFilterOptions to retrieve available membershipTypes, durations, accessOptions, priceRanges, and sortOptions. 4) Filter controls and sort controls are populated from getMembershipFilterOptions, and any URL-provided values are pre-selected where they match available options. 5) Page calls searchMembershipPlans with the current filter state and status set to only return active plans. 6) Results list displays membership plan cards. 7) User can scroll and inspect plans.

Workflow 2 – Arrive with pre-applied filters (e.g., from index.html or about.html): 1) User clicks a link on index.html or about.html that includes one or more query parameters (e.g., membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, sortBy). 2) Memberships page parses these parameters into the initial filter state. 3) Page calls getMembershipFilterOptions and populates filter controls. 4) Matching options in the filters are pre-selected to reflect the incoming parameters (e.g., the passed membershipType is selected in the membership type control if available). 5) Page calls searchMembershipPlans with the pre-applied filters and status set to active. 6) User sees a result set already narrowed according to the context from the previous page.

Workflow 3 – User refines filters: 1) User adjusts one or more filters (e.g., selects a membership type, picks a duration, toggles access options, adjusts a price limit). 2) Each change updates the internal filter state. 3) The URL query string is updated (using history APIs) to reflect the new filter values without reloading the page. 4) After each change (or after the user confirms, depending on implementation), the page calls searchMembershipPlans with the updated parameters and status set to active. 5) Membership list re-renders with the new results. 6) If no results are found, an empty-state component prompts the user to broaden or clear filters.

Workflow 4 – User changes sort order: 1) User selects a sort option from the sort control (e.g., price-based or time-based options defined by getMembershipFilterOptions.sortOptions). 2) Selected sort value updates the internal sortBy state. 3) The sortBy parameter in the URL is updated. 4) Page calls searchMembershipPlans again with the same filters but new sortBy. 5) Result list is re-rendered in the new order.

Workflow 5 – View membership details and compare: 1) User identifies a membership of interest in the results list. 2) User clicks the membership card (or a dedicated action within it). 3) Browser navigates to membership_detail.html with membershipPlanId set to the selected plan's id in the URL parameters. 4) User reviews details and can use browser back button to return to memberships.html; the list, filters, and sort order are restored based on URL parameters. 5) User can then click another membership to view its detail page, allowing manual comparison by visiting multiple detail pages in sequence or via multiple tabs.

Workflow 6 – Clear all filters: 1) User clicks a 'Clear all filters' or equivalent control. 2) Internal filter state is reset to its baseline (no specific type/duration/access/price constraint beyond any global defaults). 3) URL parameters for membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, and sortBy are removed or reset. 4) Page calls searchMembershipPlans with the cleared filter state and status set to active. 5) Results list updates to show the broader set of memberships.

Workflow 7 – Browser navigation with preserved state: 1) User interacts with filters and opens one or more membership details, causing the memberships.html URL to reflect the current state via query parameters. 2) When the user presses the back or forward buttons, the browser navigates between URLs with different parameter sets. 3) On each navigation, the memberships page re-parses the current URL, reconstructs the filter and sort state, re-runs searchMembershipPlans, and repopulates controls so the user sees a view consistent with that URL.
interactions: Selecting a membership type from a dropdown, segmented control, or equivalent input on the filter bar updates the membershipType state, syncs the membershipType query parameter, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.
Selecting a membership duration (e.g., via select or buttons) updates the duration state, syncs the duration query parameter, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.
Toggling pool access inclusion updates poolAccessIncluded (boolean) in state, syncs the poolAccessIncluded query parameter, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.
Toggling fitness center access inclusion updates fitnessCenterIncluded (boolean) in state, syncs the fitnessCenterIncluded query parameter, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.

Adjusting a price control (slider, range selector, or numeric field backed by priceRanges from getMembershipFilterOptions) updates maxPrice state, syncs the maxPrice query parameter, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.
Selecting a sort option from a sort control updates the sortBy state, syncs the sortBy query parameter, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.
Clicking on a membership card invokes navigation to membership_detail.html with membershipPlanId set to the card's underlying MembershipPlan.id.
Clicking a 'Clear filters' action resets all filter-related pieces of state, removes related URL parameters, and triggers searchMembershipPlans.

If implemented, a 'Show more filters' or collapsible filter section can be toggled to reveal or hide advanced options without changing current filter values; this toggle affects only UI state and does not call APIs directly.

When searchMembershipPlans returns a new plans array, the membership results list re-binds to this array and re-renders; if the array is empty, an empty-state message appears with a control to clear or adjust filters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Initial state: On load, the page reads the current URL's query parameters: membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, sortBy. Any missing parameters are treated as undefined in the filter state. A default status value (e.g., 'active') is applied internally and not controlled by URL.

Filter options: The page calls getMembershipFilterOptions once on initialization to obtain the available membershipTypes, durations, accessOptions, priceRanges, and sortOptions. These values populate the filter UI and sort UI. If URL parameters reference values that are not present in the options returned, those specific parameters are ignored for initial selection, and the filter UI falls back to an unselected state for that dimension.

Search execution: After building the initial filter state from URL parameters and available options, the page calls searchMembershipPlans with parameters derived from the current state: membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, sortBy, and status set to a value that returns only relevant (typically active) membership plans. Whenever any filter or the sort value changes, the same searchMembershipPlans call is re-executed with the updated values.

URL synchronization: The filter and sort state is treated as the single source of truth, mirrored into the URL query string on every change. When a user selects or clears any filter or sort option, the page updates the query parameters accordingly using navigation APIs (e.g., pushState or replaceState) without a full reload: membershipType is set or removed based on selection; duration is set or removed; poolAccessIncluded and fitnessCenterIncluded are set to appropriate boolean representations or removed when not enforced; maxPrice is set to the current numeric value or removed; sortBy is set to the selected sort option or removed if using a default. The status parameter is managed internally and not exposed in the URL.

Navigation and back/forward behavior: Because the URL parameters always reflect the current state, standard browser navigation restores previous views. On any page load or popstate event, the Memberships page re-parses the URL parameters, rebuilds filter and sort state, repopulates controls, and re-calls searchMembershipPlans. No state is stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies; the URL is the only persistent state mechanism for this page.

Outgoing navigation: When the user clicks a membership card, the page performs a navigation to membership_detail.html with membershipPlanId set to the selected MembershipPlan.id in the query string. The current memberships.html URL (with all its parameters) is preserved in the browser history, so returning via the back button restores the same state. The Memberships page itself does not pass additional parameters to membership_detail.html beyond membershipPlanId.

components.functionality: On initial load or when the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation), this component parses URL parameters into an internal filter/sort state, calls getMembershipFilterOptions to obtain available options, binds those options to filter and sort components, and then calls searchMembershipPlans with the derived parameters to populate the membership results list.

Provides user controls for filtering membership plans by membership type, duration, included access, and maximum price. It displays selectable options populated from getMembershipFilterOptions and reflects any current URL-derived selections. On change, it updates the shared filter state, syncs URL parameters, and triggers a new search via searchMembershipPlans.

Displays available sort options for membership plans using the sortOptions returned from getMembershipFilterOptions. The currently selected sort is initialized from the sortBy URL parameter when present. When the user selects a different sort option, this component updates the sortBy state, syncs it to the URL, and triggers searchMembershipPlans with the new sort order.

Shows a concise summary of all currently applied filters (membership type, duration, access requirements, price cap) derived from the current filter state. It allows the user to remove individual filters (e.g., clear membership type only) or clear all filters at once, which updates the URL parameters and re-runs searchMembershipPlans.
data_binding: Interface:getMembershipFilterOptions (for translating filter values into human-readable labels)

components.functionality: Renders the current set of membership plans as a list or grid of membership cards. It binds to the plans array returned from searchMembershipPlans. When the bound data changes, the component rebuilds the list of membership-card components. It also determines whether to show the empty-state component when there are no plans.

Represents a single membership plan in the results list. Displays fields from the bound MembershipPlan such as name, membership_type, duration, price, included access flags, and weekday_open_time/weekday_close_time when provided. On click, it navigates to membership_detail.html with membershipPlanId equal to the plan's id. It does not store local state beyond what is required for user interaction.

Acts as a controller that listens to changes from the filter bar and sort control components, assembles the current search parameters, updates the URL query string, and calls searchMembershipPlans. It ensures that membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, status, and sortBy are always passed consistently in each search call.

Displays a functional message when searchMembershipPlans returns an empty plans array, indicating that no memberships match the current filters. Provides controls to clear all filters or adjust specific filters, which delegate to the filter bar and search controller to update state, URL parameters, and re-run the search.

Manages two-way synchronization between the internal filter/sort state and the URL query parameters. On initialization and on browser navigation events, it reads parameters (membershipType, duration, poolAccessIncluded, fitnessCenterIncluded, maxPrice, sortBy) into state. On any state change triggered by user interaction, it updates the corresponding URL parameters using history APIs without reloading the page. It does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence.
data_binding: MembershipPlan (indirectly, through filter/sort state that drives search)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global utility/header and hero, the Memberships page starts with a single full-width search/filter hero band, then flows into a two-column content area: a narrower filter sidebar on the left and a wider results column on the right. Inside the results column, the sort control sits at the top, followed by either a grid of membership cards or an empty state. The applied filters summary is a slim horizontal band that bridges the search hero band and the two-column area, visually tying them together.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global utility/header and hero, the Memberships page starts with a single full-width search/filter hero band, then flows into a two-column content area: a narrower filter sidebar on the left and a wider results column on the right. Inside the results column, the sort control sits at the top, followed by either a grid of membership cards or an empty state. The applied filters summary is a slim horizontal band that bridges the search hero band and the two-column area, visually tying them together.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The search hero band occupies most of the initial viewport width within a centered max-width container, with generous vertical padding (roughly 32–48px) to feel prominent but shallow enough to leave room for the filters/results above the fold. Below it, the filter sidebar takes about 25–30% of the content width (roughly 3 of 12 columns), and the results column takes the remaining 70–75% (about 9 of 12 columns). Vertical spacing between major bands (search hero, applied filters, filters/results area) follows the 32–48px rhythm, while spacing inside components uses 8–24px padding based on the 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low-to-medium overall. The search band is roomy, with a large, easy-to-scan search input and minimal surrounding elements. The filter sidebar carries medium density with clearly separated groups and ample vertical spacing between filter sections. The results grid is visually richer due to multiple cards per row, but each card maintains comfortable padding and clear hierarchy. The empty state, when shown, is intentionally sparse and centered, keeping the page light and approachable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the page header into the Memberships search hero band at the top of the main content, scanning left-to-right across the search form. It then drops to the applied filters summary, reading any active chips. From there, attention splits to the left filter sidebar (for control) and the right sort control and results list (for browsing). Within the results, the eye follows a left-to-right, top-to-bottom scan of membership cards. When present, the empty state sits centered in the results area, becoming the main focal point once the user sees no cards. The layout preserves an F-pattern: controls start on the left (search, filters) and key decision tools (sort, card content) align along the top and right of the results column.

layout.overall_description: The Memberships page appears as a bright, welcoming search and compare experience beneath the site’s header and hero. A spacious, full-width search band sits at the top of the content area, centered within a max-width container, making it clear this is where users begin. Just below, a slim applied-filters bar stretches across the same width, lightly framing any active filter chips. The main body then resolves into a classic two-column layout: a tidy filter sidebar anchored on the left and a larger results column on the right. At the top of the results column, a compact sort control aligns to the right, reinforcing a familiar F-pattern. Below it, rows of colorful membership cards form a grid, each card resembling a friendly, poster-like tile that’s easy to compare at a glance. If no memberships match the criteria, a centered empty-state message replaces the grid, surrounded by generous white space. Structural spacing is consistent and airy, so even with numerous membership options, the page feels organized, readable, and family-friendly rather than cluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual component is logically positioned at the very top of the Memberships page content hierarchy, just inside the main content area that begins below the global header/hero. It does not render any UI and therefore occupies no visible width or height; it simply initializes page-level state and configuration before any visible components (search hero, filters, results) appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component with no impact on layout; it does not take up space or affect positioning of other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search controller forms the topmost visible band of the Memberships content area, immediately below the global header/hero. It sits inside a centered max-width container (around 1120–1200px) and spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width on large screens, leaving comfortable side margins. Vertically, it is treated like a shallow hero: a horizontal band with about 32–48px of padding above and below the search form. The primary search input is aligned to the left, matching the left edge of the subsequent filter/results grid, and typically spans about 60–70% of the container width, making it dominant and easy to target. Any supporting controls (e.g., quick filter toggles or submit button) align on the same horizontal row or just below, maintaining a linear, left-to-right reading order. White space around the search elements keeps density low and echoes the airy hero style from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow; it scrolls off the screen as the user moves down the page. On smaller screens, it may stack its internal elements vertically while maintaining the same full-width band behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters summary sits directly below the search controller, inside the same max-width container and aligned to its left and right edges. It appears as a low-height horizontal strip, about 40–56px tall, with 16–24px of vertical margin separating it from the search band above and the two-column section below. Visually, it spans nearly the full content width (roughly 100% of the container), with a compact label on the left followed by inline filter chips that wrap to multiple lines if needed. Its background is subtly distinct (e.g., a soft tint or hairline border) but lighter and less attention-grabbing than the search band, reinforcing its role as a status readout rather than a hero element. When no filters are applied, this component can either collapse completely (no height) or display a minimal, unobtrusive placeholder; in either case, it maintains the continuity between the search band and the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow. It pushes the filter sidebar and results area downward but does not have sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership filter bar occupies a left-aligned sidebar in the main content section beneath the applied filters summary. On desktop, it takes roughly 25–30% of the available content width, equivalent to about 3 of 12 grid columns, and extends vertically down the page alongside the results column. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the sort control in the results column, so both controls form a clean, shared baseline. There is a consistent horizontal gap of about 24–32px between the right edge of the filter bar and the left edge of the results column, preserving a clear distinction between controls and results. Inside the sidebar, filters are organized into stacked groups (e.g., Type, Duration, Access, Price) with 16–24px vertical spacing between groups and 8–16px padding around each control, giving it a medium-density, form-like feel that is still visually breathable. The background is white or very light, with subtle borders or dividers between filter sections to avoid heaviness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sidebar scrolls with the page but becomes sticky relative to the viewport once its top edge reaches a comfortable offset below the global header (roughly 64–96px), so it remains visible while the user scrolls through the results. On smaller screens, it collapses into an expandable panel positioned above the results list, transitioning from a sidebar to a full-width block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is placed at the top of the right-hand results column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter sidebar to its left. Within the results column (which occupies about 70–75% of the content width, around 9 of 12 grid columns), the sort control sits in a slim horizontal strip with 16–24px of vertical padding. The control itself is right-aligned within this strip to support the F-pattern: users glance across the top of the results to the right-hand side to change the sort order. A label may appear to the left of the dropdown or buttons, but the primary interactive element is anchored on the right. Below the sort control, there is about 16–24px of vertical space before the first row of membership cards. The background remains white to keep the focus on the control and the results just beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the results column, the sort control can be configured to stick to the top of the column’s visible area when the user scrolls, maintaining a small offset from the top of the viewport and staying just below any global sticky header. This ensures sorting remains easily accessible while scanning long lists of memberships.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the primary content column on the right, starting directly beneath the sort control, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between them. It spans roughly 70–75% of the content width (around 9 of 12 grid columns), aligned with the search band and applied filters container. Inside this column, membership cards are arranged in a responsive grid: on large desktops, three cards per row (each approximately 1/3 of the column width) with horizontal gaps of about 24px and vertical row spacing of 24–32px; on medium screens, the grid can collapse to two cards per row, and on small screens, to a single-column stack. The grid extends downward as needed, creating a visually dense but organized matrix of options. Generous outer padding (16–24px) around the grid keeps it from pressing against the container’s edges, while ample vertical spacing between sections ensures the area feels more like a curated product gallery than a cramped table of plans.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results list scrolls as part of the main page content. It dynamically expands or contracts based on the number of membership cards. When there are no results, it yields its space entirely to the empty state component within the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each membership card is a rectangular tile within the results grid, designed to echo the colorful, poster-like feel of the hero promotions while staying clean and readable. On large screens, a single card occupies roughly one-third of the results column width (about 3 of 9 columns), with a fixed aspect ratio that results in a medium height (approximately 260–320px depending on content). The card has 16–24px of internal padding and a slight drop shadow or border radius to separate it from the background. The layout within the card follows a clear vertical hierarchy: a vibrant colored header stripe or block at the top (10–20% of card height) highlighting the membership type (e.g., “Family Pass”), followed by a prominent price and key attributes area in the center, and one or two concise features/bullets. At the bottom, a full-width call-to-action button or link is anchored, with sufficient padding above and below (~12–16px) to ensure tap-friendly sizing. The card content is left-aligned, maintaining consistency with surrounding text, while the overall tile format supports quick side-by-side comparison across a row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Cards are fully responsive, resizing and reflowing based on the number of columns in the grid. They do not have independent scrolling; they simply flow within the grid container, adjusting width on different breakpoints while maintaining internal spacing and hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state component occupies the same right-hand results column as the membership grid, appearing only when there are no matching memberships. In that scenario, it replaces the grid entirely and is positioned below the sort control with a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to visually separate the control from the message. The empty state is centered horizontally within the results column, with its content constrained to roughly 50–60% of the column width to keep text lines short and readable. It typically includes a friendly illustration or icon at the top, followed by a clear headline and explanatory text, with optional inline actions (e.g., a Reset Filters button). Ample white space surrounds the empty state, making it feel like a calm pause rather than an error, matching the welcoming, family-friendly aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state is static within the results column and scrolls with the page. It only renders when there are no membership cards to show; otherwise, it takes up no space and has no impact on the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual state synchronization component is conceptually positioned alongside the search controller, filters, and sort control in the component tree, but it does not render any visible UI. It occupies zero width and height and simply observes and updates URL parameters to mirror the current search, filters, and sort selections. Its presence ensures that the visible controls and the results layout can be restored via the URL, without altering spatial relationships on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and layout-neutral; it has no spatial behavior and does not affect the size or position of any visible components.

name: Membership Detail
title: Membership Plan Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected membership plan and allows the user to purchase it and view purchase confirmation.

core_features: Load and display full membership plan details using getMembershipPlanDetail based on the membershipPlanId URL parameter.
Show plan benefits, included facilities, guest policies, and operating hours (including weekday closing time) from the plan and detail interface response.
Display pricing information for the selected membership plan and any textual pricing notes or eligibility information contained in the detail response.
Provide a purchase form where the user enters primary adult name, selects a membership start date, provides a contact email, and submits a purchase via purchaseMembership.
Show purchase success or failure feedback based on the purchaseMembership response, including any error message provided.
Display a purchase confirmation summary using getMembershipPurchaseSummary when a membershipPurchaseId is available (from a recent purchase or URL parameter).
Provide navigation actions to return to the list of membership plans (memberships.html) or go to My Schedule (my_schedule.html) after a successful purchase.
Handle invalid or missing membershipPlanId values and API errors with clear error states and retry options.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View membership details from Memberships listing:
1. User clicks a membership plan on memberships.html, which links to membership_detail.html with a membershipPlanId URL parameter.
2. On page load, the page reads membershipPlanId from the URL.
3. The page calls getMembershipPlanDetail(membershipPlanId).
4. While loading, a loading state is shown instead of details and the purchase form.
5. When getMembershipPlanDetail returns, the page displays the plan name, description, membership_type, duration, price, included facilities, operating hours (including weekday_open_time and weekday_close_time), benefitsSummary, guestPolicies, and termsAndConditions.
6. If plan.status indicates the plan is not active, the purchase form is disabled and a textual notice explains that the plan is not currently available for purchase.

Workflow 2 – Purchase a membership from the detail page:
1. User arrives on membership_detail.html with a valid membershipPlanId and sees the plan details and purchase form.
2. User selects a membership start date using a date input control (limited to valid dates by client-side logic, e.g., not in the past if that is required by business rules).
3. User enters the primary adult’s full name and a contact email address into the form fields.
4. User submits the purchase form.
5. The form validates that membershipPlanId, primaryAdultName, startDate, and contactEmail are present and that contactEmail appears to be in a valid email format.
6. If validation passes, the page calls purchaseMembership with membershipPlanId (from URL or loaded plan), primaryAdultName, startDate, and contactEmail.
7. While purchaseMembership is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a submitting indicator is shown.
8. If purchaseMembership returns success = true and a purchase object, the page stores the returned purchase.id in page state, optionally updates the URL to include membershipPurchaseId, and switches to a confirmation mode.
9. The page calls getMembershipPurchaseSummary(membershipPurchaseId) using the new purchase.id.
10. When getMembershipPurchaseSummary returns, the page displays a confirmation summary including key information from purchase (such as start_date and price_paid if available) and plan data (such as plan name and duration).
11. User can click 'View in My Schedule' to go to my_schedule.html or 'Back to Memberships' to return to memberships.html.

Workflow 3 – Return to a purchase confirmation from a saved link or My Schedule:
1. User accesses membership_detail.html via a link from my_schedule.html or another saved link that includes membershipPlanId and membershipPurchaseId in the URL.
2. On page load, the page reads membershipPlanId and membershipPurchaseId from the URL.
3. The page calls getMembershipPurchaseSummary(membershipPurchaseId) to load the purchase and associated plan.
4. In parallel or after membershipPurchaseId is validated, the page calls getMembershipPlanDetail(membershipPlanId) to load detailed descriptive fields (benefitsSummary, guestPolicies, operatingHoursSummary, termsAndConditions) for consistency with the normal detail view.
5. When both calls return successfully, the page shows the confirmation summary (highlighting purchase information) along with the plan detail sections.
6. The purchase form is either hidden or disabled in this mode to avoid accidental duplicate purchases, depending on business rules.
7. User can navigate to my_schedule.html or memberships.html using the provided buttons.

Workflow 4 – Handle errors and invalid parameters:
1. If membershipPlanId is missing from the URL on initial load, the page displays an error state explaining that no membership plan was specified and offers a link back to memberships.html.
2. If getMembershipPlanDetail returns an error or fails (e.g., network issue), the page displays an error message and a 'Retry' control which triggers the same getMembershipPlanDetail call again.
3. If purchaseMembership returns success = false, the page displays the returned message, keeps the form values, and allows the user to correct any issues and resubmit.
4. If getMembershipPurchaseSummary fails when membershipPurchaseId is present, the page displays an error message in the confirmation area and still allows the user to view basic plan details (from getMembershipPlanDetail) and, if allowed, attempt a new purchase.
interactions: On page load (initialization): read membershipPlanId and optional membershipPurchaseId from the URL query string. If membershipPlanId is present, call getMembershipPlanDetail; if membershipPurchaseId is also present, call getMembershipPurchaseSummary.
Click 'Back to Memberships': navigate to memberships.html without additional parameters, allowing the user to continue comparing plans.
Click 'Retry' on load error: re-invoke getMembershipPlanDetail with the same membershipPlanId from the URL.
Open/close sections (e.g., benefits, operating hours, guest policies, terms): toggle visibility of these sections so users can focus on relevant information while still accessing all details.
Change start date in purchase form: update internal form state, revalidate the date field, and ensure the selected date is used in the next purchaseMembership call.
Change primary adult name in purchase form: update internal form state and clear any validation error associated with that field.
Change contact email in purchase form: update internal form state and run email format validation, enabling or disabling the submit button accordingly.
Submit purchase form: perform client-side validation of required fields; if valid, call purchaseMembership. During the API call, disable the submit action, and show a processing state.

Handle purchaseMembership success: when success is true, capture the returned purchase.id, update the page URL to add membershipPurchaseId, switch to confirmation mode, and call getMembershipPurchaseSummary. Hide or disable the form to prevent duplicate submissions.

Handle purchaseMembership failure: when success is false or the API call fails, show the returned message (if available) or a generic error message and keep the form data intact so the user can revise and resubmit.

Load confirmation summary: when membershipPurchaseId is present (either from URL on load or after a successful purchase), call getMembershipPurchaseSummary and bind the returned purchase and plan data into the confirmation component.
Click 'View in My Schedule' in the confirmation area: navigate to my_schedule.html (no additional parameters), where the user can see their membership in context with other items.
