Click 'Edit Start Date and Repurchase' (if allowed by business rules): clear or override membershipPurchaseId in URL and switch back to detail mode so the user can initiate a new purchase for the same plan.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is controlled by URL parameters and API responses.

1) membershipPlanId (required for standard detail view):
- If membershipPlanId is absent on initial load, the page enters a 'missing-plan' state and shows an error message with navigation back to memberships.html.
- If membershipPlanId is present, the page calls getMembershipPlanDetail(membershipPlanId) and enters a 'loading-plan' state.
- On successful getMembershipPlanDetail response, the page enters a 'plan-loaded' state and binds the returned plan and associated descriptive strings (benefitsSummary, guestPolicies, operatingHoursSummary, termsAndConditions) to the corresponding components.
- If getMembershipPlanDetail fails, the page enters a 'plan-error' state and exposes a retry interaction.

2) membershipPurchaseId (optional, indicates confirmation context):
- If membershipPurchaseId is not present in the URL and no purchase has just been completed, the page stays in 'detail-only' mode: it shows plan details and the purchase form (subject to plan.status), but no confirmation summary.
- If membershipPurchaseId is present on initial load, the page calls getMembershipPurchaseSummary(membershipPurchaseId) and enters a 'loading-confirmation' state parallel to loading plan details.
- On successful getMembershipPurchaseSummary, the page enters a 'confirmation-loaded' state and shows the confirmation summary. The purchase form may be hidden or disabled in this state, depending on business rules.
- If getMembershipPurchaseSummary fails, the page enters a 'confirmation-error' state: plan details remain visible, but confirmation content is replaced with an error message, and the user may still navigate away.

3) Transition after purchase:
- When the user submits the purchase form in 'detail-only' mode, the page enters a 'purchase-submitting' state and calls purchaseMembership with membershipPlanId, primaryAdultName, startDate, and contactEmail from the form.
- If purchaseMembership returns success = true and a purchase object, the page extracts purchase.id and updates the URL to include membershipPurchaseId = purchase.id (for example, via a full redirect or history API update). The page then transitions into 'confirmation-pending' state and calls getMembershipPurchaseSummary(membershipPurchaseId).
- Once getMembershipPurchaseSummary returns successfully, the page transitions into 'confirmation-loaded' state and shows the confirmation summary.
- If purchaseMembership fails, the state returns to 'plan-loaded' with an additional 'purchase-error' flag used by the purchase form component to render error messaging.

4) Optional view mode param (if implemented):
- The page may optionally use a viewMode URL parameter with values such as 'detail' or 'confirmation'. If viewMode = 'confirmation' is present without membershipPurchaseId, the page treats this as invalid and falls back to 'detail' mode while displaying a warning, because confirmation requires a specific purchase.

5) No localStorage:
- All navigable state (selected plan and whether the page is in confirmation mode) is derived from the URL parameters membershipPlanId and membershipPurchaseId plus live API responses. Temporary UI state (e.g., form field values before submission, loading flags, and validation errors) is held only in in-memory page/component state and is not persisted to localStorage.

components.functionality: On initial page load and whenever membershipPlanId in the URL changes, this component reads membershipPlanId from the URL, calls getMembershipPlanDetail, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded plan and detail strings to child components.

Displays high-level information about the membership plan loaded by membership-detail-loader, including plan name, description, membership_type, duration, and a concise overview derived from benefitsSummary and operatingHoursSummary.

Shows a structured view of benefits and included access for the membership plan. It uses plan fields such as pool_access_included and fitness_center_included along with the benefitsSummary text from getMembershipPlanDetail to describe what facilities and privileges are included.

Displays guest policies and any additional notes related to guests for this membership plan, using the guestPolicies text from getMembershipPlanDetail. The component may support expandable/collapsible content if the text is long.

Displays operating hours information for the membership, including weekday_open_time and weekday_close_time and any descriptive text from operatingHoursSummary, so users understand access times associated with the plan.

Shows the pricing information for the membership plan using plan.price and any descriptive pricing notes or eligibility details found in the text fields (such as benefitsSummary or termsAndConditions). It highlights if any special eligibility such as resident versus non-resident pricing is mentioned in the received text without hardcoding specific pricing logic.
Displays key terms, policies, and renewal information using the termsAndConditions string from getMembershipPlanDetail. It can support 'show more/show less' behavior to manage long policy text.

Provides input fields for primary adult name, membership start date, and contact email, performs client-side validation, and on submit calls purchaseMembership with membershipPlanId from URL and the entered form data. It manages submitting state, displays validation errors, handles API error messages, and, on success, triggers the transition to confirmation mode.

Coordinates between the purchase form and confirmation summary based on URL parameters and API responses. After a successful purchaseMembership call, it updates the URL to include membershipPurchaseId, triggers getMembershipPurchaseSummary, and switches the visible UI from 'detail-only' to 'confirmation' mode.

When membershipPurchaseId is available, this component uses getMembershipPurchaseSummary to display a confirmation summary that includes core purchase details (from MembershipPurchase) and associated plan information (from MembershipPlan). It presents confirmation messaging and key data such as plan name and start_date and exposes actions to go to My Schedule or back to Memberships.

Provides navigation buttons and links for returning to the memberships listing and for viewing the membership in My Schedule after purchase. It does not modify data, only triggers navigation based on the current state (e.g., the 'View in My Schedule' button is only active when a purchase exists).

Displays contextual error and status messages for plan loading, purchase submission, and confirmation summary loading. It listens to error states from the data-loader, purchase form, and confirmation components and renders user-friendly messages along with actions like retry or navigation back to memberships.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top‑anchored, banded layout as the mockup: global utility bar and header (outside this spec), then a full‑width hero‑style band dedicated to the membership overview, and below that a center‑constrained content column where all detailed panels, pricing, and purchase flow are stacked vertically. The hero band behaves as a single, visually strong section (membership overview), while the rest of the content flows in a clean, linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence inside a max‑width container.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top‑anchored, banded layout as the mockup: global utility bar and header (outside this spec), then a full‑width hero‑style band dedicated to the membership overview, and below that a center‑constrained content column where all detailed panels, pricing, and purchase flow are stacked vertically. The hero band behaves as a single, visually strong section (membership overview), while the rest of the content flows in a clean, linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence inside a max‑width container.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero membership overview band takes up the majority of the above‑the‑fold height on desktop (roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport), with the header above remaining shallow. Below the hero, the main content column sits centered at ~80–90% page width (max ~1040–1200px), with 32–48px vertical spacing between major blocks: pricing/purchase area, each information panel (benefits, policies, hours, terms), and the bottom navigation controls. Within panels, padding follows the 8px scale (typically 16–24px internal padding). The loader, when active, temporarily claims the entire visible content area as an overlay.
layout_strategies.content_density: The membership overview and pricing/purchase blocks are relatively graphic and information‑dense, acting like promotional cards, while the supporting detail panels are more relaxed and text‑oriented with generous line heights and whitespace. The main content column is intentionally low‑to‑medium density, prioritizing clarity and readability. The hero band and pricing panel carry the visual energy; the benefits and terms sections feel more editorial and open.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the site logo and nav (global) down into the membership overview hero band at the top of this page, scanning left‑to‑right across the key membership name, tagline, and high‑level highlights. From there, the eye drops directly into the centered content column, first encountering price and primary purchase actions, then scrolling down through benefits, guest policies, hours, and terms in a clear vertical sequence. Any error notice appears near the top of this column, intercepting attention immediately after the hero. The purchase confirmation occupies the same visual region as the form so the user perceives a smooth state change rather than a jump. A floating site‑wide chat button in the lower left of the viewport remains peripheral but noticeable as a secondary action.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s utility bar and horizontal navigation, the Membership Detail page opens with a bright, full‑width hero band dedicated to the selected membership. This band stretches edge‑to‑edge but its content is centered within a max‑width container, following a 12‑column grid. On larger screens, the left two‑thirds highlight the membership name, short description, and a few key badges (e.g., family, lap swim, kids programs), arranged in a promotional, poster‑like composition. The right portion of this same band can include supporting highlights or imagery, maintaining an energetic, family‑friendly feel against a vibrant background. Below this hero, the layout tightens into a single, centered content column occupying about 80–90% of the page width. At the top of this column sits the pricing and purchase area: a clear pricing card followed by a generously padded purchase form, so the primary call‑to‑action is encountered early with minimal friction. Once a purchase is completed, that same area smoothly transforms into a confirmation panel, preserving the spatial context. Further down, the supporting information panels—benefits, guest policies, hours, and terms—are stacked vertically with 32–48px of breathing room between them, using large headings and comfortable line spacing so families can easily skim and read the details. A slim navigation control strip at the very bottom of the content column offers links to navigate back to the memberships list or to other plans. Throughout, the page maintains a low‑to‑medium density, combining colorful hero and call‑to‑action elements with clean, editorial‑style text sections, while a floating chat widget at the bottom left of the viewport remains visible but unobtrusive as a secondary help option.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi‑opaque overlay that covers the entire membership detail content area from just below the global header to the bottom of the viewport. It spans 100% of the available width, including the hero membership overview band and the centered content column beneath it. In the center of the viewport, a circular loading indicator and a short status label (e.g., “Loading membership details…”) are vertically and horizontally centered, occupying roughly the middle third of the screen. All underlying panels (overview, pricing, form, details) remain visually dimmed and non‑interactive while the loader is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full‑viewport overlay positioned above all membership detail components; blocks interaction and scroll while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This panel forms the hero band at the top of the Membership Detail page, immediately below the site header. It stretches full‑width edge‑to‑edge across the viewport, with its content constrained to a centered max‑width container (~1040–1200px) aligned to an implicit 12‑column grid. On desktop, the content within this band is split horizontally: the left 7–8 columns (roughly two‑thirds of the container width) feature the membership name in large type, a short tagline, and a concise summary paragraph with relaxed line spacing, plus 1–3 colorful badges indicating the membership type (family, adult, youth). The right 4–5 columns can display secondary highlights or a playful aquatic illustration, keeping the band visually engaging. Vertical padding of 32–48px above and below the text makes this hero feel tall—approximately 40–50% of the initial viewport height. The background is bright and energetic (e.g., gradient or water‑themed graphic), while the typographic layout remains clean so the membership identity is clear at a glance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full‑width hero section; content is center‑constrained and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The benefits panel sits within the main content column, below the pricing and purchase area and above the guest policies panel. It shares the same center‑constrained width (about 80–90% of the page, max ~1040–1200px) and is aligned to the same vertical column as the other detail sections. There is a clear vertical gap of 32–40px above it. At the top of the panel, a large, friendly heading (e.g., “What’s Included in Your Membership”) and a short, slightly larger introductory paragraph mimic the mockup’s editorial intro section, occupying the full width and prioritizing readability over density. Below this intro, benefits are arranged in 1–2 columns depending on screen width: on desktop, a two‑column list of key benefits with icons and short descriptions; on smaller screens, these stack into a single column. Internal padding of 24px keeps the content airy, and the panel may sit on a subtly tinted background or card outline to visually group it while keeping the overall feel clean.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the vertical content flow; expands vertically with content and maintains consistent margins relative to adjacent panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This panel appears directly beneath the benefits panel, separated by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace within the same centered content column. It spans the same width as the benefits panel and aligns flush with its left and right edges to maintain a strong vertical rhythm. The layout is simpler and more text‑driven: a clear heading (“Guest Policies”) at the top, followed by short paragraphs or a single column of bullet points outlining guest rules. Line height is generous to keep the legal and rule‑type content readable for parents. Internal padding of 16–24px surrounds the text, and the visual styling is slightly more subdued than the benefits panel (e.g., white or very light background, subtle divider line at the top) to reflect that this is essential information but not the primary selling feature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the main content stack; scrolls with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hours panel is placed below the guest policies panel with a 24–32px vertical gap, maintaining the centered column alignment. It uses the same width as the other detail panels (roughly 80–90% of the page width, max ~1040–1200px). At the top, a bold heading (“Hours & Access”) is left‑aligned, followed by a structured layout of operating hours. On desktop, the content may be arranged into two columns: one for regular weekly hours and another for special or seasonal hours; on mobile, this collapses into a single stacked column. Each row of hours has comfortable vertical spacing (~8–12px between rows) to avoid crowding. Internal padding of about 16–24px and clear table‑like alignment help quick scanning, while the overall panel remains visually light so it doesn’t compete with the hero or pricing‑purchase area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element, aligned with the main content column and extending vertically as needed for the hours content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing panel is the first substantive element in the centered content column beneath the hero overview, positioned with a generous 32–40px gap from the bottom of the hero band. It spans the full width of the content column (about 80–90% of page width within the max‑width container), appearing as a slightly elevated card with a white or very light background. Internally, the layout uses a 12‑column grid: on desktop, the left 7–8 columns present the membership price in large, bold type (e.g., monthly fee) with a smaller secondary line for billing details (e.g., “Billed monthly, cancel anytime”), while the right 4–5 columns can show a compact highlight list (e.g., “No sign‑up fee”, “Includes family access”), aligning with the mockup’s promotional card feel. Below the primary price, a prominent button or call‑to‑action area visually connects to the purchase form located directly beneath, encouraging the user to proceed. Vertical padding of 24px and clear separation from the next panel make this area feel important but still clean and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the main content column; participates in the normal scroll flow and stays visually attached to the purchase form beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms panel sits toward the bottom of the main content column, directly below the hours panel, with about 32–40px of vertical spacing above it. It spans the same centered width as the other detail sections. The overall density is intentionally low for readability: a clear heading (“Terms & Conditions”) followed by multi‑paragraph text or concise bullet points describing contractual details. The line height is generous and paragraphs are broken into readable chunks, often constrained to a slightly narrower measure than the full card width to prevent long line lengths on wide screens. Internal padding of 24px gives the legal text breathing room. Visually, the panel is subdued: fewer colors, perhaps just a subtle border or a faint divider at the top, signaling importance without competing with the more promotional sections above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block at the lower end of the vertical stack; scrolls normally and defines the end of the detailed informational content before navigation controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchase form is positioned immediately beneath the pricing panel, with 24–32px of vertical separation, inside the same centered content column. On desktop, it spans the full width of the column, appearing as a substantial card with 24px internal padding and clearly grouped fields (e.g., member information, payment details). The form’s internal layout uses the 12‑column grid to create two‑column rows for shorter fields (e.g., first/last name) and full‑width rows for longer content (e.g., address), but the overall card remains visually simple and approachable. The primary submit button is aligned either centered or right within the form’s final row, with adequate spacing (~16–24px) above and below. The form is placed high enough on the page that it is typically within the first screenful below the hero, aligning with the mockup’s emphasis on early, clear calls to action. On smaller screens, all fields stack into a single column with increased vertical spacing between field groups for tap comfort.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block‑level form in the main content column. When submission is successful, it is replaced or visually transitioned out in favor of the purchase confirmation panel, under control of the state controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not render a visible UI element. Conceptually, it is associated with the area occupied by the pricing panel, purchase form, and purchase confirmation, managing which of those states is currently visible. It occupies no explicit space in the layout: when the user is in the pre‑purchase state, the pricing and form panels occupy the primary action area; once a purchase is successful, this same area is taken over by the confirmation panel. The controller ensures that transitions between these states occur in place, preserving the user’s spatial context within the centered content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual; no spatial footprint. Controls visibility and state of other components within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel appears in the same vertical position and width as the purchase form, effectively replacing it once a purchase is completed. It sits below the pricing panel, aligned to the center‑constrained content column, and matches the form card’s width and approximate padding (24px), creating a consistent footprint. The content focuses on a large, friendly confirmation message at the top, possibly with a subtle icon, followed by concise details: membership name, start date, and a summary of next steps. Secondary actions (e.g., “Print receipt”, “View all memberships”) are grouped at the bottom, right‑aligned or centered. Vertical spacing above and below (24–32px) and a clean layout make this feel like a clear end‑state while keeping the visual tone welcoming and celebratory in line with the family‑friendly design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying the same spatial slot as the purchase form; appears under control of the state controller and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls sit at the very bottom of the membership detail content stack, directly beneath the terms panel with about 32–40px of vertical breathing room. They align to the same centered content column but occupy a shallower horizontal strip, roughly the full width of the column but only 48–64px tall. Within this strip, one or two buttons or links (e.g., “Back to Memberships”, “View Other Plans”) are right‑aligned on desktop, echoing the mockup’s horizontal navigation logic, while a simpler centered layout may appear on mobile. The controls are visually lighter than primary CTAs—smaller, less saturated buttons or link‑style treatments—yet still clearly clickable, helping users exit or move on once they’ve reviewed or purchased the membership.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control strip at the bottom of the main content column; scrolls with the page and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notice appears within the centered content column just below the hero membership overview and above the pricing panel, occupying the full width of the column. It takes the form of a horizontal alert bar or card with a slightly higher visual intensity (e.g., a warm or red‑tinted background) but remains shallow in height—typically 56–80px depending on message length. Vertical spacing is tight enough to keep it visually connected to the content it affects: around 16–24px below the hero and 16–24px above the pricing panel. The message text is left‑aligned with a clear icon on the left for quick recognition, while the right side may include a simple action (e.g., “Try again” link). Its placement ensures the user sees it immediately after scanning the hero, but before interacting with the pricing or purchase form elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow; appears and disappears between the hero overview and pricing panel without affecting the layout of other components beyond pushing them up or down.

name: Parties & Rentals
title: Parties & Rentals
description: Search and compare party and rental packages by date, party size, amenities, and price, then open a package detail page to request a reservation.

core_features: Load and display available party and rental categories using getPartyCategories, allowing users to switch between categories such as birthday parties and room rentals.
Initialize filters (date, party size, amenities, maximum total price, sort order) using getPartyFilterOptions and any incoming URL parameters, and expose them in a single filter form.
Run package searches via searchPartyPackages based on the current category, selected date, party size, amenity selections, maximum total price, and sort order.
Display a list of party package cards using the PartyPackage data returned by searchPartyPackages, including computedTotalPrice and isAvailableOnSelectedDate flags.
Allow users to open a detailed package page (party_detail.html) by clicking a package card, passing the selectedPartyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren via URL parameters.
Keep the current search state (category, date, party size, amenity filters, max price, sort order) encoded in the page URL so searches can be bookmarked, shared, and restored on reload.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arriving with preselected birthday party search from the home page:
1. User clicks a 'Birthday Parties' tile or similar link on index.html that navigates to rentals.html with URL parameters such as category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, and sortBy.
2. On page load, the page-init controller parses these URL parameters and pre-populates the category selector, date field, party size field, amenity checkboxes, price filter, and sort order.
3. The controller calls getPartyCategories and getPartyFilterOptions. Once filters are configured, it triggers searchPartyPackages with the current state.
4. The results list displays matching party packages with prices computed for the given partySizeChildren and shows whether each is available on the selectedDate.
5. The user reviews the list, optionally adjusts filters (e.g., changes party size or price cap) and the results update using new calls to searchPartyPackages.
6. When the user finds a suitable package, they click its card and are navigated to party_detail.html with partyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren in the URL.

Workflow 2 – Browsing parties from the site header with no pre-applied filters:
1. User clicks the 'Parties & Rentals' link in the header or footer, arriving at rentals.html without search-related URL parameters.
2. On load, the page-init controller calls getPartyCategories and getPartyFilterOptions, selects a default category (for example, the first returned category), and applies any default guidance such as suggested price ranges.
3. The filter form starts with empty or default values: no date, no party size, amenity filters off, and a default sort option from getPartyFilterOptions.sortOptions.
4. The user chooses a category, picks a date, enters an estimated number of children, optionally toggles amenities and sets a maximum price.
5. Submitting or changing the filters updates the URL parameters and triggers searchPartyPackages. The results list shows matching packages.
6. The user can refine results by changing filters or the sort order; each change updates both the URL and the displayed results.

Workflow 3 – Returning to a saved search or using the browser back button:
1. The user previously used rentals.html and the URL contains full search state (e.g., category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, sortBy).
2. The user bookmarks this URL or uses the browser back button to return to it.
3. On load or on a browser navigation event, the url-state-sync component re-reads current URL parameters and updates the filter form and category selector to match.
4. The page-init controller or navigation handler calls searchPartyPackages with the restored state, recreating the same results list as before.
5. The user continues adjusting filters normally; each change overwrites the URL parameters so the new state can again be bookmarked or navigated back to.

Workflow 4 – Handling no matching results:
1. The user sets filters that are too restrictive (for example, a low maxTotalPrice combined with multiple required amenities and a specific date).
2. The filter form submits these criteria and searchPartyPackages returns an empty results array.
3. The results list component displays a generic 'no results' message and may highlight which filters are currently applied.
4. The user relaxes one or more filters (e.g., increases maxTotalPrice or clears an amenity requirement); URL parameters update accordingly.
5. A new searchPartyPackages call returns results which are then displayed in the results list.
interactions:
Changing category:
- Interaction: User clicks or taps a category tile or option in the category selector.
- Effect: The category selector updates the internal selected category state, writes ?category=... to the URL, and triggers a new searchPartyPackages call with the updated category while retaining other filters (date, partySizeChildren, amenities, maxTotalPrice, sortBy).

Selecting or changing date:
- Interaction: User selects a date from a date picker in the filter form.
- Effect: The filter form updates its selectedDate value, validates that the chosen date aligns with guidance from getPartyFilterOptions.availableDaysOfWeek (e.g., disabling dates not in that list), writes selectedDate=YYYY-MM-DD to the URL, and triggers searchPartyPackages with the new date so isAvailableOnSelectedDate is meaningful for the results.

Entering party size:
- Interaction: User inputs the expected number of children in a numeric field or chooses from quick range options derived from getPartyFilterOptions.partySizeRanges.
- Effect: The partySizeChildren field updates; if the user picks a range, the component may set the numeric field to a specific value within that range according to product rules. The URL is updated with partySizeChildren=<number>. searchPartyPackages is called so computedTotalPrice in each result reflects the new party size.

Toggling amenity filters:
- Interaction: User checks or unchecks amenity checkboxes derived from getPartyFilterOptions.amenities.
- Effect: For each amenity with id 'private_party_room', the checkbox state maps to includesPrivatePartyRoom=true/false in the URL. For the amenity with id 'pizza_included', the checkbox maps to includesPizza=true/false. The filter form then calls searchPartyPackages so results are filtered to matching packages.

Setting maximum total price:
- Interaction: User adjusts a numeric field or slider for maximum total price.
- Effect: The filter form updates maxTotalPrice, writes it to the URL, and triggers searchPartyPackages. The backend uses this limit when returning results; only packages whose computedTotalPrice is within this maximum should appear.

Changing sort order:
- Interaction: User selects an option from the sort dropdown populated from getPartyFilterOptions.sortOptions.
- Effect: The selected sortBy value is stored in state, written to the URL as sortBy=<value>, and a new searchPartyPackages call is made. The returned results are displayed in the new order.

Submitting the filter form:
- Interaction: User clicks an 'Apply filters' or similar button, or the form may auto-apply on change depending on product decisions.
- Effect: The current filter values (category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, sortBy) are validated, written to the URL, and sent in a searchPartyPackages call. The results list is then updated.

Opening package detail:
- Interaction: User clicks anywhere on a party package card (or a dedicated 'View details' button within it).
- Effect: The card’s click handler reads the underlying PartyPackage.id from the bound search result, reads the current selectedDate and partySizeChildren from state, and navigates the browser to party_detail.html with URL parameters partyPackageId=<id>&selectedDate=<current-selectedDate>&partySizeChildren=<current-partySizeChildren>.

Responding to browser navigation (back/forward):
- Interaction: User presses the browser back or forward button.
- Effect: The url-state-sync component listens for URL change events (e.g., popstate), re-parses the query parameters, updates the category selector and filter form state, and triggers searchPartyPackages with this restored state so the results list matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses only URL query parameters and in-memory component state (no localStorage or other persistent client storage). On initial load, the page-init controller parses the query string for: category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, and sortBy. Any present parameters initialize the corresponding component states.

If category is missing, the controller calls getPartyCategories and, once categories are returned, selects a default category (e.g., the first active category) and sets category in the URL. If sortBy is missing, the controller uses getPartyFilterOptions.sortOptions to determine a default sort value and writes it to the URL.

Filters and sort parameters are kept in sync with the URL at all times. Whenever the user changes a filter or sort option:
- The component updates its internal state.
- The url-state-sync component updates or removes the corresponding URL parameter (e.g., removing maxTotalPrice if the user clears it, or setting includesPrivatePartyRoom=true if the amenity checkbox is checked).
- The page-init controller (or an equivalent search controller) calls searchPartyPackages with the latest values read from state (which mirrors the URL).

Values are encoded in the URL as follows: category is a string; selectedDate is an ISO YYYY-MM-DD string; partySizeChildren and maxTotalPrice are numeric values serialized as strings and parsed to numbers before calling searchPartyPackages; includesPrivatePartyRoom and includesPizza are boolean values represented as strings (e.g., 'true'/'false') and converted to booleans for the API call; sortBy is a string matching one of the values from getPartyFilterOptions.sortOptions.

On any browser navigation event that changes the query string, the url-state-sync component re-reads the parameters, updates the category selector and filter form to match, and triggers a fresh searchPartyPackages call so the rendered results always reflect the URL state. This guarantees that sharing or bookmarking a URL reproduces the same filters, sort order, and therefore the same result set at the time of loading.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parsing, and triggering of searches. On page load, it calls getPartyCategories and getPartyFilterOptions, derives any default values, initializes filter state from URL parameters, and then invokes searchPartyPackages with the current state whenever a search is needed.
data_binding: getPartyFilterOptions result object (amenities, partySizeRanges, priceHints, availableDaysOfWeek, sortOptions)
URL parameters: category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, sortBy

components.functionality: Shows the list of party and rental categories returned by getPartyCategories and lets the user choose which category of packages to search. It updates the category URL parameter and triggers new searches when the category changes.
data_binding: getPartyCategories result array (each item: category, name, description)
Current selected category state (used when calling searchPartyPackages.category)

components.functionality: Central filter form that manages selected date, party size, amenity filters, and maximum total price. It also configures validation (e.g., allowable dates and party size ranges) using getPartyFilterOptions. Any change updates URL parameters and triggers searchPartyPackages via the controller.
data_binding: getPartyFilterOptions.amenities (to render amenity toggles and map specific IDs such as 'private_party_room' to includesPrivatePartyRoom and 'pizza_included' to includesPizza)
getPartyFilterOptions.partySizeRanges (to configure quick-select options and min/max constraints on partySizeChildren)
getPartyFilterOptions.priceHints (to configure default range and input hints for maxTotalPrice)
getPartyFilterOptions.availableDaysOfWeek (to constrain or highlight selectable dates in the date picker)
URL parameters: selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice
Internal filter state: selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice

components.functionality: Provides a dropdown or equivalent control for choosing how results are sorted (e.g., by price ascending or descending) using sortOptions from getPartyFilterOptions. When the user changes the sort option, it updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a new search.
data_binding: Current sortBy state (used when calling searchPartyPackages.sortBy)

components.functionality: Displays the list of search results returned from searchPartyPackages. For each result, it renders a party-package-card. If the result array is empty, it shows an appropriate generic message via the empty-state-notice component.
data_binding: Current selectedDate and partySizeChildren state (for contextual labeling in list items)

components.functionality: Represents a single party or rental package within the results list. Displays key information from the PartyPackage and search result (name, short description if present, computedTotalPrice, availability for the selected date, and amenity indicators). Clicking the card navigates to party_detail.html with the appropriate URL parameters.
data_binding: PartyPackage fields from searchPartyPackages.results[].partyPackage (id, name, description, category, base_price, included_children_count, min_children, max_children, includes_private_party_room, includes_pizza, status, available_days_of_week)
searchPartyPackages.results[].computedTotalPrice (display as total price for the current partySizeChildren)
Current selectedDate state (to pass as outgoing URL parameter)
Current partySizeChildren state (to pass as outgoing URL parameter)

components.functionality: Displays contextual feedback when searchPartyPackages returns no results. It informs the user that no packages match the current filters and prompts them to adjust filters such as date, amenities, or maximum price.
data_binding: Current filter state: category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, sortBy

components.functionality: Synchronizes component state with URL query parameters. When filters or sort options change, it updates the URL; when the URL changes (including via browser navigation), it updates the filter and category components and triggers searches. It ensures the URL is always the single source of truth for search state.
data_binding: URL parameters: category, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, includesPrivatePartyRoom, includesPizza, maxTotalPrice, sortBy

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same top-down, banded structure as the mockup: global utility bar and header at the very top (out of scope), then a full-width hero band for Parties & Rentals, followed by a wide, center-constrained introductory text block. Directly beneath the intro, a dedicated filter/search band holds the category-selector and party-filter-form as the functional equivalent of the hero card strip (a visually distinct, colorful action band). Below that, the main content area is a centered results column where the sort-control sits at the top right and the party-results-list flows vertically down the page. The empty-state-notice occupies the same results column when needed. Non-visual controllers (page-init-controller and url-state-sync) sit logically at the top of this page section but do not alter the visual flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same top-down, banded structure as the mockup: global utility bar and header at the very top (out of scope), then a full-width hero band for Parties & Rentals, followed by a wide, center-constrained introductory text block. Directly beneath the intro, a dedicated filter/search band holds the category-selector and party-filter-form as the functional equivalent of the hero card strip (a visually distinct, colorful action band). Below that, the main content area is a centered results column where the sort-control sits at the top right and the party-results-list flows vertically down the page. The empty-state-notice occupies the same results column when needed. Non-visual controllers (page-init-controller and url-state-sync) sit logically at the top of this page section but do not alter the visual flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and hero consume the upper portion of the viewport, with the hero and intro text accounting for roughly the top half of the first screen. The filter/search band (category-selector + party-filter-form) occupies a relatively shallow but visually strong band immediately below the intro, about 15–20% of the initial viewport height, with generous padding. The remaining vertical space is given to the results region: sort-control plus party-results-list, which extends for the remainder of the scroll. Within this results region, each party-package-card occupies a full-width slot of the centered content column with 24–32px vertical spacing between cards, keeping density comfortable. Section margins between hero/intro, intro/filter, and filter/results follow the 32–48px pattern to preserve breathing room.
layout_strategies.content_density: The introductory band and filter/search band are low to medium density: large type, plenty of white space, and clear separation between controls. The hero and the party-package-card content are more visually dense with imagery and text (poster-like for party themes), but they are kept inside their own bands or cards with generous margins so the overall page does not feel cluttered. The results list is denser than the intro but uses consistent vertical spacing and card padding (16–24px internal) to keep it readable and scannable. Empty states are intentionally sparse, with centered content and extra whitespace to emphasize clarity.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows the same pattern as the mockup: starting at the top-left logo and nav (global), then down to the colorful page hero for Parties & Rentals. From there it drops to the large intro text explaining how to book. The eye is then drawn to the filter/search band, first scanning the category-selector row of pills left-to-right, then moving down into the larger party-filter-form. From the filter, the gaze moves slightly down and right to the sort-control, then follows a vertical scroll through the stacked party-package-card elements in the party-results-list. If there are no results, the empty-state-notice becomes the focal point in the same results area. The non-visual controllers work in the background and do not affect visible flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s bright header and Parties & Rentals hero, the page settles into a calm, centered content column. A large, welcoming intro paragraph spans about two-thirds of the content width, centered with wide margins on both sides and generous line height, echoing the editorial feel from the mockup. Immediately underneath, a distinct filter/search band stretches across roughly 80% of the page width, centered and backed by a soft, playful color to signal interactivity. At the top of this band, a slim row of category pills runs left to right, behaving like a utility strip for quickly switching between party types. Directly beneath, a larger, card-like search form groups key inputs (date, party size, amenities, price range) into a single, neatly padded block that feels like the main tool of the page. Below this band, the layout transitions back to a clean white background where a single results column is centered. In the top-right of this column, a small sort dropdown aligns with the first row of content. Under it, party cards stack vertically one per row; each card is as wide as the content column and mixes bold, colorful imagery with concise details, separated by comfortable vertical gaps so each option reads like its own small poster. When no options match, the results area instead shows a friendly empty state centered in the column, surrounded by extra white space. Throughout, the layout maintains a bright, family-oriented look while keeping the interactive UI disciplined and orderly, guiding the user smoothly from understanding the offer into tailoring and browsing party options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual component is conceptually placed at the very top of the Parties & Rentals content area, just below the page hero and introductory text section. It occupies no visible space and does not affect the grid; instead, it initializes data and state before the user interacts with filters or sees results. In the document flow, it appears above the filter/search band (category-selector and party-filter-form), but users never see it as a rendered element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic component with no explicit rendered footprint; it participates only in page initialization and scrolls logically with the page structure if inspected, but does not alter layout or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-selector sits at the top of the filter/search band, directly beneath the introductory text block and spanning approximately 70–80% of the page width, centered within the main content container. It appears as a shallow horizontal strip of pill-shaped buttons (e.g., Birthday Parties, Group Rentals, Lock-ins), aligned left within this band. Vertically, it uses about 16–24px of padding above and below the pills, making the strip visually light and utility-like, similar in spirit to the narrow utility bar in the mockup. On desktop, the pill row follows the 12-column grid, occupying about 8–10 columns centered, leaving equal white space on either side. There is about 16–24px of vertical space below this strip before the party-filter-form begins, clearly separating quick category toggles from the more detailed search form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page as part of the filter/search band. It does not stick or float; its position remains fixed relative to the intro content above and the party-filter-form below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The party-filter-form is the main focal element of the filter/search band and sits directly below the category-selector, centered within the same width-constrained container. It takes the form of a card or panel spanning roughly 8 out of 12 grid columns on desktop (about 70% of the viewport width), with equal left and right margins to maintain a clean, framed feel. The panel has generous internal padding (24–32px) on all sides, giving the inputs room to breathe and matching the spacious, family-friendly look. The form fields (date picker, party size, amenities, price slider or dropdown, and a prominent search button) are arranged in a single horizontal row where space allows (desktop), each aligned to columns within the 12-column grid and separated by 16px gaps. On narrower viewports this can stack into two rows while remaining centered. The top edge of the form sits about 16–24px below the category-selector; its bottom edge sits about 32px above the start of the results/sort area, visually tying the filter band together as a single, prominent section between the intro text and the results. The form’s background may be subtly tinted to echo the hero’s energy and differentiate it from the white results area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard in-flow section: it scrolls with the page and stays in place relative to content above and below. No fixed or sticky behavior by default, ensuring the top-of-page band feels similar to the hero structure outlined in the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control is positioned at the top of the results region, directly beneath the filter/search band and aligned to the right edge of the centered content column. Horizontally, it occupies approximately 2–3 grid columns (about 20–25% of the content width) and sits on the same baseline where a "X results" label or section title might appear to its left if present, though the label is not required. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the party-filter-form and the sort-control, allowing the visual rhythm of separate bands: filter, then results. Below the sort-control, the first party-package-card begins after another 16–24px of spacing. This control itself is compact—likely a dropdown or segmented toggle—keeping density low and not competing visually with the larger cards below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed within the normal document flow at the top of the results list; it scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down through many results. No sticky behavior by default, preserving the simple linear scroll pattern of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The party-results-list forms the main vertical content column beneath the sort-control. It is centered on the page, constrained to roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–80% of the viewport width), mirroring the width of the intro text and filter band for a coherent column. Within this column, party-package-card components are stacked vertically, one per row. Each card spans almost the full width of the column (minus consistent horizontal padding or gutters of 16–24px inside the container) and is separated from the next card by 24–32px of vertical space, echoing the low-to-medium density approach. The list can extend well beyond the initial viewport height, forming the primary scrollable content of the page. When present, the list begins just below the sort-control (with 16–24px gap), and the bottom of the list flows directly into any further page content or footer with a generous margin of 48–64px to avoid a cramped ending.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertically scrolling list within the main content column; cards flow in a linear sequence. The list has no fixed or sticky elements itself and relies on the page’s default scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each party-package-card is a visually rich, poster-like card within the party-results-list. It occupies the full width of the results column (approximately 8–10 grid columns, centered on the page) and has rounded corners with a light shadow or border to distinguish it from the white background. Internally, the card uses 16–24px padding on all sides. The upper portion of the card (roughly the top 40–50% of its height) is dominated by colorful imagery or a graphic banner representing the party theme—this aligns with the mockup’s vibrant promotional cards. Below the image, the lower portion is a structured information area: title and key highlights aligned to the left, with price and primary action button aligned to the right or at the bottom-right, depending on layout. Content is arranged in 2–3 vertical groupings with clear 8–16px spacing between text blocks (title, details, badges). The overall card height is moderate—around 220–320px depending on content—maintaining a dense but controlled feel. Between cards, a consistent 24–32px vertical gap preserves visual breathing room and makes each option feel distinct and selectable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully in-flow within the results list; each card scrolls with the page. Cards do not overlap or float and maintain consistent width and spacing regardless of list length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-notice occupies the same spatial region as the party-results-list when no matching packages are found. It is centered within the main content column beneath the sort-control and below the filter band. Horizontally, it matches the width of the results column (about 8–10 grid columns centered), but its content is concentrated in the middle, leaving generous white space on both sides. Vertically, it sits 24–32px below the sort-control and uses extra top and bottom padding (32–48px) to create a sense of calm and clarity. The notice typically includes an icon or illustration at the top center, a clear heading, and a short explanatory paragraph, all center-aligned, with 8–16px spacing between elements. Optional actions (such as a "Clear filters" button) appear below, also centered. This arrangement ensures that when there are no results, the user’s attention is immediately drawn to this message in the same location where they would expect the cards, without introducing a new band or disrupting the overall layout rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in place of the results list and scrolls normally with the page. It does not fix or float; it simply replaces the list content inside the same column, preserving the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly alongside the filter and results logic. Conceptually, it sits behind the scenes of the filter/search band and the results region, synchronizing the current filter, sort, and selection state with the URL. It has no visual representation, occupies no explicit space in the layout, and does not affect the grid or spacing between visible components. Its presence ensures that the page can be reloaded or shared while preserving the currently selected category, filter choices, and sort order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic component with no direct spatial behavior; it moves with the document structure but introduces no rendered footprint or fixed positioning.

name: Party Package Detail
title: Party Package Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected party package, confirms date and party size, shows computed total price, and lets users submit a reservation request and view reservation summaries.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a selected party package using getPartyPackageDetail, including inclusions, rules, and availability for the chosen date and party size.
Confirm and summarize the selected date and party size, and display the computed total price returned by getPartyPackageDetail.
Allow users to adjust the party date and party size, updating the URL parameters and re-requesting getPartyPackageDetail to refresh price and availability.
Provide a reservation request form to collect organizer details and preferred start time, and submit the request via createPartyReservation.
After a successful reservation request, show a confirmation message and key reservation details based on the createPartyReservation response and/or getPartyReservationSummary.
Support direct access to an existing reservation summary (e.g., from My Schedule) by loading reservation details via getPartyReservationSummary when a reservation ID is present.
Present follow-up information such as response expectations and payment or deposit instructions using data from interface messages and policy text.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View package details from Rentals
1. User navigates from rentals.html to party_detail.html with URL parameters partyPackageId and optionally selectedDate and partySizeChildren.
2. On page load, the controller reads partyPackageId (required) and optional selectedDate and partySizeChildren from the URL.
3. The page calls getPartyPackageDetail with partyPackageId, selectedDate (or a null/empty value if missing), and partySizeChildren (or a null/empty value if missing).
4. The package detail components display the package name, description, inclusions (e.g., room access, food options), rules and policies, and any availability message.
5. If selectedDate or partySizeChildren are missing, the date/size controls prompt the user to enter them; on change, the URL is updated and getPartyPackageDetail is called again to compute a current total price.
6. The price and availability panel shows the computedTotalPrice and availabilityMessage returned by getPartyPackageDetail, along with the selected date and party size.
7. The user reviews inclusions and rules, confirms the summary, and proceeds to fill out the reservation request form.

Workflow 2: Configure date and party size
1. The user views the current selectedDate and partySizeChildren displayed in the summary panel.
2. If the user changes the date (via a date input control), the date component rewrites the URL parameter selectedDate and triggers a new getPartyPackageDetail call with updated parameters.
3. If the user adjusts partySizeChildren (via a numeric input control), the component updates the URL parameter partySizeChildren and re-calls getPartyPackageDetail.
4. The price and availability panel is refreshed with updated computedTotalPrice and availabilityMessage.
5. The reservation request form always uses the current URL state (partyPackageId, selectedDate, partySizeChildren) when submitting createPartyReservation.

Workflow 3: Submit a new reservation request
1. With date and party size confirmed, the user enters organizerName, organizerPhone, organizerEmail, and preferredStartTime in the reservation form.
2. On form submit, client-side validation ensures required fields are present and that selectedDate and partySizeChildren are available in URL state.
3. The form component calls createPartyReservation with partyPackageId, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, organizerName, organizerPhone, organizerEmail, and preferredStartTime.
4. While awaiting the response, the form shows a submitting state and disables inputs to prevent duplicate submissions.
5. If createPartyReservation returns success = false, the form displays the message from the API and keeps the form editable so the user can correct or retry.
6. If createPartyReservation returns success = true, the page stores the returned reservation.id in memory and optionally updates the URL with a partyReservationId parameter, then shows the reservation confirmation panel.
7. The confirmation panel uses the reservation object from createPartyReservation (and optionally calls getPartyReservationSummary with reservation.id for a complete summary) to display reservation details, including total_price, selected_date, and party_size_children.
8. The follow-up section displays the message from createPartyReservation (such as response time or deposit instructions) along with any relevant rulesAndPolicies content already loaded.

Workflow 4: View an existing reservation from My Schedule
1. The user navigates from my_schedule.html to party_detail.html using a link that includes a partyReservationId URL parameter.
2. On page load, the controller detects partyReservationId in the URL.
3. The page calls getPartyReservationSummary with partyReservationId.
4. Once getPartyReservationSummary returns, the page displays the reservation summary (date, time, party size, total price, organizer contact details) and related party package information.
5. The package detail section is populated from the partyPackage returned by getPartyReservationSummary, and the reservation summary panel is populated from reservation.
6. In this mode, the reservation request form can be hidden or shown in a read-only or disabled state, depending on design rules, to indicate that the reservation has already been requested or confirmed.
7. The user can click a navigation control to go back to my_schedule.html to review all scheduled items.

Workflow 5: Post‑reservation navigation
1. After a successful reservation submission, the reservation confirmation panel offers a 'View in My Schedule' action.
2. When the user activates this action, the page navigates to my_schedule.html, optionally including a URL parameter indicating the reservation ID to focus on.
3. At any time, the user can select 'Back to Party Packages', which navigates to rentals.html, optionally preserving any referral parameters present in the current URL (such as selectedDate and partySizeChildren) for consistent list filtering.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial load and mode detection
- Trigger: Page load or URL change.
- Behavior: The controller component reads URL parameters partyPackageId, selectedDate, partySizeChildren, and optionally partyReservationId.
- If partyReservationId is present: call getPartyReservationSummary and show reservation summary mode (existing reservation view); optionally call getPartyPackageDetail using the partyPackage.id and derived date/size if needed.
- If partyReservationId is absent and partyPackageId is present: call getPartyPackageDetail to show package details and allow new reservation requests.
- If required parameters are missing (e.g., no partyPackageId), show an error state instructing the user to return to rentals.html.

Interaction: Change selected date
- Trigger: User changes the date in the date input control.
- Behavior: The date/size component updates the selectedDate URL parameter in the browser (e.g., using query string update) and calls getPartyPackageDetail with the new selectedDate and current partySizeChildren.
- Result: Package detail and price/availability panels update with new computedTotalPrice and availabilityMessage for the selected date.

Interaction: Change party size
- Trigger: User changes partySizeChildren via a numeric input or stepper control.
- Behavior: The component validates that the value is a number, updates the partySizeChildren URL parameter, and calls getPartyPackageDetail with the new partySizeChildren and current selectedDate.
- Result: The total price and any capacity-related messages (e.g., limits from the package model) update based on the API's computedTotalPrice and availabilityMessage.

Interaction: Submit reservation request
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Submit Reservation Request' button in the reservation form.
- Behavior: The form gathers organizerName, organizerPhone, organizerEmail, preferredStartTime from its inputs and reads partyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren from the URL state.
- Validation: Ensure organizerName and selectedDate and partySizeChildren are present; optionally validate email and phone formats.
- API call: Call createPartyReservation with all required parameters.
- Success path: If success = true, show confirmation information using reservation and message, update URL with partyReservationId if desired, and optionally disable or hide the reservation form to prevent duplicate requests.
- Failure path: If success = false, show the message from the API, keep the form active, and allow resubmission after corrections.

Interaction: View reservation confirmation
- Trigger: Successful response from createPartyReservation or successful response from getPartyReservationSummary.
- Behavior: The confirmation component displays reservation details from reservation and related package details from partyPackage (either from getPartyPackageDetail or getPartyReservationSummary).
- Follow-up info: Display the message field from createPartyReservation or any policy/response-time text available from rulesAndPolicies or other response fields, emphasizing what will happen next and any payment/deposit requirements.

Interaction: View in My Schedule navigation
- Trigger: User clicks 'View in My Schedule' in the confirmation panel.
- Behavior: Navigate to my_schedule.html, optionally including a URL parameter containing the reservation.id so that My Schedule can highlight the relevant item.

Interaction: Back to Party Packages navigation
- Trigger: User clicks 'Back to Party Packages'.
- Behavior: Navigate to rentals.html. If selectedDate and partySizeChildren are present in the current URL, they can be passed through to rentals.html as query parameters so the listing page can restore previous filters or selections.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and interface responses.

1) URL Parameters
- partyPackageId (required for new reservation view): Identifies the PartyPackage to display. When present without partyReservationId, the page treats the state as 'package detail and request' mode.
- selectedDate (optional on initial entry, recommended): Represents the requested party date in ISO YYYY-MM-DD format. When changed by the user, the URL is updated and getPartyPackageDetail is re-invoked. The reservation form always uses the current selectedDate from the URL when submitting.
- partySizeChildren (optional on initial entry, recommended): Represents the number of children the user plans to host. When changed, the URL is updated and getPartyPackageDetail is re-invoked to recompute total price. The reservation form uses this value when calling createPartyReservation.
- partyReservationId (optional, used for existing reservations): When present, the page loads in 'reservation summary' mode and calls getPartyReservationSummary. In this mode, partyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren can be derived from the reservation data if not already present in the URL.

2) Mode Determination
- If partyReservationId is present: The page prioritizes reservation summary mode. It calls getPartyReservationSummary first. If partyPackageId is not present in the URL, it can be inferred from reservation.party_package_id for internal use (e.g., linking back to details) but does not need to be written back to the URL unless desired.
- If partyReservationId is not present and partyPackageId is present: The page operates in package detail and reservation request mode and calls getPartyPackageDetail.
- If neither partyPackageId nor partyReservationId is present: The page shows an error state suggesting navigation back to rentals.html.

3) Data Refresh Rules
- Any change to selectedDate or partySizeChildren made through user controls updates the URL and triggers a fresh getPartyPackageDetail call. This ensures computedTotalPrice, availabilityMessage, and any derived UI text stay in sync with the current parameters.
- The reservation form does not maintain its own independent copies of partyPackageId, selectedDate, or partySizeChildren; instead, it reads them from the URL immediately before calling createPartyReservation.

4) Post-Reservation URL Updates
- On successful createPartyReservation, the page can append or replace the query string with a partyReservationId corresponding to reservation.id while preserving partyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren where possible. This allows the user to reload the page or return later to the same reservation summary view using the same URL.
- The confirmation display uses reservation data directly and, if needed, can call getPartyReservationSummary based on the newly added partyReservationId for a canonical summary.

5) No Local Storage
- All persistent state relevant to this page (which package, which date, which party size, which reservation) is encoded in URL parameters. No localStorage or similar browser storage mechanisms are used to determine behavior or content.

components.functionality: Initializes the page, reads URL parameters, determines whether to show package detail mode or reservation summary mode, orchestrates API calls, and distributes data to child components.
Displays the party package name and category, and in reservation summary mode may also show the reservation date and status for quick reference.
Shows what is included in the selected party package, such as access to facilities and food options, based on fields from PartyPackage and any descriptive text returned by getPartyPackageDetail.

Displays rules, policies, and expectations associated with the party package, including any follow-up information about response time or payment if provided in rulesAndPolicies or similar interface fields.
Allows the user to view and adjust selectedDate and partySizeChildren, updates the URL parameters to reflect changes, and triggers getPartyPackageDetail to refresh pricing and availability.
Displays the computed total price and availability message returned by getPartyPackageDetail for the current partyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren.

Collects organizerName, organizerPhone, organizerEmail, and preferredStartTime, validates required inputs, and calls createPartyReservation using URL parameters partyPackageId, selectedDate, and partySizeChildren.

Shows a confirmation summary for a reservation request, using the PartyReservation returned from createPartyReservation or getPartyReservationSummary, and displays any message from the API along with key details like total price, date, and party size.
Displays follow-up instructions such as expected response time and payment or deposit steps, derived from API messages (e.g., createPartyReservation.message) and/or rulesAndPolicies content.
Provides navigation actions such as returning to rentals.html and opening my_schedule.html for the current reservation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence of full‑width bands, mirroring the mockup: global header (outside this scope), then a visually strong hero‑style band for the package detail header, followed by a center‑constrained content column. Within this column, informational panels (inclusions, rules) appear first, then a more transactional band where configuration (date/size) and pricing sit side‑by‑side, followed by the reservation form, confirmation, and closing follow‑up info. The navigation actions bar sits as a persistent, bottom‑anchored utility, similar in hierarchy to the floating chat from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence of full‑width bands, mirroring the mockup: global header (outside this scope), then a visually strong hero‑style band for the package detail header, followed by a center‑constrained content column. Within this column, informational panels (inclusions, rules) appear first, then a more transactional band where configuration (date/size) and pricing sit side‑by‑side, followed by the reservation form, confirmation, and closing follow‑up info. The navigation actions bar sits as a persistent, bottom‑anchored utility, similar in hierarchy to the floating chat from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height is assumed for the global header (outside this page), about 30–40% for the package detail header hero band, and the remaining above‑the‑fold space is split between the opening info panels and the top of the configuration band. Each major band is full width but content is constrained to a centered max‑width container (~70–80% of viewport width on large screens). Vertical spacing between bands follows the 32–48px rhythm, with generous internal padding (16–24px) for card‑like panels. The bottom navigation actions bar reserves a shallow (56–64px) strip fixed at the bottom edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps low‑to‑medium density overall: the hero‑style package header is bold and graphic, while all text sections are given wide margins and relaxed line height. The date/size selector, price summary, and reservation form are the densest areas but are structured into clear, card‑like blocks with breathing room around groups of fields. Info panels (inclusions, rules, follow‑up) read more like editorial content with bullets and short paragraphs, maintaining legibility and a family‑friendly, inviting tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye lands first on the package detail header at the top, scanning left‑to‑right across the hero band (title, key facts, and imagery). From there the gaze drops into the centered content column: first across the inclusions and rules (which sit in a left‑right relationship on desktop), then down into the configuration band where attention shifts left‑to‑right between the date/size selector and the price/availability summary. The flow then continues vertically to the reservation request form and, after submission, to the confirmation panel and follow‑up info. Throughout, a subtle bottom navigation actions bar remains in peripheral view, ready as a secondary action strip without competing with the main content.

layout.overall_description: Within the site’s standard header shell, the Party Package Detail page opens with a bright, full‑width hero band that feels like a promotional poster for the selected party. This band is visually rich but structurally clean: on desktop, the left two‑thirds show the package name in large, friendly typography, a short description, and quick‑glance specs (age range, capacity, duration, base price), while the right third features a colorful illustration or photograph of kids enjoying the aquatic center. Directly beneath this hero, the layout shifts into a calm, centered content column with generous left and right margins. The first band in this column is a pair of card‑like info panels—package inclusions and rules/policies—laid out side‑by‑side on larger screens and stacked on smaller ones, inviting a quick scan of what’s included and what to expect. Below that, a slightly tinted configuration band presents the interactive core: on the left, a spacious date and party size selector; on the right, a compact but prominent price and availability summary card that updates based on selections. Scrolling down, a full‑width (within the central column) reservation request form continues the editorial feel with clear grouping and comfortable spacing, followed by a highlighted confirmation panel that appears after submission. A final follow‑up info panel closes the page with next steps and contact details, echoing the large, welcoming text treatment from the mockup’s intro section. All the while, a slim navigation actions bar rests fixed along the bottom edge of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s floating control pattern—present but unobtrusive—providing quick navigation or utility actions without disturbing the clean, vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The party-detail-controller acts as the full-page canvas and structural wrapper. It spans the entire browser width and height, sitting directly below the global site header and utility bar. Inside, it centers the main content in a max-width container (approximately 1080–1200px) with automatic side margins. All other components are arranged vertically within this controller in a linear, top-to-bottom stack of visually distinct bands, each separated by 32–48px vertical spacing. The controller establishes a white or very light neutral background and enforces consistent horizontal padding (16–24px on mobile, 32–48px on desktop) so that all panels and forms align on a shared vertical grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width wrapper that scrolls with the page; defines max-width and vertical stacking but has no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-detail-header occupies the first content band within the page, directly below the global header. It spans the full browser width with a bright, branded background color or gradient, giving it a hero-band presence. Within this band, content is constrained to the central max-width container. On desktop, the internal layout follows a 12-column grid: the left 7–8 columns are devoted to the package title, subtitle, and key details (age range, number of swimmers, duration, base price) arranged in stacked rows with generous vertical padding (24–32px). The right 4–5 columns host a large, colorful image or illustration, aligned to the right edge of the container, extending close to the band’s top and bottom to reinforce the energetic, poster-like feel. Vertical padding on the band itself is generous—approximately 40–64px top and bottom—so the header consumes about the upper third of the initial viewport on laptop screens. On tablet and mobile, the image collapses below the text, with both elements centered and margins tightened slightly while maintaining comfortable spacing. A subtle breadcrumb or back link can sit at the top-left of this band, above the main title, as a small, low-contrast line of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal scroll behavior; treated as a hero section that remains at the top of the scroll stack without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-inclusions-panel sits immediately below the package-detail-header, within the main centered content column. It forms the left half of a two-panel information band on desktop. The band begins about 32–48px below the hero, and the inclusions panel aligns to the left edge of the central container, occupying roughly 7 of 12 columns (about 60% of the available width). It is rendered as a card with a soft, very light tinted background and rounded corners, separated from the page background by a subtle shadow or border. Internal padding is 24px on all sides, giving the text room to breathe. The panel’s title appears at the top left, with a short introductory line followed by a bulleted list of inclusions (e.g., room access, lifeguard supervision, decorations), set with comfortable line height and 8–12px spacing between bullet items. On tablets, this card shrinks to about 100% width but remains visually grouped with the rules-and-policies-panel below or above it; on narrow mobile screens, it stacks above rules with full-width alignment and consistent side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main scrollable flow; on larger screens it participates in a side-by-side layout with the rules-and-policies-panel but has no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rules-and-policies-panel is positioned to the right of the package-inclusions-panel within the same information band.
