On desktop, it occupies approximately 5 of 12 columns (about 40% of the width) and aligns to the right edge of the central container, creating a balanced two-column layout. Its top aligns roughly with the top of the inclusions panel, and its height may be similar or slightly taller depending on text length. It uses the same card treatment—light tinted background, soft corners, and 24px internal padding—to visually connect it with the inclusions panel. The content is more text-dense (house rules, safety policies, cancellation terms), so headings and line spacing are carefully used to avoid clutter, with 16–24px spacing between sections. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, this panel drops below the inclusions panel and expands to full width, maintaining 16–24px vertical separation so the two panels still feel related but not crowded. A slightly more neutral or cautionary accent color (e.g., soft amber or teal border) can be used for its title to distinguish it semantically without increasing visual weight excessively.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with normal document scroll; participates in a responsive two-column/stacked pattern alongside the package-inclusions-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-and-size-selector forms the interactive starting point for booking and sits in a distinct configuration band below the info panels. This band begins 32–48px below the information band and spans the full width of the page, using a soft, tinted background (e.g., a very light aqua) to differentiate it from the white content above. Within the central max-width container, the date-and-size-selector card is positioned on the left, occupying roughly 7–8 of 12 columns (about 60–65% of the width). Its top aligns with the top of the price-and-availability-panel on the right, creating a cohesive left-right relationship. The card uses 24px internal padding, with form fields grouped into horizontal rows (e.g., date picker on a full-width row, then party size and time slot selection sharing a row). Labels are top-aligned, and there is 12–16px vertical spacing between field groups to keep the density comfortable. On smaller screens, this card becomes full width and appears above the price-and-availability-panel, preserving the logical flow of selecting options before reviewing price.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal scroll; in desktop layouts, it sits side-by-side with the price-and-availability-panel within a shared tinted background band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-and-availability-panel is anchored to the right side of the same configuration band as the date-and-size-selector. On desktop, it occupies about 4–5 of 12 columns (approximately 35–40% of the container width) and sits flush to the right edge of the central container. Its top edge aligns with the date-and-size-selector, but its bottom edge may be shorter, making it feel like a compact summary card. The card uses a slightly stronger accent color or border (e.g., a bright brand hue for the header strip) to draw attention without overshadowing the hero at the top. Inside, 24px padding frames the content: a bold total price at the top, followed by smaller lines for breakdown (base price, extra swimmers, taxes), and a clear indicator of availability (e.g., “Spots Available” or “Waitlist Only”) using colored tags or icons. There is 16–24px vertical spacing between these blocks to prevent a cramped feel. On tablets and mobile, this panel stacks below the date-and-size-selector, spans nearly full width, and is centered, maintaining a 24–32px vertical gap from the selector to reflect their close relationship.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Always within the main scroll flow; visually paired with date-and-size-selector in a side-by-side band on desktop, stacking below it on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-request-form is placed directly below the configuration band, separated by about 32–48px of vertical space to clearly indicate a new section of the booking flow. It returns to the neutral page background and sits fully within the central max-width container. The form spans approximately 70–80% of the container width on desktop and is centered, allowing generous left and right margins; on mobile it uses nearly full width with standard side padding. The internal layout is vertical, with clearly labeled groups such as contact details, party details (if distinct from earlier selections), and special requests. Each group is introduced by a section title and separated by 24–32px vertical spacing. Field rows use 8–16px spacing between inputs to maintain the low-to-medium density feel. Primary action buttons (e.g., “Submit Reservation Request”) are left-aligned to match form field alignment, with ample padding and a bright brand color that resonates with the hero section. The form’s height may extend beyond the initial viewport, encouraging a natural scroll downward without feeling heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable section; occupies a prominent chunk of vertical space but has no sticky behavior. Its visual weight is driven by content and button style rather than structural tricks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-confirmation-panel appears directly beneath the reservation-request-form within the central content column. When present, it is separated from the form by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, making it feel like the immediate next step in the flow. It spans roughly the same width as the form (70–80% of the container) and is centered so their edges align. The panel uses a brighter success-oriented accent (e.g., soft green or teal background with white text or a colored header strip) to differentiate it from other info cards and to signal completion. Inside, 24–32px padding encloses a concise confirmation message at the top, followed by clearly formatted reservation details (date, time, party size, total price, reservation ID) laid out in a simple two-column detail grid or a list with labels and values. There is 16–24px spacing between rows of details to keep it readable and friendly. If the confirmation is not yet available, this panel remains hidden and does not occupy space, preserving the linear scroll of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in the normal scroll flow immediately after form submission; no fixed or sticky behavior, but may animate into view (visual behavior only) without affecting its spatial relationship.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The followup-info-panel sits near the bottom of the page, below the reservation-confirmation-panel (or below the reservation-request-form if no confirmation is present yet). There is a slightly larger vertical gap—around 40–48px—above this panel to give it a closing, epilogue-like feel, similar to the large intro text section in the mockup. It spans about 60–70% of the central container width and is centered, creating comfortable side margins that make the text block feel airy and editorial. The panel may be visually simple—a plain white or very light tinted background without a strong card border—emphasizing the text: a heading like “What Happens Next?” followed by short paragraphs outlining timelines, contact methods, and any additional instructions. Line height is generous, and paragraphs are separated by 16–24px, reinforcing low density and readability. This section is designed to be easy to skim at the end of the booking journey and not compete visually with the interactive elements above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal scroll flow; serves as a final informational section with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-actions-bar is positioned at the very bottom of the viewport as a slim, persistent bar that spans the full width of the browser window. It sits above any system browser UI and overlays the page content with a subtle shadow or border at its top edge to separate it from underlying sections. The bar’s height is modest—approximately 56–64px—and it uses a solid neutral or slightly tinted background to remain visible but unobtrusive. Within this bar, content is constrained to the same central max-width as the main page; on desktop, a secondary text label (e.g., the current package name or step indicator) can align to the left, while action buttons such as “Back to Packages,” “Print Summary,” or “Contact Us” align to the right, spaced with 16–24px gaps. Buttons follow the same visual style as other interactive elements but use a slightly smaller size to avoid competing with primary actions in the main content. On mobile, the bar remains fixed to the bottom edge, with its actions stacked or arranged in a simple row centered horizontally. As the user scrolls, the bar stays in place, always available for quick navigation or support actions without drawing focus away from the main vertical narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the bottom of the viewport across scroll; full-width with internally constrained content, providing persistent navigation and utility actions.

name: Pool Schedule
title: Pool Schedule
description: View and interact with the weekly pool schedule, filter by activity and pool type, limit by time of day, navigate between weeks, and add specific sessions to My Schedule.

core_features: Load and display a weekly pool schedule using getPoolScheduleWeek, showing all sessions for the selected week.
Retrieve available filters (activity types, pool types, time-of-day presets) using getPoolScheduleFilters and apply them to the schedule query.
Allow users to adjust filters (activity type, pool type, time-of-day range) and update the schedule display and URL parameters accordingly.
Provide week navigation (previous/next week and return to a reference week) that updates the weekStartDate URL parameter and reloads the schedule via getPoolScheduleWeek.
Support direct deep-linking to a pre-filtered week view via URL parameters (weekStartDate, activityType, poolType, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo).
Enable users to add individual sessions from the schedule to their My Schedule list by calling addSessionToMySchedule.
Offer quick navigation to the My Schedule page and to program_detail.html for sessions that are linked to a related program.
user_workflows: View current weekly schedule: 1) User navigates to schedule.html (via header or home tile). 2) On page init, the controller reads URL parameters (if present) for weekStartDate, activityType, poolType, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo. 3) The page calls getPoolScheduleFilters to obtain available filter options. 4) The page calls getPoolScheduleWeek with the resolved parameters. 5) The timetable grid displays all returned sessions grouped by day and time for that week.

Filter by activity type, pool type, and time-of-day: 1) User changes one or more filter controls (activity, pool, time-of-day preset or custom time range). 2) Filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters. 3) The controller re-calls getPoolScheduleWeek with the updated parameters. 4) The timetable grid re-renders with only sessions matching the applied filters.

Navigate between weeks: 1) User clicks a Previous Week or Next Week control. 2) Week navigation logic calculates a new weekStartDate based on the current one. 3) The new weekStartDate is written to the URL. 4) The controller calls getPoolScheduleWeek with the updated weekStartDate and existing filters. 5) The timetable grid displays sessions for the new week.

Add a session to My Schedule: 1) User locates a desired session in the timetable. 2) User clicks an Add to My Schedule action for that session. 3) The session action handler calls addSessionToMySchedule with the session’s id. 4) On success, the handler receives a MyScheduleItem, updates the UI state to reflect that the session has been added, and may show a success state. 5) The user can navigate to my_schedule.html to see all saved items.

Open program details from a class session: 1) User clicks the main area or a dedicated details action on a session that has a related_program_id. 2) The session detail component constructs a link to program_detail.html with programId set to related_program_id. 3) Browser navigates to program_detail.html?programId=<related_program_id> where the user can see full program information.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters and trigger getPoolScheduleFilters and getPoolScheduleWeek to populate filters and the initial schedule.
On change of any filter control (activity type, pool type, time-of-day preset, or start/end time fields), update URL parameters and refresh schedule data via getPoolScheduleWeek.
On click of week navigation controls (previous, next, or reset to reference week), adjust the weekStartDate URL parameter and re-fetch sessions via getPoolScheduleWeek.

On click of a schedule session row or cell, open a detail panel or overlay showing information derived from the PoolScheduleSession and providing actions such as Add to My Schedule and View Program Details (when applicable).
On click of Add to My Schedule for a session, call addSessionToMySchedule with the session id, then update local UI state based on the success and message returned.
On click of the My Schedule link or button in the header, navigate to my_schedule.html without altering the user’s filter state on schedule.html.
On click of a View Program Details link for a session with a related_program_id, navigate to program_detail.html with programId set from that related_program_id.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL query parameters and API responses, with no reliance on localStorage. The primary state parameters are weekStartDate, activityType, poolType, startTimeFrom, and startTimeTo. On initial load, the page reads these parameters from the URL. If one or more parameters are not provided, the page derives defaults using client-side logic (for example, selecting a reference weekStartDate based on the current date, or leaving filters unset) but does not hardcode specific filter values. The resolved values are passed to getPoolScheduleWeek to retrieve sessions. getPoolScheduleFilters is called independently on load to obtain the sets of selectable activityTypes, poolTypes, and timeOfDayOptions; the UI binds filter controls directly to these returned collections. When the user changes filters or week navigation, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (using navigation or history APIs) and then calls getPoolScheduleWeek again with the new parameter values. The schedule displayed in the timetable is always consistent with the latest URL state. Adding a session to My Schedule does not modify URL parameters; it updates only transient UI state using the MyScheduleItem returned from addSessionToMySchedule. Deep links from other pages pre-populate the URL parameters so that bookmarked or shared links open directly to the corresponding week and filter combination.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial loading of the schedule page by reading URL parameters, fetching filter options, and loading the weekly sessions.
event_handlers: onInit: Read weekStartDate, activityType, poolType, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo from the URL query string and store them in page state.
onInit: Invoke getPoolScheduleFilters with no parameters and store the returned activityTypes, poolTypes, and timeOfDayOptions for use by the filter component.
onInit: Invoke getPoolScheduleWeek with the resolved URL parameter values and store the returned weekStartDate and sessions list.
onUrlChange: When URL query parameters change due to user interaction (filters or week navigation), re-invoke getPoolScheduleWeek with the updated parameter values and update the sessions state.

components.functionality: Displays the page title and a My Schedule navigation action, always accessible at the top of the page.
event_handlers: onClickMySchedule: Navigate to my_schedule.html, preserving or discarding query parameters based on global navigation rules (this component does not modify schedule state).

components.functionality: Presents controls to filter the weekly schedule by activity type, pool type, and time-of-day, using options returned by getPoolScheduleFilters and reflecting the current URL parameter values.
event_handlers: onFiltersLoaded: When getPoolScheduleFilters results are available, populate activity type, pool type, and time-of-day preset controls with the returned options.
onFilterActivityTypeChange: Update the activityType URL parameter based on the user’s selection and trigger a schedule reload via schedule-page-init-controller.
onFilterPoolTypeChange: Update the poolType URL parameter based on the user’s selection and trigger a schedule reload.

onTimeOfDayPresetSelect: When a time-of-day preset is chosen, set startTimeFrom and startTimeTo URL parameters using the corresponding values from the selected timeOfDayOptions item and trigger a schedule reload.
onStartTimeFromChange: When the user adjusts the earliest start time manually, update the startTimeFrom URL parameter, clear any active time-of-day preset if necessary, and trigger a schedule reload.
onStartTimeToChange: When the user adjusts the latest start time manually, update the startTimeTo URL parameter, clear any active time-of-day preset if necessary, and trigger a schedule reload.
onClearFilters: Remove activityType, poolType, startTimeFrom, and startTimeTo from the URL query string and trigger a schedule reload with unfiltered parameters.

components.functionality: Provides controls to move backward or forward by calendar weeks and optionally return to a reference week, updating weekStartDate in the URL and reloading the schedule.
event_handlers: onRender: Read the current effective weekStartDate from page state (which originates from URL or getPoolScheduleWeek response) to display the week range label.

onClickPreviousWeek: Compute a new weekStartDate representing the previous calendar week relative to the current value, update the weekStartDate URL parameter, and trigger a schedule reload via schedule-page-init-controller.
onClickNextWeek: Compute a new weekStartDate representing the following calendar week, update the weekStartDate URL parameter, and trigger a schedule reload.
onClickResetWeek: Optionally compute a reference weekStartDate (for example, a week that includes the current date), write it to the URL, and trigger a schedule reload.

components.functionality: Displays the weekly schedule as a structured grid grouped by day and time, using the sessions returned from getPoolScheduleWeek, and exposes actions on each session such as viewing details and adding to My Schedule.
event_handlers: onSessionsUpdate: When the sessions array from getPoolScheduleWeek is updated, group and render sessions into the timetable structure organized by day_of_week and time.
onClickSessionCell: When a user selects a session cell, pass the corresponding PoolScheduleSession object to the session-detail-panel to display detailed information.
onClickAddToMyScheduleFromCell: When the user activates the Add to My Schedule action from within a session cell, call the session-add-to-my-schedule-handler with that session’s id.

components.functionality: Shows detailed information for a selected PoolScheduleSession and offers contextual actions, including adding the session to My Schedule and navigating to the related program details when available.
event_handlers: onOpen: Receive and display the selected PoolScheduleSession, including title, session_date, start_datetime, end_datetime, activity_type, pool_type, and any pool-related context already available.
onClickAddToMySchedule: Invoke the session-add-to-my-schedule-handler for the currently displayed PoolScheduleSession.
onClickViewProgramDetails: If the selected PoolScheduleSession has a related_program_id, construct a URL to program_detail.html with programId set to related_program_id and navigate there.
onClose: Hide the detail panel and clear any transient selection state.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic of adding a PoolScheduleSession to the user's My Schedule using addSessionToMySchedule and updating UI state based on the result.
event_handlers: onRequestAdd: Accept a poolScheduleSessionId from a caller (timetable grid or detail panel) and call addSessionToMySchedule with that id.

onAddSuccess: When addSessionToMySchedule returns success, store or update the returned MyScheduleItem in local component state so that related UI elements (e.g., buttons for that session) can reflect an added state.
onAddFailure: When addSessionToMySchedule returns a non-success response, expose the returned message to the calling UI component so it can display an error indication.
onReAddAttempt: Optionally prevent duplicate Add to My Schedule actions for the same session within the current page lifecycle by checking against stored MyScheduleItem instances.

components.functionality: Provides a consistent navigation control for users to view their aggregated My Schedule page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to my_schedule.html using standard link behavior without modifying any schedule parameters.

components.functionality: Keeps the page state synchronized with URL query parameters so that the visible schedule, filters, and week selection always reflect and update the URL.
event_handlers: onFilterChange: When filters or week navigation components signal a state change, update the corresponding URL parameters (weekStartDate, activityType, poolType, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo) and notify the schedule-page-init-controller to re-fetch data.

onPopState: When the browser history changes (e.g., back/forward navigation), re-read the current URL parameters and request an updated schedule via getPoolScheduleWeek, as well as re-sync filter control values.

onDeepLinkLoad: On initial entry from an external link with pre-set query parameters, ensure these values are treated as the source of truth for initial filter and week state before any API calls are made.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom structure: a shallow header bar at the very top (logo + main navigation + contextual link to My Schedule), followed by a colorful, full-width filter band that visually echoes the hero band from the mockup. Immediately below, a centered content column contains the week navigation controls and the timetable grid. On desktop, a session detail panel appears to the right of the grid within the same visual band; on smaller screens it appears below the grid. Non-visual logic components (init, URL sync, add-to-schedule handler) run in the background and do not alter the visible layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom structure: a shallow header bar at the very top (logo + main navigation + contextual link to My Schedule), followed by a colorful, full-width filter band that visually echoes the hero band from the mockup. Immediately below, a centered content column contains the week navigation controls and the timetable grid. On desktop, a session detail panel appears to the right of the grid within the same visual band; on smaller screens it appears below the grid. Non-visual logic components (init, URL sync, add-to-schedule handler) run in the background and do not alter the visible layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar occupies roughly 10–12% of the initial viewport height with airy padding. The filter bar takes another 10–15%, rendered as a visually strong but shallow band. The main schedule band (week navigation + timetable + detail panel) consumes the majority of the viewport (around 60–70% initially), mirroring how the hero plus intro text dominate in the mockup. Vertical spacing between header, filter bar, and main schedule band uses 24–32px gaps to maintain clear section separation. Within the main band, the timetable grid occupies roughly two-thirds of the central width and the detail panel one-third on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: Structural areas (header, filter bar, week navigation) are low-to-medium density with plenty of white (or light) space and clear typography. The timetable grid is denser—many cells and labels—similar to the dense hero cards in the mockup, but it is contained within a single dominant band so the page doesn’t feel cluttered. The detail panel uses generous padding and readable line height to feel like an editorial info card rather than a cramped dialog.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first hits the top-left logo in the header bar, then scans right across the main navigation and My Schedule link. From there it drops to the bright filter bar, scanning left-to-right across filter controls. The gaze then moves down to the week navigation at the top of the main schedule band, then into the timetable grid where it scans both horizontally (across days) and vertically (down time slots). When a session is selected, attention shifts to the detail panel on the right (desktop) or below (mobile). Background behaviors like URL state sync and add-to-schedule handling are invisible and do not compete for visual attention.

layout.overall_description: The Pool Schedule page reads like a modern, spacious web app built on top of the family-friendly, colorful aesthetic of the mockup. A shallow, clean header runs full-width across the top, with the aquatic center logo on the left and a horizontal navigation on the right, including a clearly visible My Schedule link. Immediately beneath this, a vibrant, full-width filter bar spans edge-to-edge, behaving like a functional hero band: colorful pills, dropdowns, and toggles for activity type, pool type, and time of day are arranged in a single horizontal strip with generous padding. Below that, the content is constrained to a centered, max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. At the top of this container, the week navigation controls form a concise horizontal row, allowing users to switch weeks while maintaining breathing room around the controls. The core of the page is a large schedule band: a timetable grid anchored on the left two-thirds shows days versus times, while on desktop a session detail panel occupies the right third, aligned to the same top as the grid and matching its height visually. The grid is denser but framed by generous margins, so it feels like a focused tool rather than clutter. On smaller screens the detail panel slides underneath the grid as a full-width card. All interactive elements follow consistent spacing (8–16px padding, 24–32px gaps between groups), and the overall composition guides the user smoothly from navigation to filtering to exploring sessions and then into details, without overwhelming them with controls at once.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller operates entirely behind the scenes and does not occupy any visual space in the layout. It initializes the schedule state as the page loads so that, visually, the user immediately sees the correct default week and filters applied in the header, filter bar, and timetable grid. Its effect is perceived only in that the visible components render in a ready-to-use state without intermediate loading fragments; it does not add any pixels, borders, or areas on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; runs at page load and on state changes without impacting layout or consuming screen real estate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header bar sits pinned to the very top of the page, spanning 100% width edge-to-edge. It follows the shallow header band pattern from the mockup, occupying about 10–12% of the initial viewport height with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding inside a max-width content container centered on the page. Within this container, the logo is anchored to the left, taking up roughly 2–3 of the 12 columns, while the right 9–10 columns hold a horizontal navigation list and the My Schedule link aligned to the right. The bar uses a white or very light background with subtle bottom border or shadow to separate it from the filter band below. On scroll, the bar remains fixed (sticky) at the top so that navigation and My Schedule remain accessible while users scroll through the timetable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the viewport; full-width band with centered max-width content, remaining visible while the rest of the page scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned directly below the header bar, forming a distinct full-width band that visually echoes the colorful hero section from the mockup. It spans 100% of the viewport width with a vibrant background color (e.g., aqua or a friendly gradient), and its content is constrained to a centered container matching the header’s max width. Vertically, it is a shallow but prominent strip, occupying around 10–15% of the initial viewport height with 16–24px of top and bottom padding. Within the 12-column grid, filter controls (activity type, pool type, time-of-day, and possibly a reset button) are laid out in a single horizontal row on desktop: starting flush left, filters align from left to right across roughly 9–10 columns, with consistent 16–24px gaps between each control. On the far right, there is a small area of whitespace to prevent crowding against the edge. On smaller viewports, the filter controls wrap into two rows while preserving 16px vertical spacing between rows. The overall effect is a bright, functional strip that feels like an interactive hero for the schedule.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width band positioned under the sticky header; scrolls away with the page content (not fixed) so it anchors the top of the main content but does not permanently occupy viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The week navigation controls sit immediately below the filter bar inside the main content column, separated from the filter band by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. The controls are horizontally arranged within the centered max-width container, aligned to the implicit 12-column grid. On desktop, the current week label is left-aligned, occupying about 4–5 columns, while previous/next week buttons and a date-picker-style control are grouped to the right, spread across 4–5 columns with 8–16px gaps between buttons. This row occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the main schedule band’s height, leaving ample space below for the timetable grid. The area has a clean, low-density look—minimal backgrounds, plenty of whitespace above and below (~16px)—so that the controls feel lightweight and easy to scan before the user’s eye moves down into the denser grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row inside the main content column; scrolls with the page. It could optionally become sticky within the schedule area on taller screens, but primarily it behaves as a standard block above the timetable grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timetable grid is the dominant functional element of the page and lives directly below the week navigation controls, with around 16–24px of vertical spacing separating them. It is contained within the same centered max-width container but uses the majority of the available width and height. On desktop, the grid occupies the left two-thirds of the 12-column layout (approximately 7–8 columns), starting at the left margin of the content column. Its top aligns horizontally with the top of the session detail panel on the right so that both form a single, unified schedule band. Vertically, the grid extends for most of the remaining viewport height and beyond as needed, enabling vertical scrolling within the page. Days of the week run along the top as column headers, and time slots run down the left, creating a dense but organized matrix. Session blocks appear as colorful cards within cells, echoing the energetic, poster-like style seen in the hero cards from the mockup: rich colors, rounded corners, and compact text. Margins of 24–32px are maintained on the left and right edges of the grid within the container so it does not feel pressed against the page edge. On smaller screens where there is no room for a side-by-side detail panel, the grid becomes full width (spanning all 12 columns) while preserving generous padding around its edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable as part of the page content. On taller screens, the grid extends below the fold and scrolls vertically, while maintaining its alignment with the detail panel where present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On desktop, the session detail panel sits to the right of the timetable grid within the same main schedule band, creating a two-column layout. It starts aligned to the top of the grid and occupies approximately the right one-third of the 12-column width (about 4–5 columns). It has a card-like appearance with a white or very light background, a subtle shadow or border, and generous internal padding of 16–24px. The panel stretches vertically to roughly match the visible height of the grid so that the two areas feel balanced, but can scroll internally if the content exceeds that height. By default, when no session is selected, it can either show helpful instructional text (Select a session to see details) or a friendly illustration to keep the space from feeling empty. When a session is selected, the panel becomes visually active, showing session title, time, pool, description, and the Add to My Schedule action grouped toward the bottom with extra padding and emphasis. On tablet and mobile, the panel reflows: it moves below the timetable grid as a full-width section, separated by 24–32px vertical spacing. In that mode, it behaves like a bottom card, potentially with a slight elevation or top-rounded corners to echo a slide-up sheet, while still living in the normal scroll flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive, anchored to the right of the grid on desktop (side-by-side layout) and reflowing underneath the grid as a full-width block on smaller screens. It scrolls with the page; no fixed positioning is used to avoid obstructing the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Visually, the Add to My Schedule action appears as a primary button within the session detail panel, typically positioned toward the lower portion of the panel with 16–24px padding around it and at least 16px spacing from surrounding text. The button spans roughly 60–80% of the panels width on desktop and is center-aligned horizontally within the panel content area, making it easy to hit while keeping enough white space around it to stand out. However, the handler component itself is logic-only: it manages the interaction when the user taps or clicks the button. It does not add any separate visible UI beyond what is already represented by the button in the detail panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavior wired to the Add to My Schedule button inside the detail panel; it does not occupy layout space on its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The My Schedule link is placed within the right side of the schedule-header-bar, integrated into the main horizontal navigation area. It appears as a prominent navigation item near the far right edge of the nav list, possibly styled as a slightly more emphasized link or pill (e.g., a subtle accent color or outlined button) to indicate its importance without overshadowing the brand. It sits within the same 12-column header grid, aligned to the right margin with 16–24px horizontal padding around its clickable area and 8–12px vertical padding to ensure a comfortable touch target. Visually, it participates in the initial left-to-right scan across the header, making it easy for users to jump from viewing the schedule to their personal schedule.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline part of the header bars horizontal navigation; remains visible at the top of the viewport due to the headers sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is entirely non-visual and does not alter any spatial relationships on the page. It synchronizes the current schedule state (selected week, filters, possibly selected session) with the URL so that when users navigate or share links, the visible layout (filter bar selections, week navigation state, highlighted session, and the content shown in the timetable grid and detail panel) reflects the encoded state. From the users perspective, the effect is that reloading or sharing the page brings them back to the same visible configuration; no additional UI elements, bands, or icons are rendered for this behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background behavior; no layout footprint and no interaction with the document flow.

name: My Schedule
title: My Schedule
description: Displays the user’s saved pool sessions, lane reservations, program registrations, party reservations, and membership purchases in chronological order, with options to review, manage, and navigate to related pages.

core_features: Load all MyScheduleItem records for the current user via getMyScheduleItems and display them in a single chronological list, optionally grouped by date.
Visually highlight a specific MyScheduleItem when the page is opened with a highlightItemId URL parameter, and ensure the highlighted item is easy to find (e.g., auto-scrolled into view).

Show key information for each MyScheduleItem, including its title, date, time range when available, and a label derived from item_type to indicate whether it is an open swim session, lap lane reservation, program registration, party reservation, or membership purchase.
Provide a remove action on appropriate MyScheduleItem entries that calls removeMyScheduleItem and updates the list without reloading the entire page, with basic success and error feedback.

Allow users to access related detail or management pages from each item where applicable, including navigating to party_detail.html for party reservations and membership_detail.html for membership purchases, using URL parameters expected by those pages.

Offer persistent navigation links for adding more activities, including links to schedule.html (pool schedule and open swim sessions), lap_lanes.html (lap lane reservations), and programs.html (classes and programs).
Support optional in-page filtering or segmentation between upcoming and past items using client-side logic on the loaded MyScheduleItem list, without additional API calls.
user_workflows: View my saved schedule: User clicks the 'My Schedule' header link; on page load, getMyScheduleItems is called; the returned MyScheduleItem list is sorted chronologically and rendered; if the list is empty, an empty-state message with links to schedule.html, lap_lanes.html, and programs.html is shown.

Arrive from another page after saving an item: User adds an item on schedule.html, lap_lanes.html, program_detail.html, party_detail.html, or membership_detail.html; that page redirects to my_schedule.html with highlightItemId set to the new MyScheduleItem.id; on load, the page calls getMyScheduleItems, finds the matching item, applies a highlighted state to that row, and optionally scrolls it into view so the user can quickly confirm the item was added.

Remove an item from My Schedule: User locates an item in the list and clicks its remove action; an optional confirmation step is shown; on confirmation, removeMyScheduleItem is called with the item’s id; on success, the item is removed from the rendered list and a brief success message is shown; on failure, the item remains and an error message with a retry option is displayed.

Navigate to party reservation details: User finds a MyScheduleItem with item_type = 'party_reservation' and clicks a 'View party details' link; the page constructs a URL to party_detail.html with a partyPackageId query parameter derived from the associated party package identifier available on this item or its related data, then navigates there.

Navigate to membership details: User finds a MyScheduleItem with item_type = 'membership_purchase' and clicks a 'View membership details' link; the page constructs a URL to membership_detail.html with membershipPlanId derived from the associated membership plan identifier available on this item or its related data, then navigates there so the user can review membership benefits.

Add more activities from My Schedule: User scrolls to the top or bottom quick-links section and clicks one of the actions: 'Add more pool sessions' navigates to schedule.html, 'Reserve more lap lanes' navigates to lap_lanes.html, and 'Find more classes' navigates to programs.html; these links do not require additional parameters from this page.

Filter schedule items (optional, client-side): User toggles a control such as 'Upcoming only' or 'Show past items'; the page recomputes which MyScheduleItem entries are visible based on their date and any available start_datetime/end_datetime fields, without making additional API calls, and updates the list display accordingly.
interactions: Page load interaction: On initial render, the page reads the highlightItemId parameter from the current URL (if present), then calls getMyScheduleItems once; when the response arrives, the page stores the full list in memory, sorts it by date/start_datetime, derives any grouping or labels, and marks the item whose id equals highlightItemId as highlighted.

Highlight behavior interaction: If a highlighted item is found, the corresponding row is rendered with a distinct visual state and may display a brief inline note such as 'Recently added'; this highlight state persists as long as the page is not reloaded or until the user navigates away; removing the highlighted item clears the highlight state automatically because the item is removed from the in-memory list.

Item expansion interaction: Clicking an item row (or a 'Details' toggle within the row) expands or collapses an inline detail section for that MyScheduleItem, showing additional metadata derived from its fields (e.g., item_type label, date and time range, and contextual help text) without leaving the page.

Remove item interaction: Clicking the 'Remove' button in a row opens an inline confirmation (or modal) tied to that MyScheduleItem; confirming triggers removeMyScheduleItem with myScheduleItemId equal to the item’s id; while awaiting the response, the row’s controls are disabled or show a loading state; on success, the item is removed from the in-memory list and the UI re-renders; on error, the item remains and the error message is displayed in the context of that row.

Error handling interaction for load: If getMyScheduleItems fails, the page shows a non-blocking error message (for example, above the list area) and exposes a 'Try again' action that re-invokes getMyScheduleItems; until a successful response is received, no schedule items are shown.

Navigation interactions: Clicking 'Add more pool sessions' always navigates to schedule.html; clicking 'Reserve more lap lanes' navigates to lap_lanes.html; clicking 'Find more classes' navigates to programs.html; clicking the 'View party details' control inside a party_reservation row navigates to party_detail.html with the appropriate partyPackageId URL parameter; clicking the 'View membership details' control inside a membership_purchase row navigates to membership_detail.html with membershipPlanId set accordingly.

Client-side filter interaction: Changing a filter control (for example, switching between 'All', 'Upcoming', or 'Past') updates a local filter state; the list component recomputes which MyScheduleItem entries match the selected filter using their date and optional start_datetime/end_datetime attributes and re-renders without additional API calls and without altering URL parameters.

Empty state interaction: If the loaded MyScheduleItem list is empty, the page displays an empty-state message and the same quick links to schedule.html, lap_lanes.html, and programs.html; clicking any of these links immediately navigates away from my_schedule.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the optional URL query parameter highlightItemId, which is read once on page load. If present, its value is treated as the id of a MyScheduleItem that should be visually emphasized after items are loaded. The page does not modify this URL parameter; it is set only by the source pages (schedule.html, lap_lanes.html, program_detail.html, party_detail.html, membership_detail.html) when they redirect or link into my_schedule.html. Internal page state includes: (1) the loaded MyScheduleItem list returned from getMyScheduleItems, stored in memory; (2) a derived, sorted version of that list (e.g., ordered by date/start_datetime); (3) a highlight state storing the id that matches highlightItemId, if any; (4) an optional filter mode (such as showing only upcoming items), which is maintained as in-memory UI state and does not affect the URL; and (5) transient UI flags such as loading, error state, active confirmation dialogs, and which item rows are currently expanded. No data is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; all persistent data comes from the assigned interfaces on each page load, and all navigation uses URL parameters as described.

components.functionality: On page initialization, this component reads the highlightItemId URL parameter, calls getMyScheduleItems to retrieve the user’s full MyScheduleItem list, applies client-side sorting by date/start_datetime, and exposes the sorted list plus the highlight target id to child components. It manages loading and error states and provides a retry mechanism if the API call fails.

Displays the page title, an optional summary (such as total item count or count of upcoming items, computed from the bound MyScheduleItem list), and optional filter controls for toggling between all, upcoming, and past items using only client-side logic.

Provides persistent links that allow the user to add more content to their schedule: a link to schedule.html for pool schedule and open swim sessions, a link to lap_lanes.html for lap lane reservations, and a link to programs.html for classes and programs.

Conditionally rendered when the MyScheduleItem list is empty after a successful load; shows a concise message that no items are currently saved in My Schedule and surfaces the same navigation links managed by my-schedule-quick-links so the user can immediately add sessions, lane reservations, or classes.

Renders the non-empty, filtered subset of MyScheduleItem entries in chronological order, optionally grouped by date. It applies a highlighted style to the item whose id matches the highlightItemId value propagated from the data loader. It delegates row-level actions (details toggle, navigation, remove) to my-schedule-item-row components.

Represents a single MyScheduleItem in the list. It displays the title, date, and time range when available, along with a type label derived from item_type. It supports expanding a details section, highlighting when this item id equals highlightItemId, providing a remove action that triggers the removeMyScheduleItem API, and exposing contextual navigation actions for supported item types (e.g., party reservations and membership purchases).

Provides a simple confirmation step before permanently removing a MyScheduleItem from the schedule. When triggered for a specific item, it shows contextual text derived from that item’s title and requires the user to confirm or cancel. On confirmation, it invokes removeMyScheduleItem with that item’s id and reports the outcome back to the row component.

Shows an optional inline notification near the top of the page when highlightItemId corresponds to a loaded MyScheduleItem, indicating that a new item was just added to the user’s schedule. It may include a shortcut control that scrolls directly to the highlighted item row.

Displays high-level error messages related to loading or mutating MyScheduleItem data (for example, if getMyScheduleItems or removeMyScheduleItem fails). For load errors, it offers a retry action; for remove errors, it presents feedback while leaving the item visible.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing site utility bar and main header, the My Schedule page introduces a full-width, colorful hero-style band dedicated to quick navigation (My Schedule Quick Links), echoing the four-column promotional strip from the mockup. Beneath this hero band, a centered main content column hosts status and highlight banners at the top, followed by a compact scheduling toolbar, and then either the schedule list (with stacked item rows) or an empty state panel. A modal confirmation dialog floats above everything when needed, and the data loader appears as an overlay or skeleton within the main content region.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing site utility bar and main header, the My Schedule page introduces a full-width, colorful hero-style band dedicated to quick navigation (My Schedule Quick Links), echoing the four-column promotional strip from the mockup. Beneath this hero band, a centered main content column hosts status and highlight banners at the top, followed by a compact scheduling toolbar, and then either the schedule list (with stacked item rows) or an empty state panel. A modal confirmation dialog floats above everything when needed, and the data loader appears as an overlay or skeleton within the main content region.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top utility + header region remains relatively shallow (~10–15% of viewport height on load). The quick-links hero band is visually dominant below it, occupying roughly 30–40% of the above-the-fold area on desktop with generous vertical padding and four equal-width cards. The main content column beneath (banners + toolbar + list/empty state) fills the rest of the viewport and scrolls vertically. Section separations (hero-to-content, banners-to-toolbar, toolbar-to-list) use 32–48px vertical spacing. Most interactive components use 8–16px padding internally, with list rows using 16–24px padding to maintain comfortable readability.
layout_strategies.content_density: Visual density is low-to-medium overall. The quick-links hero band is the visually densest area, with vibrant, poster-like cards; it is isolated in its own band to prevent clutter. The main content column leans more editorial: banners are concise, the toolbar is compact but not cramped, and the schedule list uses clear hierarchy, ample line-height, and vertical spacing between rows. When the empty state appears, it intentionally lowers content density with a large central message, icon/illustration, and plenty of white space.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a classic F-pattern: logo at top-left, across navigation at the top, then drops to the colorful quick-links hero band. Within that band, attention scans horizontally across 3–4 quick-link cards. From there, the gaze moves downward into the main content column, first encountering any error banner (most urgent), then highlight banner, then the toolbar as a control strip, and finally the chronological schedule list or empty state in a vertical flow. When opened, the confirmation modal interrupts this flow by centering the user’s focus, dimming out background elements. The data loader, when active, also temporarily dominates the main content region while keeping the global layout recognizable.

layout.overall_description: The My Schedule page appears as a bright, family-friendly management view that borrows the structure of a promotional landing page. At the top, the existing site utility bar and header with logo and horizontal navigation sit in a shallow band, leaving most of the screen for the page content. Immediately below, a full-width hero-style band spans edge to edge with a colorful background and a max-width content container centered on the page. Inside this container, three to four quick-link cards are arranged in a single horizontal row, each occupying about a quarter of the width on desktop and styled like mini posters for key actions (e.g., reserve a lane, view memberships). Beneath this hero band, the page transitions to a calmer white background where a centered content column (about 960–1200px wide depending on breakpoint) holds functional elements: first a prominent error banner (if present), then a friendlier highlight banner, then a low-profile toolbar with filters and date controls. Below the toolbar, the schedule area stretches downward in a vertical stack of lightly carded rows representing each upcoming session or reservation, ordered chronologically, with plenty of breathing room between rows and clear typography. If there are no items, this list area is replaced with a generous empty state panel that’s centered and surrounded by white space. Whenever a user attempts to remove an item, a modal confirmation dialog appears above a dimmed backdrop, centered in the viewport and taking focus. Loading states either overlay the main content area with a spinner and dimming, or temporarily show skeleton rows where schedule items will appear. The overall effect is energetic at the top and calmly structured below, balancing promotional energy with clear, manageable scheduling tools.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the main content column beneath the hero-style quick-links band, aligning with the same max-width container and centered on the page. When loading the page or refreshing the schedule data, it appears as either a semi-opaque overlay or skeleton content that covers the area where the banners, toolbar, and schedule list/empty state would normally be. On desktop, the loader spans roughly 80–90% of the page width (matching the main content column) and extends vertically to cover the visible portion of the list area, starting just below any persistent page header and hero band. Visual elements like a spinner or skeleton rows are centered horizontally within this region, with generous vertical padding (32–48px) to maintain the airy, low-density feel. The loader does not obscure the global header or the quick-links hero band; instead, it sits within the content column so users can still see the site structure while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a transient overlay within the main content column, positioned relatively within that container but visually layered above the toolbar, list, or empty state. It scrolls with the page if content exceeds the viewport height, but in typical loading states it is visible without scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar is positioned at the top of the main content column, directly below any inline banners (error then highlight) and below the hero-style quick-links band. It spans the full width of the centered content container (roughly 960–1200px on desktop, or about 70–80% of the browser width), with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. Horizontally, controls such as filters, date range selectors, and view toggles are arranged left-to-right. The primary controls (like a date selector or filter dropdown) align flush to the left, while less-critical actions (e.g., “Export” or “Print”) align to the right edge on larger screens, creating a balanced bar. The toolbar’s vertical height is relatively shallow, about 56–72px, maintaining a tidy strip that clearly separates navigation controls from the schedule content below. A 24–32px gap separates the bottom edge of the toolbar from the first schedule row or the empty state, reinforcing it as a distinct control section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow within the main content column and scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down. On shorter viewports, it remains at the top of the content region without sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-links component forms a full-width hero band immediately below the global site header. This band stretches from the left to right edges of the browser window, with a vibrant background color or subtle gradient that visually separates it from the white page body. Inside this band, a centered max-width container (approximately 1140–1200px wide on desktop) houses a horizontal row of three to four quick-link cards. Each card occupies roughly 25% of the container width (effectively 3 columns of a 12-column grid), with 16–24px horizontal gaps between cards, and is visually rich: colorful backgrounds, icons, and short action titles. The band has generous vertical padding (32–48px top and bottom), making it a substantial visual block that consumes about 30–40% of the above-the-fold area on a typical desktop screen. On narrower screens, the cards wrap to multiple rows while preserving generous spacing, with each card expanding to roughly 50–100% width depending on the breakpoint. The bottom of this hero band is separated from the main content column by a 32–48px gap, ensuring the transition from promotional energy to functional schedules feels clear and structured.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in position relative to the page content (not sticky), part of the normal scrollable flow. It remains at the top of the content stack and scrolls out of view as users move down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state sits in the same spatial slot as the schedule list, directly beneath the toolbar within the main content column. It is centered both horizontally and visually within the column, spanning roughly 60–70% of the content width on desktop, with ample white space around it. The panel includes an illustration or icon at the top, a bold headline, supporting text, and a primary action button (e.g., “Browse Programs” or “Book a Lane”). Vertically, there is 48–64px of breathing room above and below the empty state card before the next section or the bottom of the viewport, emphasizing its importance without feeling cramped. The content inside the panel uses generous line-height and 16–24px internal padding, creating a light, editorial feel consistent with the mockup’s intro text section. The empty state effectively becomes the focal point of the page when no schedule items exist, drawing attention to next steps rather than to absent data.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main content area in the normal document flow and scrolls naturally. It replaces the schedule list; only one (list or empty state) is visible at a time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule list is the primary content column beneath the toolbar, occupying the full width of the centered content container (about 960–1200px on desktop) with left and right margins aligning visually with the toolbar above. The list is a vertical stack of schedule item rows, ordered chronologically from soonest upcoming at the top to later items down the page. Each item row spans nearly the full width of the column, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between rows to avoid visual clutter. The list area begins approximately 24–32px below the toolbar, and can extend well beyond the initial viewport height, encouraging a natural scroll. The background of the list region remains white, while individual rows may have subtle borders or cards to maintain structure. At the bottom of the visible list, additional rows appear as the user scrolls (if many items exist), preserving a clean, linear top-to-bottom reading pattern that echoes the mockup’s long intro text block and subsequent content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in the normal scrollable flow within the main content column. It expands vertically as needed to accommodate more rows and is the main scroll driver for the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each schedule item row is styled as a lightly elevated or bordered horizontal card within the list. It spans the full width of the main content column, minus a small inset (for example, 8–16px internal padding that visually separates content from the card edges). The row height is content-driven, typically around 72–120px depending on the complexity of the information (e.g., basic swim session vs party reservation). Within each row, the left two-thirds are dedicated to textual details: a bold title (session or program name) at the top, date and time immediately below in a medium-weight style, and supporting metadata (location, lane number, or membership type) beneath, all with relaxed line-height and 4–8px vertical spacing between lines. The right third of the row is reserved for actions such as “View Details,” “Manage,” or “Cancel,” aligned horizontally to the right edge and vertically centered within the row. On very narrow screens, these action buttons wrap below the text, maintaining 8–16px vertical separation. A 12–16px gap exists between consecutive rows, and the entire card uses 16–24px of internal padding, consistent with the spacing scale, giving the rows a medium density that remains easy to scan and tap. Rows respond visually to hover or focus with subtle shadow or border-color changes, enhancing their interactive nature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives as repeated elements within the schedule list’s vertical flow. Each row participates in normal scrolling, with no fixed or sticky behavior; the interactive elements within the row are reachable via standard scroll and tap/click interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove confirmation dialog appears as a modal overlay centered in the viewport, floating above all other page content. The background behind the dialog (including the hero band, toolbar, and schedule list) is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark scrim that covers the full browser window, anchoring attention on the dialog. The dialog itself is a medium-sized card, approximately 400–480px wide on desktop (or up to 90% of screen width on mobile), with 24–32px padding on all sides. The title and confirmation message are top-aligned within the card, with clear, readable line-height and around 16px spacing between text blocks. At the bottom right of the dialog, primary and secondary buttons (e.g., “Cancel” and “Remove”) are aligned horizontally, with 8–16px spacing between them. The dialog is vertically centered, leaving equal or near-equal space above and below on typical screens, and never touches the screen edges. This layout makes the confirmation feel serious but not intimidating, consistent with a family-friendly aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal that overlays the entire page; it remains centered in the viewport regardless of scroll, preventing interaction with underlying content until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight banner appears as a horizontal inline notification bar at the top of the main content column, just below any error banner and beneath the hero-style quick-links band. It spans the full width of the centered content container (aligned with the toolbar and list), with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The banner uses a friendly, attention-catching background color (e.g., soft teal or yellow) and contains a small icon on the left, followed by concise text describing the highlight (such as “New family swim times added!”) and optionally a subtle link or button on the right. The content is vertically centered within the bar. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of this banner and the toolbar below, clearly separating it from functional controls. The overall height remains compact (around 48–64px), adding emphasis without dominating the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal document flow within the main content column. It scrolls with the page and is always positioned above the toolbar and schedule list while it is visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner is positioned at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the hero-style quick-links band and above the highlight banner and toolbar. It spans the same width as the content container and is visually more urgent, using a high-contrast background color (such as red or deep orange) and clear, legible text. Internally, it includes an error icon on the left, a concise description of the issue (e.g., “Couldn’t load your latest reservations”), and, when appropriate, a retry action or link. The banner has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with content vertically centered. It remains relatively shallow in height (about 48–64px) to avoid feeling oppressive, yet due to its color and placement it naturally captures the user’s attention before they interact with the toolbar or list below. A 8–16px gap separates the bottom of this error banner from the highlight banner when both are present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline component within the main content column’s normal flow. It scrolls with the page and is always stacked above the highlight banner, toolbar, and schedule list while visible.

name: Passes
title: Passes
description: Allows users to search, filter, and compare visit-based passes (such as guest passes) by type, format, visit counts, and budget, then open a specific pass detail page for configuration and purchase.

core_features: Load available pass filters (pass types, formats, sort options) via getPassFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls.
Parse URL parameters (passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy) to establish the initial filter and sort state for the page.
Query pass products via searchPassProducts using the current filter/sort state, including an isActive flag to restrict results to active passes.
Display the returned pass products in a structured list/grid, showing key attributes such as visits_per_pass and price for comparison.
Support user entry of desiredTotalVisits and maxTotalPrice to help find passes that can satisfy a specific number of visits within a budget.
Allow users to change filters and sorting; each change updates URL parameters and triggers a new searchPassProducts call without using localStorage.
Provide a clear no-results state when searchPassProducts returns an empty passProducts array, with options to adjust or clear filters.
Enable navigation to pass_detail.html when a pass product is selected, passing passProductId via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from Home with prefilled guest pass search
1. User clicks a link on index.html (for example, a guest passes shortcut) that navigates to passes.html with some URL parameters set (e.g., passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy).
2. On page load, the page reads the URL parameters and initializes internal state for pass type, format, desired total visits, max price, and sort order.
3. The page calls getPassFilterOptions to obtain the available passTypes, formats, and sortOptions and preselects values that match the URL parameters when present.
4. The page calls searchPassProducts with passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy, and an isActive flag so only active passes are returned.
5. The results are displayed in the pass results grid, showing each pass product with visits_per_pass and price, allowing quick comparison.
6. The user reviews the options; if one looks suitable, the user clicks that pass card, and the browser navigates to pass_detail.html with passProductId set to the selected product's id in the query string.

Workflow: Manually refine passes to fit a specific visit count and budget
1. User navigates directly to passes.html from the site header or footer without URL parameters.
2. On load, the page reads an empty or minimal URL query string, sets default filter state with no specific passType/format/visit/price constraints, calls getPassFilterOptions, then calls searchPassProducts with any applicable defaults (including isActive).
3. The user decides to target a specific visit count and opens the visit/budget controls, entering a desiredTotalVisits value and (optionally) a maxTotalPrice.
4. On form submission or blur/change events, the visit/budget component updates the URL parameters for desiredTotalVisits and maxTotalPrice (adding, modifying, or removing them as needed) and triggers a new searchPassProducts call using the updated URL-driven state.
5. The results grid updates with passes that better match the new criteria; if no passes are returned, the empty state component is shown with guidance to relax filters.
6. The user may further adjust passType or format filters; each change updates the URL parameters and triggers another searchPassProducts call.
7. Once a suitable pass is identified, the user clicks the card and is taken to pass_detail.html with passProductId passed via URL.

Workflow: Adjust sorting to find the best-priced pass combination
1. User is already on passes.html with some filters applied (from URL parameters or prior interactions), and a list of results is visible.
2. The user opens the sort control and selects a sort option (such as a price-based option) that is sourced from getPassFilterOptions.sortOptions.
3. The sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter to match the chosen sort option, then signals the results controller that URL parameters have changed.
4. The results controller re-calls searchPassProducts using the updated sortBy value, along with the current passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, and isActive flag.
5. The product grid re-renders with the passProducts array reordered according to the selected sort criterion, helping the user quickly see the least expensive or most appropriate passes at the top.
interactions:
Interaction: Change pass type filter
- User selects a pass type option in the filters form.
- The filter component updates the passType value in the URL query string (adding or updating the parameter).
- The results controller detects the URL change, reads the latest parameters, and calls searchPassProducts with the new passType while preserving other parameters.
- The product grid re-displays the new passProducts list.

Interaction: Change pass format filter
- User selects a pass format option (for example digital or physical) in the filters form.
- The filter component updates the format URL parameter, removing it if the user clears the filter.
- The results controller re-queries searchPassProducts with the updated format parameter.
- The results grid updates to include only passes that match the selected format.

Interaction: Enter desired total visits
- User types a numeric value into the desiredTotalVisits input.
- On change or form submission, the visit/budget component writes the numeric value into the desiredTotalVisits URL parameter (or removes it if the input is cleared or invalid).
- The results controller calls searchPassProducts with the new desiredTotalVisits value so the backend can compute which passProducts are suitable.
- The results grid refreshes to reflect the passes that help achieve the requested total visits.

Interaction: Enter maximum total price
- User enters a numeric value into the maxTotalPrice input.
- On change or form submission, the visit/budget component updates the maxTotalPrice URL parameter accordingly.
- The results controller calls searchPassProducts using the new maxTotalPrice to restrict the result set to passes below or equal to that threshold.
- If no results are returned, the empty-state component appears to help the user adjust filters.

Interaction: Change sort order
- User selects a sort option from the sort dropdown that is populated from getPassFilterOptions.sortOptions.
- The sort component updates the sortBy URL parameter.
- The results controller calls searchPassProducts with the updated sortBy value so the backend returns passProducts in the correct order.
- The results grid re-renders in the new sequence.

Interaction: Clear all filters
- User clicks a clear/reset filters control.
- The filters component removes relevant filter-related parameters (such as passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy) from the URL query string.
- The results controller, seeing the URL change, calls searchPassProducts with only the base criteria (and isActive flag), effectively resetting the list of passes.
- The filter inputs and sorting controls reset to reflect the cleared URL state.

Interaction: Open pass detail
- User clicks anywhere within a pass product card in the results grid.
- The product grid component reads the id of the clicked PassProduct.
- The browser navigates to pass_detail.html with a query string that includes passProductId set to this id.
- No additional data is persisted in localStorage; pass_detail.html will use the passed parameter to load details.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely driven by URL query parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page parses passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, and sortBy from the current URL. These values define the initial filter and sort state. The page first calls getPassFilterOptions (without parameters) to obtain valid options for passTypes, formats, and sortOptions. It then calls searchPassProducts with passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy, and an isActive flag, using the parsed URL values as arguments where present. Whenever the user changes a filter, visit/budget input, or sort selection, the relevant component updates the corresponding URL parameters using navigation methods (for example, by rewriting the query string). No state is stored in localStorage. A URL-change listener or central controller reacts to these URL updates by re-parsing the query string and re-invoking searchPassProducts with the new parameters, ensuring that the displayed passProducts always reflect the URL. This design makes the page fully sharable and bookmarkable: loading the same URL reproduces the same filter configuration and result set.

components.functionality: Initializes and manages the pass type and format filters, synchronizing selections with URL parameters and triggering searches through state changes.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getPassFilterOptions and populate selectable pass types and formats. Preselect options matching URLParameters.passType and URLParameters.format when they exist.
onPassTypeChange: When the user selects or clears a pass type, update URLParameters.passType in the query string, then notify the results controller so it can re-call searchPassProducts.
onFormatChange: When the user selects or clears a format, update URLParameters.format in the query string and notify the results controller.
onClearFilters: When the user chooses to reset filters, remove passType and format keys from the URL query string, then notify the results controller.

components.functionality: Captures desiredTotalVisits and maxTotalPrice inputs to refine pass search results, keeping both values reflected in URL parameters and driving new searchPassProducts calls.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URLParameters.desiredTotalVisits and URLParameters.maxTotalPrice to prefill the numeric inputs if present.

onDesiredTotalVisitsChange: When the user updates the desired total visits value, validate it and write it to the desiredTotalVisits URL parameter (or remove it if cleared), then notify the results controller to refresh searchPassProducts.

onMaxTotalPriceChange: When the user updates the maximum price, validate it and write it to the maxTotalPrice URL parameter (or remove it if cleared), then notify the results controller to refresh searchPassProducts.
onSubmit: Optionally used when the inputs are grouped in a form; on submit, ensure both values are synchronized to URL parameters before the results controller re-queries searchPassProducts.

components.functionality: Displays available sort options for passes, sourced from getPassFilterOptions.sortOptions, and updates the sortBy URL parameter when the user changes sorting.
event_handlers: onInit: After getPassFilterOptions has resolved, populate the sort dropdown with sortOptions and select the option matching URLParameters.sortBy if present.
onSortChange: When the user chooses a sort option, update the sortBy URL parameter to the chosen value and notify the results controller so it can re-invoke searchPassProducts with the new sortBy.

components.functionality: Acts as the central orchestrator for calling searchPassProducts based on current URL parameters and distributing the resulting passProducts to the display components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read all relevant URL parameters (passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy), then call searchPassProducts with these values and isActive set to restrict results to active passes. Store the returned passProducts in controller state.

onUrlParamsChange: Listen for changes to the URL query string caused by filter, visit/budget, or sort components. When a change is detected, re-read the URL parameters and call searchPassProducts again with the updated values and same isActive behavior.
onSearchComplete: When searchPassProducts responds successfully, update the internal passProducts collection and notify the results grid and empty-state components to re-render.
onSearchError: Handle any searchPassProducts errors (for example, by clearing the current passProducts array and signaling the UI to show an error message component, if implemented).

components.functionality: Renders a list or grid of pass products returned from searchPassProducts, showing key information such as visits_per_pass and price, and enabling navigation to the pass detail page on selection.
event_handlers: onRender: For each PassProduct in pass-results-controller.passProducts, create a card or row bound to the product's fields (such as name, description, visits_per_pass, price) without hardcoding any values.
onCardClick: When the user clicks a pass product card, read the PassProduct.id and navigate to pass_detail.html with a query parameter passProductId set to that id.
onProductsUpdate: When the controller updates the passProducts list (for example after a new search), re-render the grid to match the latest data.

components.functionality: Displays an informative state when searchPassProducts returns no passProducts, and provides a way to adjust or clear filters via URL changes.
event_handlers: onProductsUpdate: When pass-results-controller.passProducts becomes empty, show this component; hide it when the array is non-empty.

onAdjustFiltersClick: Offer an action that focuses relevant filter or visit/budget inputs so the user can modify criteria; this does not directly call interfaces but leads to filter components changing URL parameters and triggering a new search.

onClearFiltersClick: When invoked, clear relevant URL parameters (such as passType, format, desiredTotalVisits, maxTotalPrice, sortBy) which in turn triggers pass-results-controller.onUrlParamsChange and a new searchPassProducts call.
