components.functionality: Provides a unified mechanism for reading and writing URL parameters so that all components consistently share filter and sorting state without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onRead: Parse the current window.location.search string into a URLParameters object for consumption by filter, visit/budget, sort, and controller components.

onWrite: When any component requests a parameter change (e.g., passType or maxTotalPrice), update window.location.search accordingly using standard navigation methods, preserving unrelated parameters.

onLocationChange: Listen for URL changes (such as history state updates or direct navigation) and notify the pass-results-controller.onUrlParamsChange handler to re-run searchPassProducts with the updated parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top‑anchored, banded structure as the mockup: global utility bar and header with logo/nav at the very top, followed by the full‑width hero band (with promotional swim/passes content) and a large, center‑aligned intro text block for the Passes page. Directly beneath this editorial intro, the functional area begins as a full‑width but center‑constrained band. Within this band, the first row is a prominent, center‑weighted search/visit‑budget form with an adjacent sort control, followed by a two‑column layout where a left filter sidebar sits alongside a right results column containing the results controller and the pass grid/empty state.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top‑anchored, banded structure as the mockup: global utility bar and header with logo/nav at the very top, followed by the full‑width hero band (with promotional swim/passes content) and a large, center‑aligned intro text block for the Passes page. Directly beneath this editorial intro, the functional area begins as a full‑width but center‑constrained band. Within this band, the first row is a prominent, center‑weighted search/visit‑budget form with an adjacent sort control, followed by a two‑column layout where a left filter sidebar sits alongside a right results column containing the results controller and the pass grid/empty state.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above the functional components, the header plus hero still take roughly the top 50–60% of the first viewport, leaving the lower 40–50% of the initial view for the introductory text and the very top of the search/budget controls. After this, the main Passes section uses a centered max‑width container (e.g., 1080–1200px) laid out on a 12‑column grid. The left filter sidebar occupies about 3 of 12 columns (~25% width) and extends vertically down the page. The right results area occupies about 9 of 12 columns (~75% width) and contains the results controller and grid. Vertical spacing between major bands (intro → controls, controls → filters/results, filters/results → footer) follows the 32–48px rhythm, while internal component padding is in the 16–24px range to keep things airy but not sparse.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header, hero, and intro text maintain a low‑density, editorial feel with ample white space and relaxed line height. The functional Passes area shifts to low‑to‑medium density: the visit/budget search form is visually prominent but not crowded, with clear grouping of controls. The filter sidebar has denser content (multiple filter groups) contained within a single vertical card so that density is localized rather than spread across the page. The results grid is moderate‑to‑high density (multiple product cards with text and pricing), but large gutters and a white background prevent clutter. The empty state uses very low density—lots of white space and a simple illustration/message—to contrast with the grid and make the “no results” case feel calm and intentional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention flows in a classic F‑pattern mirrored from the mockup: logo at the top left, then nav to the right, then down into the colorful hero band and its promotional cards. Below the hero, the eye drops to the large, center‑aligned intro text for the Passes page, then moves slightly downward to the primary functional entry point—the visit/budget search form, which sits centered near the top of the main content band. From there, gaze moves left to discover the filter sidebar and right to scan the top row of the results area (result count and sort), then continues vertically through the pass results grid. The sort control, placed toward the upper right of the results band, fits naturally into the F‑pattern’s top horizontal stroke. Any floating chat/help control (global pattern from the mockup) remains peripheral at the lower left, picked up by peripheral vision only after the main content is scanned.

layout.overall_description: Below the bright, family‑friendly header and hero that introduce the aquatic center and passes, the Passes page transitions into a clean, functional band framed by generous white space. A wide, centered intro paragraph explains how passes work, set in large, readable type with relaxed line spacing. Immediately beneath this, a prominent visit and budget search form stretches across the upper middle portion of the content container, giving users a clear starting point to find the right pass. To its right (on desktop), a smaller sort control aligns with the top of the results, forming a tidy horizontal control bar that echoes the header’s horizontal nav. Dropping down, the layout splits into a two‑column structure: on the left, a vertically stacked filter sidebar card, about one quarter of the width, anchors the page and scrolls alongside the content; on the right, taking up roughly three quarters of the width, the main results area displays a slim controller bar (result count, view options) above a structured grid of pass cards. The cards are arranged with generous gutters, creating a poster‑like but organized field of options that feels consistent with the hero band’s energetic visuals. On smaller screens, this all collapses gracefully into a stacked sequence: intro text, visit/budget search, a compact filter toggle, sort and result controller, and finally a single‑column list of passes. The whole page feels bright, approachable, and slightly promotional while keeping the core interaction—finding and comparing passes—clear and uncluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass filters form appears within the main content band, starting just below the visit/budget search controls. On desktop, it is anchored to the left side of the centered max‑width container, occupying approximately 3 of the 12 grid columns (about 25% of the content width). It is presented as a tall, card‑like panel with a subtle tinted background and a light border or shadow to distinguish it from the white page background, echoing the mockup’s pattern of isolating dense content within a single band or card. The top edge of the filter card aligns vertically with the top of the results area on the right, so that both filters and results feel like a unified section. Internally, filter groups (e.g., pass type, visit count, format) are stacked vertically with 16–24px vertical spacing between sections and 16px padding on all sides, keeping options easily scannable. On tablets, the sidebar narrows slightly but stays left‑aligned; on mobile, it collapses into a full‑width panel that sits above the results, typically revealed by a “Filters” toggle button, maintaining the linear top‑to‑bottom flow. The overall density is medium—multiple controls, but contained within a single, clearly delineated vertical strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the filter card is sticky within the viewport: as the user scrolls through the results on the right, the top of the filter panel remains pinned a short distance below the header, ensuring filters are always reachable while respecting the global header and hero. On mobile, the filter form behaves as a regular block element that scrolls with the page, optionally hidden/collapsed when not in use.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit‑budget controls form functions as the primary input surface for finding passes and is therefore given a prominent, central position. It sits immediately below the Passes intro text and above the two‑column filters/results layout, occupying the central 60–70% of the max‑width container on desktop. Horizontally, it is centered within the page, creating a strong anchor in the visual flow right after the large textual introduction, similar to how the hero cards form a visually dominant band in the mockup. Vertically, there is comfortable breathing room—approximately 32–40px margin above from the intro text and 24–32px margin below before the filters/results split—to maintain the low‑density feel. The form is displayed as a single card or bar with a white background and a subtle shadow, containing key fields like visit count, date range, and budget sliders. Inputs are arranged in a single row on wide screens (e.g., three or four grouped segments across the width) with 16–24px horizontal gaps, and wrap to a two‑row layout on smaller widths. On tablet and mobile, the entire control shrinks to full‑width, and the inputs stack vertically with 16px vertical spacing, maintaining readability. This component deliberately feels more like an inviting, central “search hero” than a small utility, mirroring the hero band’s sense of importance but in a functional form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The visit‑budget controls are a standard, non‑sticky block within the page flow. They sit fixed in the vertical sequence between the intro text and the filter/results section and scroll off‑screen naturally as users move down to explore results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass sort control is a compact UI element positioned in line with the top of the results area, making it easy to adjust ordering without competing visually with the primary visit/budget search. On desktop, it sits to the right of the visit‑budget controls in the same horizontal band, aligned with the right edge of the results column; together they form a subtle control strip across the top of the results section that echoes the header’s horizontal nav. The sort control itself might be a single dropdown or segmented button group, occupying roughly 1–2 of the 12 grid columns (about 10–15% of the width), with 8–16px internal padding to maintain touch‑friendly targets. There is about 16px of horizontal separation between the edge of the visit‑budget card and the sort control to avoid crowding. On tablet, the sort control may wrap to sit directly below the visit‑budget controls but maintain right alignment within the container, preserving the F‑pattern’s right‑hand anchor. On mobile, it becomes a full‑width or right‑aligned control stacked beneath the search form, with about 16–24px vertical spacing. It appears just above the results controller, so users can scan down from search → sort → result count → grid in a clean, linear sequence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sort control is static within the document flow, scrolling with the rest of the content. It does not stick to the viewport, but its position at the top of the results section ensures it is encountered immediately before the list of passes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass results controller is a slim, horizontal bar sitting at the top of the right‑hand results column, directly beneath the sort control band and aligned with the top edge of the pass grid. It spans the full width of the results area (approximately 9 of 12 grid columns, about 75% of the content width). The controller likely displays the number of matching passes, active filter chips, and possibly view or pagination controls. Following the low‑to‑medium density approach, the left side of the bar holds the result count and active filters in a single line, while the right side may host a layout toggle or per‑page selection, mirroring the right‑alignment of the sort control above. Vertical spacing is modest: about 16px margin above (separating it from the sort/search band) and 16–24px margin below before the grid begins. The result controller’s typography is smaller and more utilitarian than the intro or search text, ensuring it reads as informational scaffolding rather than primary content. On narrower viewports, this bar wraps content onto two lines if needed, keeping at least 8–12px vertical padding inside so it remains readable and touchable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results controller scrolls with the page as a standard block element. It acts as the visual hinge between the controls (search/sort) and the content (grid), but does not remain fixed in the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass results grid occupies the entire right‑hand column beneath the results controller, forming the main content field where users compare available passes. On desktop, it spans roughly 9 of the 12 grid columns—about three quarters of the page’s inner width—aligned flush to the right of the filter sidebar with a consistent 24–32px gutter between the sidebar and the grid. Within this area, individual pass cards are arranged in a responsive grid: typically 3 cards per row on large screens (each roughly 3 of the 12 internal grid columns), 2 per row on mid‑sized tablets, and 1 per row on mobile. The horizontal and vertical gutters between cards follow the larger spacing scale (24–32px), creating an open, poster‑like mosaic reminiscent of the hero’s promotional card strip. Each card balances imagery and text, reflecting the energetic but organized look of the site—colorful content but clean card edges, clear type, and consistent padding. The top of the grid begins approximately 16–24px below the results controller, and the bottom extends as long as needed, with ample white space before any page footer. On mobile, the grid converts to a single stacked column filling the width beneath the filters and controls, preserving the linear scroll pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results grid is a standard, scrollable content block, expanding vertically as more passes are loaded or paginated. It does not have fixed or sticky behavior; instead, it takes advantage of the page’s vertical scroll to let users browse multiple rows of cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass results empty state appears in place of the grid when no passes match the current filters or search parameters. It occupies the same right‑hand results column beneath the results controller, spanning the full width of that area (about 9 of 12 grid columns). To maintain the mockup’s low‑density, welcoming tone, the empty state is displayed as a large, centered panel with generous white space around it: there is a 48–64px margin above it (from the results controller) so it doesn’t feel cramped, and ample space below before the page footer. The panel itself may include an illustration or icon centered at the top, a bold but friendly headline, explanatory text, and buttons to clear filters or adjust criteria. Content is center‑aligned within the panel with 24–32px padding on all sides. Compared to the grid, this state feels calmer and more open, echoing the editorial feel of the intro text. On mobile, the empty state simply fills the entire width below the filters, maintaining large vertical padding so the message is clearly distinct from other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state behaves as a standard block element that conditionally replaces the results grid. When active, it scrolls with the rest of the page and sits in the same location where users expect to see passes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is non‑visual and functions purely as an underlying behavior that keeps the current search, filter, sort, and pagination state reflected in the browser URL. It does not occupy any physical space in the layout and has no visible UI. Its role is to ensure that as users adjust the visit‑budget controls, filters, sort options, or page through results, the page can be bookmarked, shared, or reloaded with the same state restored.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component has no spatial behavior in the rendered UI. It exists in the background, attached to the overall page context rather than any specific location, and does not affect layout or scrolling.

name: Pass Detail
title: Pass Details
description: Configure a specific pass product, review its usage rules and expiration, choose a quantity, and add it to the shopping cart.

core_features: Load detailed information for a specific pass product using getPassProductDetail based on the passProductId URL parameter.
Display key pass attributes, including name, price per pass, visits per pass, pass type, format, and active/inactive status.
Show usage information, including rules, expiration policy, and delivery / redemption details, from getPassProductDetail.
Allow the user to specify or confirm the number of pass products (quantity) to purchase, with behavior adjusted based on visits_per_pass.
Validate quantity input (e.g., required, positive integer) before calling addPassProductToCart.
Add the selected pass product and quantity to the shopping cart using addPassProductToCart.
Provide inline feedback on add-to-cart results, including success or error messages, and reflect updated cart information returned by addPassProductToCart.
Provide navigation to the cart page (cart.html) so the user can review or complete checkout after adding passes.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View a pass from the passes listing
1. User clicks a pass in passes.html, which navigates to pass_detail.html with passProductId in the URL.
2. On page load, pass_detail.html reads passProductId from the URL and calls getPassProductDetail(passProductId).
3. While loading, the page shows a loading state; when loaded, it displays pass summary (name, price, visits_per_pass, type, format) and usage/expiration/delivery details.
4. The quantity field is initialized (e.g., 1). The user reviews details and adjusts quantity if desired.
5. User clicks "Add to Cart". The page validates quantity, then calls addPassProductToCart(passProductId, quantity).
6. On success, the page shows a confirmation message and an updated inline cart summary, and reveals/enables a "View Cart" link to cart.html.
7. User can either change quantity and add again (if allowed) or click "View Cart" to navigate to cart.html.

Workflow: Reconfigure a pass from the cart
1. User is on cart.html and chooses to edit a pass item, which navigates to pass_detail.html with passProductId in the URL (and optionally quantity in the URL if the cart page supplies it).
2. pass_detail.html reads passProductId (and optionally quantity) from the URL, then calls getPassProductDetail(passProductId).
3. Once loaded, the page pre-fills the quantity control from the URL if provided; otherwise, it uses a default value.
4. User adjusts quantity if needed and clicks "Add to Cart" to apply changes, which calls addPassProductToCart(passProductId, quantity).
5. On success, the inline cart summary is updated based on the returned cart and cartItems, and the user can click "View Cart" to return to cart.html.

Workflow: Handling unavailable or invalid passes
1. User navigates to pass_detail.html without a passProductId or with a passProductId that does not correspond to an active pass.
2. The page detects the missing/invalid parameter before or after calling getPassProductDetail and displays an error state.
3. In this state, quantity selection and "Add to Cart" are disabled.
4. The page offers a basic navigation option (e.g., back to passes listing) so the user can select a different pass.
interactions: On page load or when the URL passProductId parameter changes, the page triggers getPassProductDetail(passProductId) and transitions between loading, success, and error states.
If getPassProductDetail returns a passProduct that is not active (is_active is false), the page disables the quantity form and add-to-cart actions and shows an unavailable message.
The quantity input supports user edits via keyboard or stepper controls; on change, the page validates that the value is a positive integer before enabling the "Add to Cart" button.

Clicking the "Add to Cart" button triggers client-side validation of quantity; if valid, it calls addPassProductToCart(passProductId, quantity) and shows a submitting state (e.g., disabled button, loading indicator).

On successful addPassProductToCart response, the page displays the returned message, updates an inline cart summary with data from cart and cartItems, and enables a contextual "View Cart" navigation control.
If addPassProductToCart returns an error (success is false), the page displays the error message and returns the form to an editable state without changing cart summary.
Clicking the "View Cart" control navigates the browser to cart.html (without using local storage), allowing further cart review and checkout.

A generic "Back" or "Return to passes" navigation control allows the user to move back to passes.html, relying on normal browser navigation or a direct link, without persisting state outside the URL and current runtime.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary URL parameter is passProductId, which is required for normal operation of pass_detail.html.

- Initial load:
  - On page initialization, the page reads passProductId from the query string (e.g., pass_detail.html?passProductId={id}).
  - If passProductId is present, the page calls getPassProductDetail(passProductId) and enters a loading state.
  - If passProductId is missing, the page immediately enters an error state indicating that no pass could be loaded and disables all configuration and add-to-cart actions.

- Handling getPassProductDetail results:
  - On success, the page stores the returned passProduct, usageRules, expirationPolicy, and deliveryDetails in in-memory state for binding to components.
  - If passProduct.is_active is false, the page sets a specific 'inactive' state: details are shown for reference, but quantity selection and add-to-cart are disabled.
  - On interface error (e.g., network failure or not found), the page enters an error state, displays a message, and disables add-to-cart.

- Quantity state:
  - The page maintains a quantity value in in-memory state for the duration of the session on this page.
  - An optional URL parameter (e.g., quantity) may be read on load to prefill the quantity field if present (for example, when arriving from cart.html). If not present or invalid, a default valid quantity (such as 1) is used.
  - URL parameters are not modified by the page during normal interaction; internal state changes (like updating quantity) remain in client memory only.

- Add-to-cart state:
  - A transient 'submitting' state is set when addPassProductToCart is called, disabling the add button and preventing duplicate submissions until a response is received.
  - On successful addPassProductToCart, the page updates in-memory cart summary data from the returned cart and cartItems objects and sets a 'lastAddSuccess' flag used to control success messaging and visibility of the 'View Cart' link.
  - On failure (success === false), the page stores the error message in state for display and clears the 'submitting' state without modifying any prior cart summary state.

- Navigation state:
  - Navigation to cart.html is done via a direct link or button; any context (such as which pass was last added) is not persisted beyond what is encoded in the current URL or what cart.html loads through its own logic.
  - Browser back/forward rely on the URL (including passProductId and optional quantity); when navigating back to pass_detail.html, the page re-runs getPassProductDetail(passProductId) to ensure current data is shown.

components.functionality: Reads the passProductId from the URL, loads the pass product detail using getPassProductDetail, and manages loading, success, and error states for the page.
event_handlers: onInit: read passProductId from URL and call getPassProductDetail(passProductId) if present; if missing, set error state.
onParamChange: when passProductId in the URL changes (e.g., via navigation), re-call getPassProductDetail(passProductId) and reset dependent child component state.
onLoadSuccess: store passProduct, usageRules, expirationPolicy, and deliveryDetails in shared page state; notify child components to render.
onLoadError: store error information in state and signal dependent components to show an error and disable configuration actions.

components.functionality: Displays core information about the selected pass product, including name, pass type, format, visits per pass, unit price, and active/inactive status, using data loaded by pass-detail-data-loader.
event_handlers: onDataChange: when PassProduct changes, re-render the summary and update any state that depends on visits_per_pass (for example, descriptive labels in the quantity form).

components.functionality: Shows textual details about how the pass can be used, when it expires, and how it is delivered and redeemed, using fields returned by getPassProductDetail.
event_handlers: onDataChange: when usageRules, expirationPolicy, or deliveryDetails are updated by pass-detail-data-loader, re-render the informational content.
onVisibilityChange: hide this panel when pass-detail-data-loader is in an error state and no data is available.

components.functionality: Allows the user to review or set the number of pass products to purchase. Adjusts helper text based on whether the pass represents single or multiple visits per pass and validates quantity input.
event_handlers: onInit: initialize quantityState using URL.quantity if present and valid; otherwise set a default valid quantity (e.g., 1).
onPassProductChange: when PassProduct is loaded or changes, update informational text about what a 'quantity' means (e.g., each pass includes visits_per_pass visits).
onInputChange: when the user changes the quantity input, update quantityState, enforce numeric-only input, and flag validation errors for non-positive or non-integer values.
onValidation: run whenever quantityState changes; if invalid, mark the form as invalid and disable the 'Add to Cart' button in the add-pass-to-cart-actions component.
onDisableForInactive: if PassProduct.is_active is false or if the data loader is in error state, disable the quantity input and clear interactive validation messages.

components.functionality: Contains the primary 'Add to Cart' button and coordinates the add-to-cart process by calling addPassProductToCart with the current passProductId and quantity.
event_handlers: onClickAddToCart: when the user clicks the 'Add to Cart' button, validate that PassProduct.is_active is true and quantityState is a valid positive integer; if validation passes, set submissionState to 'submitting' and call addPassProductToCart(PassProduct.id, quantityState).

onAddToCartSuccess: when addPassProductToCart returns with success === true, set submissionState to 'success', store addToCartMessage from the response message, and update Cart and CartItem[] in in-memory state from the response; also signal cart-summary-inline and navigation-links to show success state.

onAddToCartError: when addPassProductToCart returns with success === false or fails, set submissionState to 'error', store the error message for display, and allow the user to edit quantity and resubmit.
onStateSync: disable the 'Add to Cart' button whenever submissionState is 'submitting', when quantity validation fails, or when PassProduct.is_active is false or the data loader is in error state.

components.functionality: Shows a small, functional summary of the shopping cart after passes are added, based on the cart and cartItems returned by addPassProductToCart. Provides immediate feedback that items were added.
event_handlers: onCartUpdate: when add-pass-to-cart-actions updates the Cart and CartItem[] state on successful add, recompute and display a condensed summary (for example, total number of items and subtotal) based solely on CartItem[] data.
onClear: if the user navigates away or if the page is reloaded with a different passProductId, reset this summary until new addPassProductToCart results are received.

components.functionality: Provides navigation controls to move from the pass detail page to the cart or back to the passes listing, using URL-based navigation only.
event_handlers: onViewCartClick: when the user clicks the 'View Cart' control, navigate to cart.html via standard URL navigation; this control is enabled or highlighted only when lastAddSuccessFlag is true.
onBackToPassesClick: navigate to passes.html (or rely on browser back), without persisting additional state; this link is available regardless of add-to-cart status.

onSubmissionStateChange: when submissionState transitions to 'success', ensure 'View Cart' is visible; when the page loads fresh without a successful add, 'View Cart' can be present but not emphasized.

components.functionality: Centralizes display of loading indicators and error messages based on the state of data loading and add-to-cart operations, and coordinates disabled states for dependent components.
event_handlers: onDataLoaderStateChange: when pass-detail-data-loader enters loading, show a loading indicator and temporarily hide or dim child components; when it enters error, display dataLoaderErrorMessage and signal pass-quantity-form and add-pass-to-cart-actions to disable interactions.
onSubmissionStateChange: when add-pass-to-cart-actions enters 'submitting', show a small progress state; on 'success' or 'error', show the addToCartMessage and clear transient loading indicators.
onRetryRequested: if the error state is due to a load failure and the user clicks a retry control, re-trigger getPassProductDetail(passProductId) through the data loader.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing site-wide utility bar, header, and (if present) hero promotion band, this Pass Detail page uses a single centered content column constrained to a max-width (~1,120–1,280px). Within that column, content flows top-to-bottom in clear bands: a slim navigation/breadcrumb row, an error/status band (only when needed), then a dominant two-column product band where the pass summary sits on the left and the purchase controls sit on the right. Beneath this, detailed usage rules span the left column while a compact cart summary continues the right column, all within the same visual band of main content. The arrangement mirrors the mockup’s linear vertical structure and 12-column grid, where the left content column occupies roughly 7–8 of 12 columns and the right-hand purchase stack occupies 4–5 of 12 columns on desktop.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing site-wide utility bar, header, and (if present) hero promotion band, this Pass Detail page uses a single centered content column constrained to a max-width (~1,120–1,280px). Within that column, content flows top-to-bottom in clear bands: a slim navigation/breadcrumb row, an error/status band (only when needed), then a dominant two-column product band where the pass summary sits on the left and the purchase controls sit on the right. Beneath this, detailed usage rules span the left column while a compact cart summary continues the right column, all within the same visual band of main content. The arrangement mirrors the mockup’s linear vertical structure and 12-column grid, where the left content column occupies roughly 7–8 of 12 columns and the right-hand purchase stack occupies 4–5 of 12 columns on desktop.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and any hero promotions remain relatively shallow compared to the main content, leaving most of the above-the-fold area on this page to the core pass configuration band. On a typical desktop viewport, the navigation row and any status messages occupy a narrow strip at the top of the main content (around 10–15% of visible height). The central two-column band containing the pass summary and quantity/actions block is the dominant section (roughly 50–60% of the initial viewport height). Detailed usage information and the inline cart summary continue below with comfortable vertical separation, filling the remaining space. Vertical gaps between bands follow the 32–48px section margin pattern, while each panel uses 16–24px internal padding to feel airy but substantial.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps overall density low to medium: lots of white background, generous margins around the main content column, and readable line lengths. The left-column pass summary emphasizes a few key facts (name, type, price, quick rules) with larger text and comfortable line-height, similar to the editorial intro in the mockup. The right-column purchase controls are slightly denser—form inputs and buttons are grouped but still padded with 16–24px internal spacing. The usage details panel contains more text (rules, expiration) but uses bullet points, headings, and 8–16px vertical spacing between lines and items to avoid feeling crowded. Cart summary is compact but clearly separated by background or border to maintain clarity.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the navigation links at the top-left of the main content, then immediately drops to the pass title and key info in the pass-summary-header, following a classic F-pattern. From there, the user’s gaze moves horizontally to the right-column purchase widget (quantity form and add-to-cart actions) which is visually emphasized by card styling and button color. After the primary decision (choosing quantity and adding to cart), the eye naturally continues downward: on the left to read more granular usage rules, and on the right to glance at the inline cart summary. Loading and error states temporarily interrupt this flow by appearing as a full-width band just beneath the navigation links, while the data-loader can temporarily sit on top of the content as an overlay. This maintains a clear, predictable scanning path from top-left to bottom-right while highlighting the purchase CTAs.

layout.overall_description: Within the site’s existing bright, family-friendly shell (utility bar, header, and any top hero promotions), the Pass Detail page appears as a centered slice of content sitting on a clean white background. A slim row of navigation links or a breadcrumb anchors the top-left of this slice, providing a subtle entry point. Directly beneath, any error or status messages display as a colored alert band that spans the entire content width, clearly visible but not overly tall. Below this, the main body resolves into a two-column band stretching across most of the max-width container: the left two-thirds (about 7–8 of 12 grid columns) are devoted to the pass-summary-header and, beneath it, the pass-usage-details, stacked with generous vertical spacing. The right one-third (about 4–5 columns) holds a vertical stack of purchase-related elements: the pass-quantity-form and the add-pass-to-cart-actions combined into a prominent card-like block, followed by a smaller cart-summary-inline panel. Internal card padding and section gaps follow the 8px spacing scale (16–24px for padding, 32–48px between sections), echoing the airy but energetic feel of the mockup. Colors and typography highlight key price and call-to-action areas, while the bulk of the text sits in comfortably wide, readable columns. Loading and error handlers overlay or sit above this structure without altering the fundamental flow, ensuring that configuring a pass and adding it to the cart feels like a focused, guided experience framed within the site’s broader vibrant aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as an overlay centered within the main content column that contains all the pass detail components. When active, it covers the area from just below the navigation-links row down to just above any site-wide footer, aligning with the max-width content container. The overlay uses a semi-opaque white or very light background so the underlying layout is still faintly visible but clearly inactive. A loading indicator (spinner or animated icon) is centered both horizontally and vertically within this overlay. On desktop, this overlay spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width but is constrained to the same max-width container as the content; on mobile, it effectively covers the full width. Vertical padding around the loader content follows the generous spacing pattern (at least 48px from top and bottom within the overlay) to preserve the airy feel. This makes the loader feel like a temporary veil over the main banded layout rather than an entirely separate page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay within the main content container; centered both horizontally and vertically; blocks interaction with underlying components while visible, but does not scroll independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass-summary-header sits at the top of the main content band, directly below any error/status message and navigation-links. On desktop, it is anchored to the left side of the centered container and spans approximately 7–8 of 12 grid columns (about 60–65% of the content width), leaving the right 4–5 columns free for the purchase controls. Its top edge sits around 32–48px below any message bar, respecting the section margin pattern. The panel itself reads like a detail hero: it has a soft, lightly colored background (e.g., pale aqua) or a subtle border, rounded corners, and internal padding of 24px on all sides. The pass name appears in large, bold type at the top-left of the panel, with key metadata (pass type, location, age group) arranged in one or two rows beneath, aligned to the left. Price and high-impact info (e.g., “10-visit punch pass”) can be visually pulled out on the right side of this panel or directly under the title, but still within the same left column, forming a dense focal cluster that is easy to scan. The panel’s vertical height is moderate, roughly 25–35% of the visible content height on desktop, so it feels substantial but not overwhelming. Below it, a clear vertical gap of 24–32px separates it from the pass-usage-details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow; anchors the left column of the two-column grid and defines the starting point for the main content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass-usage-details panel is positioned directly below the pass-summary-header in the same left-side column, maintaining the 7–8 of 12 grid column width. There is a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px between the bottom of the summary header and the top of this panel, following the section margin rules while visually grouping them as related content. The panel has a simple white background with either a subtle border or a faint tint to distinguish it from the page background, and internal padding of 16–24px. Headings such as “Usage Rules” or “Expiration & Limits” sit at the top-left, with body text and bullet lists flowing below in a relaxed line-height (around 1.5) to echo the mockup’s editorial feel. This panel generally occupies the lower half of the initial viewport and continues below the fold as needed, while the right-hand column with purchase controls remains visible within the same vertical zone where possible. The content within this panel is vertically structured with 8–16px spacing between text blocks, making even dense rule sets feel scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content in the left column; grows vertically as needed while keeping its width and alignment locked to the left side of the main content grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pass-quantity-form occupies the top of the right-hand column that sits alongside the pass-summary-header. On desktop, this right column begins roughly aligned with the top of the summary header and spans about 4–5 of 12 grid columns (approximately 30–35% of the content width). The form is contained in a card-like block whose left edge lines up with the main container’s center region and whose right edge aligns with the overall max-width, mirroring the mockup’s hero cards in being compact yet visually strong.内部, there is 16–24px padding on all sides. The form fields (e.g., quantity selector, perhaps a dropdown for pass holders) are stacked vertically, left-aligned, with 8–16px spacing between fields. Labels are clear and concise, and input controls are sized with at least 8–12px padding inside. The vertical height of this form panel is moderate, often matching or slightly less than the height of the summary header next to it, so that the left and right panels feel visually balanced when first loaded. The bottom of this form panel leads directly into the add-pass-to-cart-actions area, with only a slim 8–12px separator or a divider line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; participates in the two-column grid next to the summary header. On narrower viewports, this block can stack below the summary header but still retains its card-like proportion and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-pass-to-cart-actions component is visually and functionally attached to the pass-quantity-form, forming the lower portion of the same right-column purchase card or an immediately adjacent card directly below it. It spans the same width as the quantity form (4–5 of 12 grid columns on desktop) and sits only 8–12px beneath the last input field to maintain a tight but clear connection. Inside, the primary “Add Pass to Cart” button is the key focal point: it is full-width within this column, with generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (12–16px) to make it feel substantial and easy to tap. Secondary actions (like “Cancel” or “Clear”) appear to the right of or beneath the primary button in smaller, less saturated styling. The vertical height of the actions area is relatively shallow compared to the form, but its bright button color and clear spacing give it strong visual weight. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of this actions area and the top of the cart-summary-inline panel below, preserving separation between the action and the status summary while keeping them visually related.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column and tightly coupled to the pass-quantity-form. Optionally, this actions area can become sticky within the viewport on taller pages—remaining visible as the user scrolls through usage details—by pinning to a small offset below the global header, but its default behavior is to scroll with the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-inline panel is positioned beneath the pass-quantity-form and add-pass-to-cart-actions in the right-hand column, maintaining the same width (4–5 of 12 grid columns) and alignment. A vertical gap of approximately 16–24px separates its top edge from the bottom of the actions area, signifying a new but closely related section. The panel itself is a compact status card with 16–24px internal padding, lightly contrasted background (e.g., soft gray or pale accent color), and a subtle border or shadow. It shows a short summary of the current cart state (e.g., items count, subtotal), with text aligned to the left and key figures in bolder or larger type. Its height is relatively short—often not more than 20–25% of the viewport height—so it does not compete with the main purchase controls or the detailed usage text. This card stays visually aligned with the lower part of the usage details panel on the left, forming a balanced base to the two-column band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the right column; scrolls with the page. It expands vertically if more cart details are needed but remains visually secondary to the quantity form and add-to-cart action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links component sits at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the global site header (and any hero band if present). It spans the full width of the centered content container but is visually lightweight, occupying only a shallow horizontal strip about 40–56px tall. Within this strip, breadcrumb-style links or a simple “Back to Passes” link are left-aligned, starting at the 0-column mark of the 12-column grid. There is comfortable vertical padding of 8–12px above and below the text, and ample white space to the right, maintaining the low-density, airy header feel from the mockup. A subtle divider line or a 16–24px vertical gap separates this navigation row from what follows (error/status bar or pass-summary-header). This placement ensures the user’s eye starts at the top-left and can easily orient themselves in the flow of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky row anchored at the top of the main content column; scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-loading-handler appears immediately below the navigation-links row and above the main pass-summary-header panel whenever there is an error message, warning, or important status to show. It spans the full width of the centered content container (all 12 columns) and uses a distinct background color (e.g., warm red/orange for errors, yellow for warnings) while maintaining 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Text is left-aligned and concise, with any icon or status indicator aligned to the left margin and message content to its right, following an 8–12px gap. The vertical height of this band is modest—usually 56–96px—so it is visible but not overwhelming. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of this status band and the top of the pass-summary-header, preserving the clear banded structure from the mockup. If multiple messages are needed, they stack within this bar with 8px vertical spacing between lines, but the component should avoid becoming taller than necessary to keep the main content in view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Dynamically inserted into the normal document flow; pushes the pass-summary-header and other components downward while it is visible. Does not overlay content and scrolls away with the rest of the page. May temporarily share responsibility with the pass-detail-data-loader during transitional states but occupies its own fixed vertical position just below the navigation-links row.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Page where users review items currently in their cart (passes, classes, memberships), adjust quantities, remove items, and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load the current user's cart and all cart items using getCartSummary on page load and after any cart modification.
Display each cart item with its name, item_type, unit_price, quantity, and total_price, along with the overall subtotal and itemCount from getCartSummary.
Allow users to change the quantity of any cart item and update both the item and cart summary using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove individual items from the cart and refresh the cart summary using removeCartItem.
Handle an empty cart state when getCartSummary returns no cart or zero items, and offer clear navigation back to passes.html and programs.html.
Provide a clear action to proceed to checkout, navigating to checkout.html while preserving relevant cart page context via URL parameters.
Provide feedback and error states for failed API operations (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem) with the ability to retry.
user_workflows:
1. Viewing the cart:
   - Step 1: User arrives at cart.html from any entry point (header cart icon, pass_detail.html, program_detail.html), potentially with URL parameters such as ?source=header or ?highlightItemId={cartItemId}.
   - Step 2: On page load, cart-page-controller calls getCartSummary.
   - Step 3: If getCartSummary returns a cart with items, cart-items-table renders each CartItem and cart-summary-panel shows itemCount and subtotal.
   - Step 4: If getCartSummary returns cart=null or items array is empty, cart-empty-state is shown with links to passes.html and programs.html.
   - Step 5: If a highlightItemId URL parameter is present, the matching cart item row is emphasized (e.g., scrolled into view and briefly highlighted) by cart-items-table.

2. Adjusting item quantity:
   - Step 1: User locates the desired item in cart-items-table.
   - Step 2: User uses cart-item-quantity-control (increment/decrement buttons or a quantity input) for that item.
   - Step 3: On quantity change confirmation (e.g., blur or button click), cart-item-quantity-control validates that the quantity is a positive integer and triggers updateCartItemQuantity with { cartItemId, quantity }.
   - Step 4: While waiting for the response, the specific item's quantity controls show a loading state and are temporarily disabled to prevent duplicate requests.
   - Step 5: On success, updateCartItemQuantity returns the updated cart, items, subtotal, and itemCount; cart-page-controller updates the local view state and re-renders cart-items-table and cart-summary-panel.
   - Step 6: If the updated quantity results in a subtotal or itemCount change, cart-summary-panel reflects this immediately. If the quantity change logically results in zero quantity (if allowed by back-end), the item may no longer appear in the list based on the returned items array.
   - Step 7: If updateCartItemQuantity fails (network or server error), cart-error-notification displays an error for this action with an option to retry the same quantity change.

3. Removing an item from the cart:
   - Step 1: User clicks a Remove action (e.g., a button) associated with a specific item in cart-items-table.
   - Step 2: cart-items-table triggers removeCartItem with { cartItemId }.
   - Step 3: While the removal request is in progress, the remove control and quantity controls for that item are disabled, and the item shows a loading state.
   - Step 4: On success, removeCartItem returns the updated cart summary. cart-page-controller updates the view state so that the removed item disappears from cart-items-table and subtotal/itemCount are updated in cart-summary-panel.
   - Step 5: If the removal causes the cart to have zero items, cart-items-table is hidden and cart-empty-state is shown instead.
   - Step 6: If removeCartItem fails, cart-error-notification shows an error message with a Retry action that re-calls removeCartItem for that same cartItemId.

4. Handling an empty cart and continuing shopping:
   - Step 1: User navigates to cart.html.
   - Step 2: getCartSummary returns cart=null or items.length == 0.
   - Step 3: cart-empty-state displays a clear empty-cart message and two primary actions: 'Continue shopping for passes' and 'Continue shopping for classes'.
   - Step 4: When the user clicks 'Continue shopping for passes', cart-continue-shopping-links navigates to passes.html with a URL parameter indicating the origin, such as passes.html?from=cart.
   - Step 5: When the user clicks 'Continue shopping for classes', cart-continue-shopping-links navigates to programs.html with a URL parameter such as programs.html?from=cart.
   - Step 6: When the user later adds items and returns to cart.html, getCartSummary will show the new items and cart-empty-state will be hidden.

5. Proceeding to checkout:
   - Step 1: User reviews the items in cart-items-table and confirms the subtotal in cart-summary-panel.
   - Step 2: User clicks the 'Checkout' button in cart-summary-panel.
   - Step 3: cart-summary-panel navigates the browser to checkout.html, including URL parameters that preserve origin and any highlight context, e.g., checkout.html?from=cart&returnUrl=cart.html.
   - Step 4: The checkout page (handled separately) uses its own interfaces and can navigate back to cart.html using the returnUrl if needed.
   - Step 5: If itemCount is zero or getCartSummary returns no items, the 'Checkout' button is disabled and not clickable to prevent proceeding with an empty cart.

6. Refreshing cart data when URL changes:
   - Step 1: User may update the URL manually or via navigation that changes query parameters (e.g., appending ?highlightItemId=... without full page reload in a SPA context).
   - Step 2: cart-page-controller listens for URL parameter changes (onUrlChange).
   - Step 3: If the path is still cart.html and only parameters change, cart-page-controller may re-run getCartSummary if a full refresh is desired, or simply update UI behavior (such as which item is highlighted) based on new parameters.
   - Step 4: Components like cart-items-table re-apply highlighting or scrolling based on updated URL parameters.
interactions: Click on header cart icon (from any page) navigates to cart.html, optionally with a source parameter (e.g., cart.html?source=header) for context. cart-page-controller then executes getCartSummary on page load.
On page load of cart.html, the onPageLoad handler in cart-page-controller calls getCartSummary with no parameters and populates local state with { cart, items, subtotal, itemCount }.
If getCartSummary succeeds, cart-items-table binds to the items array and renders one row per CartItem; cart-summary-panel binds to subtotal and itemCount; cart-empty-state is hidden.
If getCartSummary returns no cart or an empty items array, cart-empty-state is shown and cart-items-table and cart-summary-panel are hidden or minimized.

Changing quantity via increment/decrement buttons in cart-item-quantity-control triggers onIncrementClick/onDecrementClick, which calculate the new intended quantity and call updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity).

Manually editing the quantity input field (if present) triggers onQuantityInputBlur or onChange, which validates the input and calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity) only if the quantity has changed and is valid.
Clicking the Remove action for an item triggers onRemoveClick, which calls removeCartItem(cartItemId). On success, the updated items list is re-bound to cart-items-table.
Clicking 'Continue shopping for passes' in cart-empty-state or cart-continue-shopping-links triggers onContinueShoppingPassesClick, which navigates to passes.html?from=cart.
Clicking 'Continue shopping for classes' in cart-empty-state or cart-continue-shopping-links triggers onContinueShoppingProgramsClick, which navigates to programs.html?from=cart.

Clicking the 'Checkout' button in cart-summary-panel triggers onCheckoutClick, which navigates to checkout.html with query parameters such as ?from=cart&returnUrl=cart.html plus any existing relevant cart query parameters (e.g., highlightItemId).

If any API call (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem) fails, cart-error-notification is populated with error information and shown; clicking its Retry button triggers onRetryClick, which re-executes the last failed API call with the same parameters.

Clicking a specific cart item row (optional behavior) triggers onRowClick in cart-items-table, which may update the browser URL to include highlightItemId for deep-linking (e.g., cart.html?highlightItemId={id}) without reloading the page, and re-applies highlighting logic based on state_logic.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Cart page derives and controls its state primarily through the cart.html URL and its query parameters, while cart contents themselves are retrieved via the getCartSummary API. No cart state is stored in localStorage.

URL Parameters for cart.html:
- source: Optional. Indicates where the user came from (e.g., 'header', 'passes', 'program_detail'). Example: cart.html?source=passes. Used to adjust UI emphasis, such as which 'Continue shopping' link is visually prioritized; it does not affect API behavior.
- highlightItemId: Optional. If provided (e.g., cart.html?highlightItemId={CartItem.id}), cart-page-controller passes this value to cart-items-table so it can highlight and, if desired, scroll to that specific item row after getCartSummary returns items. This helps users find the item they just added. If no matching item is present, the parameter is ignored.
- error: Optional. Can be used by external pages (e.g., checkout.html redirecting back) to cause cart-error-notification to display a user-readable message. Example: cart.html?error=payment_failed. The page uses this parameter only to choose which generic error message to show; it does not affect API calls.

State Transitions:
- Initial Load: When cart.html is loaded, cart-page-controller parses query parameters (source, highlightItemId, error) and then calls getCartSummary. The resulting data is stored in in-memory state associated with this page instance and bound to components.
- After Quantity Update: updateCartItemQuantity returns an updated cart summary, which replaces the previous in-memory cart state. The URL parameters remain unchanged; no new parameters are added for quantity changes. If quantity changes are intended to be shareable via URLs, the page can optionally add or update highlightItemId with the affected cartItemId, but this is not required for core functionality.
- After Item Removal: removeCartItem returns an updated cart summary; the in-memory items list is replaced. If the removed item's id was referenced in highlightItemId, cart-page-controller clears or ignores highlightItemId since that item no longer exists. The URL may be updated to remove highlightItemId via history manipulation without full reload.
- Empty Cart: When getCartSummary or any subsequent cart update yields zero items, cart-empty-state becomes the primary visible component. The URL does not need to change; the UI state is derived entirely from the cart summary.
- Navigation to Checkout: When the user clicks 'Checkout', navigation from cart-summary-panel includes the current origin context as URL parameters, e.g.: checkout.html?from=cart&returnUrl=cart.html and, if present, &source={source}. The Cart page itself does not mutate the cart when this navigation occurs; it only changes location.
- Back from Checkout or Other Pages: If another page returns the user to cart.html using a returnUrl link, any existing parameters (such as source or highlightItemId) may be preserved or updated by that page. On every visit, cart-page-controller re-runs getCartSummary; no cart information is assumed from URL parameters alone.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top‑to‑bottom banded structure as the mockup: a narrow full‑width utility bar at the very top, followed by a full‑width header band with logo and horizontal navigation, then a full‑width hero band. For the cart page, the hero band becomes a colorful, promotional strip (e.g., featured passes, seasonal events) using the four‑card layout. Below the hero, the main cart content sits inside a centered max‑width container: a cart overview heading and subtext at the top, then a two‑column content area with cart items on the left and an order summary column on the right, followed by supporting information (refund policy, help text) stacked full‑width beneath. A floating chat button sits above the bottom-left edge of the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top‑to‑bottom banded structure as the mockup: a narrow full‑width utility bar at the very top, followed by a full‑width header band with logo and horizontal navigation, then a full‑width hero band. For the cart page, the hero band becomes a colorful, promotional strip (e.g., featured passes, seasonal events) using the four‑card layout. Below the hero, the main cart content sits inside a centered max‑width container: a cart overview heading and subtext at the top, then a two‑column content area with cart items on the left and an order summary column on the right, followed by supporting information (refund policy, help text) stacked full‑width beneath. A floating chat button sits above the bottom-left edge of the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Preserve the same vertical proportions: the utility bar and header take roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height combined, the hero band allocates ~35–45% of initial viewport height, and the cart content fills the remaining space. Within the main cart section, allocate roughly 65–70% of the container width to the cart items column on the left (spanning about 8 of 12 grid columns) and 30–35% to the order summary on the right (about 4 of 12 columns). Section-level vertical spacing follows the 32–48px rhythm: 32px between header and hero, 32–48px between hero and cart content, and 32px between major cart subsections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low to medium density. The header, hero band edges, and cart section container use generous white space and 16–24px internal padding to keep everything airy. Cart item rows are visually denser, with thumbnails, item details, quantity controls, and pricing grouped in each row, but they are separated with consistent vertical spacing (16–24px) and subtle dividers to avoid clutter. The order summary column is compact but not cramped, with clear grouping for subtotal, taxes, total, and primary CTA. Any explanatory text (e.g., "You’re almost ready to dive in!" or fine print) uses larger line height and comfortable margins to echo the mockup’s editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow remains classic and familiar: the eye starts top-left on the logo, moves across the navigation, then drops into the colorful hero strip. Within the hero, the eye scans left‑to‑right across the four promo cards. From there, it naturally flows down to the main cart heading and supporting text centered at the top of the cart content area, then down into the left column of cart items, scanning each row vertically. The order summary on the right becomes a strong secondary focal point due to its contrasting background and prominent checkout button. The floating chat button sits at the lower-left edge of the viewport, not competing with the primary checkout button but always within peripheral vision as a support option.

layout.overall_description: The cart page opens with a bright, clean header experience nearly identical to the rest of the site: a slim utility bar runs full‑width at the top, followed by a white header band containing the aquatic center logo on the left and a horizontal navigation menu spanning across the top-right to top-center area. Below this, a colorful, full‑width hero band showcases four promotional cards arranged in a single horizontal row, each card sitting in its own quarter of the grid, echoing event‑poster styling. This hero band stretches almost edge‑to‑edge but is visually anchored by the central content column. Under the hero, the page transitions into the cart section inside a centered max‑width container with generous left and right margins. At the top of this container is a clear cart title and a friendly introductory line, centered or slightly left-aligned, with wide line spacing that gives an editorial, welcoming feel. Directly below, the content splits into a two-column layout: on the left, a wider column lists the cart items in stacked rows; on the right, a narrower column presents the order summary card with totals and the primary checkout button. The left cart column uses subtle horizontal dividers, clear spacing between rows, and aligned pricing to keep denser information readable. The right column stands out slightly with a soft background tint or card treatment and a strong call-to-action button near the bottom. Below both columns, the layout unifies again into full‑width informational blocks—such as refund policy, scheduling notes, or a reassurance message about secure checkout—each given generous vertical separation to maintain lightness. Throughout, white background and consistent 8–24px spacing around interactive elements uphold readability and a family‑friendly tone. A floating circular or pill-shaped chat/help button sits fixed near the bottom-left corner of the viewport, slightly inset from the edges, using a bright accent color so it’s noticeable but not intrusive as users review items and move toward checkout.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Finalize purchase of passes, programs, and memberships by reviewing the order summary, entering purchaser details, selecting a payment method, and submitting the order.

core_features: Load a checkout-ready summary of the current cart using getCheckoutSummary, including all items and total amounts.
Display an itemized order summary (programs, passes, memberships) with quantities, unit prices, and total price per item.
Display calculated subtotal, tax, and total amount from getCheckoutSummary.
Show a compact cart status indicator in the header using getCartSummary (item count and subtotal) so users know what they are checking out.
Collect purchaser contact information (name, email, optional phone) in a checkout form.
Allow the user to select a payment method (e.g., credit/debit card, cash, check, online wallet) and enter high-level payment details as needed for the selected method.
Validate required purchaser fields and payment method selection before allowing submission.
Submit checkout via submitCheckout with purchaserName, purchaserEmail, purchaserPhone, and paymentMethod.
On successful submission, display an order confirmation view using the Order and OrderItem data returned by submitCheckout.
Offer navigation from the confirmation view to index.html (Back to Home) and my_schedule.html (View My Schedule).
Handle empty cart or missing checkout data gracefully by informing the user and offering navigation back to cart.html or the home page.
Handle API errors (for getCheckoutSummary, getCartSummary, submitCheckout) with clear error messages and retry options.
user_workflows: Standard checkout from cart: (1) User clicks 'Checkout' on cart.html and is navigated to checkout.html (no special parameters required). (2) On page load, checkout.html calls getCheckoutSummary to load cart items and totals, and getCartSummary for the header. (3) User reviews the order summary. If they need to edit items, they click 'Edit Cart' which navigates back to cart.html. (4) User fills in purchaser name, email, and optional phone. (5) User selects a payment method and, if applicable, enters payment details in the payment section. (6) User clicks 'Place Order' (form submit). (7) The page validates required fields; if valid, it calls submitCheckout with purchaserName, purchaserEmail, purchaserPhone, and paymentMethod. (8) On success, the page switches to a confirmation view using the returned order and orderItems, and updates the URL to checkout.html?step=confirmation&orderId={order.id}. (9) User can then click 'Back to Home' (navigates to index.html) or 'View My Schedule' (navigates to my_schedule.html).

Direct access / resume checkout via header cart icon: (1) User clicks the cart icon in the header from any page and chooses 'Checkout' which navigates to checkout.html, optionally with a parameter such as checkout.html?from=header. (2) Page loads checkout summary via getCheckoutSummary and displays existing cart items. (3) User follows the same steps as in the standard checkout workflow for entering details and submitting.

Handling empty or invalid cart at checkout: (1) User navigates to checkout.html. (2) getCheckoutSummary is called; if it returns a valid response but with zero items, the page displays an 'Empty checkout' state with explanation that there are no items to purchase. (3) A link/button is presented to navigate to cart.html or index.html to add items. (4) The purchaser form and payment sections are disabled or hidden when there are no items to check out.

Retry after failed submission: (1) User attempts to submit payment via 'Place Order'. (2) submitCheckout returns success=false and a message. (3) Page displays the error message above the form and keeps the user on the checkout step (step=checkout). (4) User can adjust information as needed and click 'Place Order' again. (5) On a successful retry, the normal confirmation workflow proceeds.
interactions: On page load, trigger getCheckoutSummary to populate the main order summary and totals; in parallel, trigger getCartSummary to populate the header cart indicator.
If getCheckoutSummary fails (e.g., network error or unexpected response), show an inline error message and provide a 'Retry' button that calls getCheckoutSummary again.
In the order summary, clicking 'Edit Cart' navigates to cart.html so the user can modify items and then return to checkout.
In the purchaser information form, changes to input fields immediately update internal form state and clear related validation messages when corrected.

In the payment method section, selecting a payment method updates visible fields (e.g., showing card-specific fields when 'credit_debit_card' is selected). This change updates internal state used when calling submitCheckout.

Clicking 'Place Order' triggers client-side validation: required purchaser fields must be non-empty, email must be in a valid format, a payment method must be selected, and there must be at least one item in the checkout summary. Validation errors are shown inline and block the submitCheckout call.

On successful submitCheckout, the page stores the returned order and orderItems in in-memory state for immediate display, updates the URL to include step=confirmation and orderId, and switches the visible view from the checkout form to the confirmation panel.
On submitCheckout failure (success=false), the page displays the returned message near the form, does not change the URL step or view, and allows the user to attempt submission again.

On the confirmation view, clicking 'Back to Home' navigates to index.html without additional parameters, and clicking 'View My Schedule' navigates to my_schedule.html (e.g., my_schedule.html?from=checkout&orderId={order.id}) so the schedule page can optionally highlight newly added items.

If the user navigates directly to checkout.html?step=confirmation&orderId={someId} without having just completed a checkout, the page cannot refetch the order via any interface; in this case, it shows a generic confirmation message (no item details) indicating that, if an order was recently placed, details were also emailed, and provides navigation back to index.html and my_schedule.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The checkout.html page uses URL parameters to track which logical view should be shown, while all data is loaded via the assigned interfaces. The primary parameter is 'step'. When 'step' is omitted or not equal to 'confirmation', the page operates in 'checkout' mode: it calls getCheckoutSummary (and optionally getCartSummary for the header), displays the order summary and checkout form, and enables the 'Place Order' action. When submitCheckout returns success=true, the page updates the URL using the History API to checkout.html?step=confirmation&orderId={order.id}. In this 'confirmation' step, the page shows a confirmation view using the in-memory order and orderItems received from submitCheckout. If the page is reloaded or accessed directly with step=confirmation, it reads the 'orderId' parameter but, because there is no interface here to load an order by id, it displays a generic confirmation state that does not rely on any static or previously cached data. An optional 'from' parameter may be used when entering checkout.html (e.g., checkout.html?from=header or checkout.html?from=cart) to adjust UI hints such as which 'Back' link to prefer, but it does not change which interfaces are called. No state is persisted via localStorage; all durable data about the order is derived from the interfaces on each page load, and transient UI state (form fields, validation, in-memory order after submission) is held only in runtime memory until the page is unloaded.

components.functionality: Initializes the checkout page by determining the current step from URL parameters, calling getCheckoutSummary to retrieve the cart, items, and totals for the main checkout view, and calling getCartSummary to populate the header cart indicator. It manages loading and error states for these calls.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: parse URL parameters (step, orderId, from) and decide whether to render checkout or confirmation state
onPageLoad: if step is not 'confirmation', call getCheckoutSummary and bind response.cart, response.items, response.subtotal, response.taxAmount, response.totalAmount into shared checkout state
onPageLoad: call getCartSummary and bind response.cart, response.items, response.subtotal, response.itemCount into shared header state
onRetryLoadCheckoutSummaryClick: re-call getCheckoutSummary if the initial attempt failed

components.functionality: Displays a compact summary of the current cart in the page header, such as total item count and subtotal, using data from getCartSummary. Provides a direct navigation link to cart.html for editing items before checkout.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: when checkout-data-loader has getCartSummary results, render itemCount and subtotal
onClickCartIcon: navigate to cart.html to allow item edits (does not modify cart via this page)

components.functionality: Shows a detailed list of all items included in the checkout, grouped or simply listed based on CartItem data from getCheckoutSummary, including name, quantity, unit price, and total price per item. Also shows the subtotal, tax amount, and total amount. Provides an 'Edit Cart' link to allow users to change items on cart.html.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: when checkout-data-loader receives getCheckoutSummary results, render list of items and totals
onClickEditCart: navigate to cart.html (optionally retaining a return parameter such as cart.html?returnTo=checkout)
onEmptyItemsDetected: display an 'empty checkout' message and hide or disable the checkout form and payment components

components.functionality: Collects purchaser contact information required to complete checkout, including purchaser name (required), email (required), and phone (optional). Validates the fields and exposes their values to the checkout submission logic.
event_handlers: onInputChangeName: update in-memory purchaserName state and clear related validation errors
onInputChangeEmail: update in-memory purchaserEmail state and clear related validation errors
onValidate: ensure purchaserName and purchaserEmail are non-empty and purchaserEmail is syntactically valid; return validation result to the checkout-submit-actions component
onResetAfterSuccess: when a successful order is submitted, clear form fields and validation state (though the user is typically moved to the confirmation view)

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose a payment method corresponding to the Order.payment_method enum (credit_debit_card, cash, check, online_wallet) and collects any high-level payment details needed for the selected method. The selected payment method value is used in the submitCheckout call.
event_handlers: onPaymentMethodChange: update in-memory paymentMethod state and adjust which payment detail fields are shown (e.g., show card fields only when credit_debit_card is selected)

onPaymentDetailsChange: update internal state for any payment details fields (such as masked card data) without sending them to submitCheckout unless they are required by an external processor integrated outside these interfaces
onValidate: ensure that a payment method is selected; optionally ensure required detail fields for that method are present; return validation results to checkout-submit-actions

components.functionality: Coordinates validation of the purchaser-contact-form and payment-method-section, ensures there are items in the order summary, and then calls submitCheckout. It manages submitting/loading state, handles success or error responses, updates URL parameters for the confirmation step, and passes the resulting order data to the confirmation panel.
event_handlers: onClickPlaceOrder: trigger validation on purchaser-contact-form and payment-method-section; verify that the order-summary-panel indicates there is at least one item; if all validations pass, call submitCheckout with the current purchaserName, purchaserEmail, purchaserPhone, and paymentMethod

onSubmitCheckoutSuccess: when submitCheckout returns success=true, store the returned order and orderItems in in-memory state, update the URL to checkout.html?step=confirmation&orderId={order.id} using the History API, and signal the page to switch from the checkout view to the confirmation view
onSubmitCheckoutFailure: when submitCheckout returns success=false, display response.message above the action bar or form, keep the user in the checkout step, and allow resubmission
onDisableIfEmptyCart: if order-summary-panel reports zero items (via shared state), disable the 'Place Order' button and show a hint to add items first

components.functionality: Displays a confirmation view after a successful checkout, summarizing the completed order using the Order and OrderItem data returned from submitCheckout. Shows key details such as order id, purchaser name and email, total amount, and a list of purchased items, and provides navigation to Home and My Schedule.
event_handlers: onShowForSuccess: when checkout-submit-actions signals a successful order, render the confirmation view using in-memory order and orderItems data

onPageLoadWithStepConfirmation: if URL step=confirmation but there is no in-memory order data (for example, after a full page reload), render a generic confirmation message that does not depend on order details and offer navigation options; do not attempt to use any static or assumed data
onClickBackToHome: navigate to index.html as defined in outgoing_connections
onClickViewMySchedule: navigate to my_schedule.html, optionally including a parameter like my_schedule.html?from=checkout&orderId={order.id} when order data is available

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s top‑anchored rhythm: a thin utility bar (global), then the main header band with logo/nav and the cart indicator on the right, followed by a single, dominant full‑width checkout band that plays the role of the hero. Inside this checkout band, content is split horizontally using the 12‑column grid: a wider left column (≈8/12) for the purchaser and payment forms and a narrower right column (≈4/12) for the order summary panel.
