In the completed state, the forms band is replaced by a full‑width, center‑constrained confirmation panel that mimics the large editorial intro section from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s top‑anchored rhythm: a thin utility bar (global), then the main header band with logo/nav and the cart indicator on the right, followed by a single, dominant full‑width checkout band that plays the role of the hero. Inside this checkout band, content is split horizontally using the 12‑column grid: a wider left column (≈8/12) for the purchaser and payment forms and a narrower right column (≈4/12) for the order summary panel. In the completed state, the forms band is replaced by a full‑width, center‑constrained confirmation panel that mimics the large editorial intro section from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The combined utility bar and header occupy a shallow slice at the top (~10–15% of the viewport height), leaving most above‑the‑fold space to the main checkout band (~50–60% of the viewport). Within that band, the left form column uses roughly two‑thirds of the available width, and the right order summary column uses about one‑third. Vertical gaps between major blocks (header → checkout band → confirmation) follow the 32–48px section spacing. Internally, forms and panels use 16–24px padding, with extra top padding (32–48px) under the header to create a clear breathing zone before the checkout content starts.
layout_strategies.content_density: The structural layout is low‑to‑medium density: broad white (or very light) background, clear separation between sections, and relaxed line height for headings and explanatory copy. The order summary panel is visually richer (colored header stripe, badges, price emphasis), echoing the mockup’s poster‑like hero cards, but it remains isolated in the right column so the page doesn’t feel cluttered. Form fields are stacked in a single column with generous vertical spacing (12–16px between fields, 24–32px between logical groups) to keep the checkout approachable for families and casual users.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a classic F‑pattern: logo at top‑left → nav → header cart indicator at top‑right, then drops into the main checkout band. Within the band, attention first lands on the left column (purchaser contact form heading), then cross‑checks the right‑side order summary. From there, the gaze moves downward through the purchaser form, then the payment method section, ending on the submit actions bar. On successful checkout, the visual focus shifts to a large, centered confirmation message in a full‑width band, mirroring the mockup’s big intro text section and creating a clear sense of completion.

layout.overall_description: The checkout page opens with the familiar aquatic center chrome: a slim utility bar at the very top, then a bright, airy header with logo on the left, navigation across the middle, and a compact cart indicator snugged to the right edge of the main header container. Immediately beneath, a full‑width checkout band spans the screen with a white or softly tinted background. Within this band, content is constrained to a centered max‑width container aligned to a 12‑column grid. The left two‑thirds form a clean, stacked flow of purchaser details and payment options, each housed in lightly outlined or subtly shaded panels with generous 24px internal padding. On the right, a vertically oriented order summary panel occupies about one‑third of the width, styled more like a colorful promo card with a vivid heading area and strong price hierarchy, and kept aligned to the top of the form column. As users scroll, the summary remains visible near the top of the viewport, while the left column continues down into payment details and a clear, bold action bar at the bottom. When the transaction completes, the busy checkout band gives way to a calmer, full‑width confirmation section: a large, centered message with wide side margins and relaxed line spacing that invites users to read and exhale, echoing the editorial intro feel from the mockup while still fitting into the same top‑to‑bottom, banded page structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a full‑viewport veil whenever checkout data is loading or being submitted. It covers the entire visible page area from the very top edge of the browser window down to the bottom, spanning 100% width and height. The overlay slightly tints or softens the underlying page (e.g., a semi‑opaque white or very light aquatic blue at around 70–85% opacity) so that the header, forms, and summary are still faintly visible beneath but clearly inactive. At the center of the viewport, a spinner or progress indicator is positioned both horizontally and vertically centered, optionally accompanied by a short status text line below it. The overlay respects the existing grid only in that it matches the page’s max width and alignment; visually it sits above everything else, unifying the surface into a single loading state with ample empty space around the spinner for a calm, focused feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay: positioned fixed to the viewport, anchored to top/left (0,0) and extending to right/bottom, with a high z-index so it floats above the header, forms, and confirmation panels. It scrolls with the viewport content behind it locked or visually inactive until loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header cart indicator lives inside the main header band, aligned to the far right of the centered header container. Horizontally, it sits in line with the primary navigation items, taking up a relatively small footprint (roughly the width of a single nav item or a compact pill containing an icon, item count, and total). Vertically, it is centered within the header’s height, with about 8–12px of vertical padding inside the clickable area to match the airy nav spacing. There is a comfortable 16–24px margin between the cart indicator’s right edge and the outer edge of the header container, echoing the mockup’s spacious edge gutters. The bright accent color used for the cart icon or badge makes it pop subtly against the cleaner header background, but its compact size keeps it from overpowering the logo or navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header band. It occupies a fixed spot at the top‑right of the header container. If the site’s global header is sticky, the indicator naturally remains visible as part of that header when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary panel is anchored in the main checkout band’s right column. Within the centered max‑width container, it begins at the same top baseline as the purchaser contact form on the left, creating a balanced top alignment. It spans approximately 4 of the 12 grid columns (roughly one‑third of the content width), with 24–32px of horizontal space separating it from the left form column. The panel itself is card‑like: a vertical rectangle with 16–24px internal padding on all sides, a subtle shadow or border to lift it from the background, and a slightly more saturated or colorful header strip at the top (mirroring the hero’s poster feel). Inside, the content is denser but structured: the pass/program list near the top, totals and fees grouped in the middle, and a compact summary or reassurance text near the bottom, each separated by 8–16px vertical spacing. The card does not stretch full height; it size itself to its contents, but on taller viewports it typically fills about half the viewport height, leaving light space beneath before the next section of the page begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the checkout band on larger screens: positioned so that once the user scrolls past the top of the checkout band, the panel remains pinned near the top‑right of the viewport (e.g., with a 24–32px offset below the header) until the end of the main checkout content is reached. On smaller viewports, it behaves like a normal block stacked above or below the forms as necessary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchaser contact form occupies the upper portion of the main checkout band’s left column. Inside the centered container, it starts around 32–48px below the bottom edge of the header band, giving a clear visual break before the transactional content. It spans approximately 8 of the 12 grid columns, starting from the left grid edge, and is separated from the right order summary panel by a 24–32px gutter. The form is structured as a vertically stacked, single‑column layout: a prominent section title at the top (aligned left, large and friendly), followed by a short line of helper text, then groups of input fields. Each field or logical group has 12–16px vertical spacing, while larger groupings (e.g., contact details vs. address) are separated by 24–32px. The entire form area is wrapped in either a light panel (soft border and 16–24px padding) or a clear grouping with generous white space at the top and bottom, maintaining the low‑density, family‑friendly feel. The left and right edges of the form align with the grid to keep the page visually structured.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the main checkout band. It scrolls naturally with the page, remaining the first major content block beneath the header until replaced or hidden after a successful checkout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method section sits directly beneath the purchaser contact form in the same left column, preserving the linear downward flow. It begins after a clear vertical gap of about 32px from the bottom of the contact form panel, visually marking it as the next major step. The section spans the same width (≈8/12 columns) and maintains consistent left/right alignment with the contact form, keeping the column edge clean. Inside, payment options (e.g., credit card, digital wallet) are presented as selectable cards or radio rows stacked vertically, each with 12–16px vertical padding and at least 16px between options. When a method is selected, its detailed fields (e.g., card number, expiry) appear directly below it but still within the section’s boundaries, with 16–24px padding around the details. The density is slightly higher than the contact form due to required fields, but the generous spacing and grouping keep it visually approachable. A concise, reassuring line about payment security might sit at the bottom with extra breathing space around it to soften the transactional feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block flowing directly after the purchaser contact form within the left column. It adds to the vertical scroll height of the main checkout band and moves naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout submit actions form the visual anchor at the bottom of the main checkout content in the left column. They appear immediately below the payment method section, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap that marks the transition from data entry to final action. Horizontally, the action bar aligns with the same 8‑column width as the forms, so its left edge lines up with the form labels and its right edge with the form’s right boundary. The main call‑to‑action button (e.g., “Complete purchase”) is placed towards the right side of this area, sized generously (typical 16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding) and using a bright, brand‑accent color, making it the most visually prominent element in this bar. Secondary actions (e.g., “Back” or “Cancel”) appear to the left of or below the primary button with reduced emphasis. There is comfortable white space above and below the buttons, with the bottom padding to the following content (or page edge) set around 32–48px, so the section doesn’t feel cramped. The alignment and spacing echo the mockup’s preference for strong, clear end‑of‑section anchors.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily inline at the bottom of the left column. On smaller screens, it may behave as a sticky bottom bar (positioned fixed to the bottom of the viewport with full‑width) to keep the primary action always reachable, but on desktop it remains part of the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout confirmation panel replaces the main checkout band’s left‑column form content after a successful submission, becoming the new focal section beneath the header. It is presented as a full‑width band similar in height and presence to the original hero/intro section from the mockup. Within this band, content is centered in the same max‑width container but no longer split into left/right columns; instead, the confirmation message is vertically stacked and horizontally centered across approximately 6–8 of the 12 grid columns, leaving generous side margins. The top of the panel sits about 32–48px below the header, and the main heading text is large and welcoming, with relaxed line height and 16–24px spacing to any supporting paragraph text. Below, key confirmation details (order number, summary, next steps) are grouped with 16–24px vertical spacing, maintaining a low‑density, editorial feel. The overall height of this panel typically fills most of the viewport on desktop, with 48–64px padding above the heading and below the last line of content so the section feels like a complete, self‑contained conclusion to the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full‑width section that either appears in place of and hides the purchaser, payment, and actions content or is inserted immediately above them while they collapse or move offscreen. It scrolls naturally with the page but is intended to be the first major element visible after the header in the post‑checkout state.

name: About
title: About
description: Informational page that loads and displays dynamic mission, history, facilities, hours, location, and amenities for the aquatic center, and routes users to Memberships, Pool Schedule, and Programs pages.

core_features: Fetch and display dynamic About content (mission, history, facilities overview, general hours, location, amenities) via getAboutContent.
Allow users to switch between About sections (mission, history, facilities, hours/location) using in-page navigation linked to a URL section parameter.
Display a structured list of dynamic amenities returned by getAboutContent.
Show general hours and location information in a dedicated panel using data from getAboutContent.
Provide contextual navigation links to Memberships, Pool Schedule, and Programs pages, optionally preserving the current About section in URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Read general information about the aquatic center
1. User navigates to about.html (from footer, sitemap, or direct URL).
2. about-content-loader initializes and calls getAboutContent with no parameters.
3. While waiting, a loading state is shown by components that depend on AboutContent.
4. On success, AboutContent (mission, history, facilitiesOverview, generalHours, locationDescription, amenities[]) is stored in page state.
5. section-router reads the section URL parameter; if present and valid, it sets the active section; otherwise, it defaults to a predefined section in internal logic (e.g., mission) without hardcoding any content.
6. about-section-display shows the active section’s text using fields from AboutContent.
7. The user can click different section navigation items; section-router updates the section URL parameter and the visible content without re-calling getAboutContent.

Workflow 2: Deep-link into a specific About section via URL
1. User opens a link such as about.html?section=history.
2. section-router reads section=history from the URL before or during initialization.
3. about-content-loader calls getAboutContent and populates AboutContent in state.
4. Once data is loaded, about-section-display shows the history content using AboutContent.history.
5. If the user changes the section via navigation, section-router updates the section parameter (e.g., to section=facilities) and updates the displayed content accordingly.
6. If the user later reloads the page, the same section parameter keeps the user on the selected section.

Workflow 3: Review amenities and navigate to key functional pages
1. User navigates to the Facilities or Amenities section via section navigation or URL parameter (e.g., section=facilities).
2. amenities-list reads AboutContent.amenities and renders each amenity item.
3. hours-location-panel shows AboutContent.generalHours and AboutContent.locationDescription alongside amenities if relevant to the current section.
4. The user decides to get started and clicks one of the quick links in quick-links-panel:
   - 'View Membership Options' → navigates to memberships.html with optional context parameters (e.g., ?from=about&section=currentSection).
   - 'View Pool Schedule' → navigates to schedule.html with optional context parameters.
   - 'Explore Programs & Classes' → navigates to programs.html with optional context parameters.
5. The browser loads the target page; About page does not perform any additional API calls when navigating away.

Workflow 4: Handle loading or error and retry
1. User navigates to about.html.
2. about-content-loader calls getAboutContent and sets a loading flag.
3. If getAboutContent fails, about-content-loader sets an error state.
4. Dependent components (about-section-display, amenities-list, hours-location-panel) show an error or fallback state controlled by the loader.
5. User clicks a 'Retry' control within about-content-loader.
6. about-content-loader calls getAboutContent again; on success, it clears the error state and populates AboutContent, and all dependent components render with the new data.
interactions: Click section navigation item: Updates the section URL parameter to the selected section key (e.g., mission, history, facilities, hours) via section-router, updates the active state of navigation, and instructs about-section-display to show the corresponding AboutContent field.

Browser back/forward navigation: When URL changes due to history navigation, section-router listens to URL changes (e.g., popstate) and updates the active section and visible content without reloading the page data.

Click 'View Membership Options' link: Navigates the browser to memberships.html, optionally appending context parameters such as from=about and section=currentSection so the destination page can adjust its state if needed.
Click 'View Pool Schedule' link: Navigates to schedule.html with optional context parameters (e.g., from=about&section=currentSection).
Click 'Explore Programs & Classes' link: Navigates to programs.html with optional context parameters (e.g., from=about&section=currentSection).
Retry loading About content: When in an error state, clicking the retry control triggers getAboutContent again in about-content-loader and updates all dependent components upon success.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page state is controlled using URL parameters and the result of getAboutContent. Primary URL parameter: section (string). Supported values correspond to the main About sections, e.g., mission, history, facilities, hours. On initial load:
- about-content-loader calls getAboutContent, which returns an object AboutContent with mission, history, facilitiesOverview, generalHours, locationDescription, amenities[].
- section-router reads window.location.search for section.
  - If section matches a known section key, that section becomes active.
  - If section is missing or invalid, the component uses an internal default section key but does not hardcode any textual data; it only changes which AboutContent field is displayed.
When the user clicks a section navigation item:
- section-router updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., about.html?section=history) via pushState or by navigating to the new URL.
- No new call to getAboutContent is made; components re-render using the existing AboutContent and the new section key.
When the user uses browser back/forward:
- section-router listens for URL changes, re-parses the section parameter, and updates the active section accordingly.
Outgoing navigation can optionally include context parameters without affecting About page functionality:
- memberships.html?from=about&section=<currentSection>
- schedule.html?from=about&section=<currentSection>
- programs.html?from=about&section=<currentSection>
These parameters are generated by quick-links-panel but are not consumed on the About page itself.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getAboutContent with no parameters, manages loading and error state, and exposes the resulting AboutContent object (mission, history, facilitiesOverview, generalHours, locationDescription, amenities[]) to child components.

Reads and writes the section URL parameter to control which About section is active. On load, it determines the initial active section from the URL and, on user interaction, updates the URL and notifies display components of the new section.

Renders interactive controls (e.g., tabs or links) representing available About sections (such as mission, history, facilities, hours). When a user selects a section, it triggers section-router to update the section URL parameter and active section state.

Displays the textual content for the currently active section based on the active section key from section-router. It binds to fields on AboutContent (mission, history, facilitiesOverview, generalHours, locationDescription) and renders only the field corresponding to the active section.

Displays a dynamic list of amenities using the AboutContent.amenities array. Each amenity item is rendered from the array without hardcoded labels or counts. The component can be shown primarily when the facilities section is active, based on section-router state.

Shows the generalHours and locationDescription fields from AboutContent, regardless of which section is active or, optionally, when the hours section is active. It can be displayed alongside other section content so users always see basic operating hours and location details.

Provides three navigation actions: link to memberships.html, link to schedule.html, and link to programs.html. When generating each link, it reads the current active section from section-router and may append context parameters (e.g., from=about, section=currentSection) to the destination URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-anchored, banded structure as the mockup: global utility bar + header + full-width hero band remain unchanged above. The About page-specific components live in the main content band directly below the hero. Inside this band, content is arranged in a centered, max-width container that uses an implicit 12-column grid. The About area starts with any page title/intro (outside this spec), then immediately presents the section-navigation-tabs as a horizontal control. Below the tabs, the layout splits into a main content column on the left (about-section-display and any embedded amenities-list) and a narrower supporting column on the right (hours-location-panel above quick-links-panel). The about-content-loader sits over this whole About content region when active, while the section-router operates behind the scenes to switch what the about-section-display shows.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-anchored, banded structure as the mockup: global utility bar + header + full-width hero band remain unchanged above. The About page-specific components live in the main content band directly below the hero. Inside this band, content is arranged in a centered, max-width container that uses an implicit 12-column grid. The About area starts with any page title/intro (outside this spec), then immediately presents the section-navigation-tabs as a horizontal control. Below the tabs, the layout splits into a main content column on the left (about-section-display and any embedded amenities-list) and a narrower supporting column on the right (hours-location-panel above quick-links-panel). The about-content-loader sits over this whole About content region when active, while the section-router operates behind the scenes to switch what the about-section-display shows.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Consistent with the mockup, the header and hero occupy the upper 50–60% of the initial viewport together. The About content band occupies the remaining 40–50% of the first viewport and then continues down via scroll. Within the About band, the centered container spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width up to a max width (~1200px). Inside that container, the main content column occupies about 8 of 12 grid columns (roughly two-thirds of the width), while the supporting right-hand column (hours-location-panel and quick-links-panel) occupies the remaining 4 of 12 columns (roughly one-third). Vertical spacing between major chunks (tabs row, main content, side panels as separate blocks) uses the 32–48px section margin pattern; internal padding of cards/panels uses 16–24px. The amenities-list, when present, uses a denser grid but still respects the same column structure within the main content area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density remains low-to-medium, echoing the mockup. The main typography in about-section-display uses large, readable text with generous line-height and comfortable margins, prioritizing an editorial, welcoming feel. The section-navigation-tabs are compact but not cramped, with enough padding and spacing between tabs to feel tappable and friendly. The amenities-list and quick-links-panel are visually denser: multiple items in a grid or stacked list, similar to the more graphic hero cards, but still contained within their own bounded regions and separated by 24–32px of vertical whitespace so the page never feels overloaded. The hours-location-panel balances readable text blocks with concise, scannable formatting.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows the site-wide F-pattern: logo and nav in the header, then across the hero band’s colorful promotions. As the user scrolls into the About band, the eye lands first on the left-aligned section-navigation-tabs near the top of the centered container, then immediately drops into the about-section-display main text. From there, the gaze moves horizontally to the right column where the hours-location-panel sits high in the viewport, making practical information easy to spot early. Below that, the quick-links-panel offers clear pathways to Memberships, Pool Schedule, and Programs. When in sections like Amenities, the eye flows from the introductory text at the top of about-section-display down into the more visually dense amenities-list grid. The about-content-loader briefly dominates attention when active by dimming and overlaying this region, but otherwise recedes entirely. The flow remains linear top-to-bottom with strong left-column narrative and right-column utility, mirroring the mockup’s structured yet energetic feel.

layout.overall_description: Below the bright, full-width hero band, the About page content sits in a wide, centered container on a clean white background, matching the site’s family-friendly, energetic tone without adding clutter. At the very top of this About band, a horizontal row of colorful yet clean section tabs hugs the left side, signaling that the content can switch between Mission, History, Facilities, Hours & Location, and Amenities. Immediately beneath these tabs, a broad left column stretches across about two-thirds of the available width, carrying large, easy-to-read headings and paragraphs that tell the center’s story; it feels spacious, with generous line-height and 32–48px gaps between sections. To the right, a narrower column—about one-third of the width—starts at nearly the same vertical position, holding a compact, card-like hours and location panel near the top so that essential info is always in peripheral view. Just below that, a second card offers quick navigation links to Memberships, the Pool Schedule, and Programs, styled like friendly, high-contrast call-to-action buttons. In sections that feature amenities, the left column expands into a grid of colorful amenity tiles beneath the introductory copy, echoing the hero’s energetic promotional feel while still being clearly partitioned. When content is loading or switching sections, a light, semi-opaque overlay with a centered spinner temporarily covers both columns, then fades away to reveal the refreshed layout. The overall effect is a structured, modern, scrollable page where narrative content leads on the left, practical information and shortcuts stay anchored on the right, and everything is paced with generous whitespace in line with the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader appears only during data fetch or section switching and is anchored to the About content band directly below the full-width hero. It spans the full width of the centered About container (roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, up to the site’s max content width) and extends vertically to cover both the main left content column and the right-hand side panels. Visually, it functions as a semi-opaque white or very light-tinted overlay that starts just below the base of the hero band and stretches down to at least the bottom edge of the currently visible About content. The loader’s focal element—a spinner or friendly loading illustration—is centered both horizontally and vertically within this overlay, keeping it immediately visible without feeling aggressive. The overlay respects the page’s generous spacing: there is still a visible margin to the browser edges, so the user recognizes it as part of the content area, not a full-screen takeover. When inactive, this component has no visible presence and does not consume layout space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative to the About content container, using absolute or fixed-in-container positioning. When active, it sits on top of the section-navigation-tabs, about-section-display, amenities-list (if present), hours-location-panel, and quick-links-panel, intercepting interactions until loading completes. It scrolls with the page if the user has scrolled, but typically appears within the current viewport region of the About band; once loading finishes, it fades out and relinquishes all space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-router operates as an invisible controller rather than a visible UI element. It lives conceptually at the top of the About content band, immediately tied to the state of the section-navigation-tabs and the about-section-display. It does not occupy any dedicated visual area or alter the spatial arrangement; instead, it determines which content block is shown inside the main left column and when to trigger the about-content-loader overlay. From a layout perspective, it can be thought of as sitting logically between the tabs row and the content viewer, but without any rendered height or width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height component. It does not render any box, overlay, or fixed element. Its behavior is purely stateful, orchestrating which section is active and which child components (like amenities-list, hours-location-panel variations) appear or hide within their designated layout zones.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation-tabs form a horizontal strip of tabbed buttons placed at the very top of the About content area, directly under the hero band’s bottom edge and any About page heading. They sit inside the centered main container, aligned to the left edge of that container but not flush with the browser edges, maintaining the site’s comfortable gutters. The tabs row spans roughly 100% of the container’s width (about 80–90% of the viewport, up to the max content width), but the individual tabs typically occupy only the left two-thirds of this row, leaving some breathing room to the right. Each tab is a pill or rounded-rectangle button with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, spaced 8–16px apart horizontally, following the 8px spacing system. The entire tabs strip is given 24–32px of vertical margin below the hero and 24–32px of spacing above the start of the about-section-display content, making it a clear, distinct control band without feeling cramped. On narrower screens, the tabs can wrap to a second line while remaining left-aligned within the same horizontal zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as the user moves down the About content. It remains within the main content flow (not sticky at the top of the viewport), always positioned immediately above the about-section-display. Its width and alignment are tied to the main content container, adjusting responsively but keeping a left-aligned, linear tab layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-display occupies the primary left column of the About band, positioned directly beneath the section-navigation-tabs. It starts aligned with the left edge of the centered container and spans approximately 8 out of the 12 grid columns, or about two-thirds of the container width. There is a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the tabs and the top of this content block, allowing the tabs and content to feel related but distinct. The top of the about-section-display typically contains a prominent section heading and possibly a short lead paragraph, set in large, readable type with generous line-height and 24–32px of spacing to the subsequent body text, reflecting the editorial feel from the mockup’s intro section. Body content runs down the left column with a relaxed line length (ideal around 60–80 characters per line), and paragraphs are separated by 16–24px. When sections like Mission or History are active, this column may be mostly narrative text with occasional imagery; when sections like Facilities or Amenities are active, the top portion remains text-heavy, while the lower portion of this same column is used to host the amenities-list grid or other structured content. The right edge of this block leaves about 24px of gutter before the right-hand column begins, visually distinguishing main narrative content from utility panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable as part of the main content flow. It forms the dominant reading column from top to bottom of the About content. On smaller viewports, it expands to full width (all 12 columns), and any right-column content (hours-location-panel and quick-links-panel) collapses below it; however, the core behavior is that it remains the first and main content area encountered after the tabs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amenities-list is a structured list or grid that appears within the about-section-display’s left column when the relevant section (e.g., Amenities or Facilities) is active. Spatially, it is anchored below the introductory copy of that section. After a 24–32px vertical gap from the last paragraph of narrative text, the amenities-list begins and extends across the full width of the main content column (the 8/12 left-column area). Amenities are presented as a visually denser grid of cards or tiles, arranged typically in 2–3 columns depending on screen width: on wider desktops, each amenity tile might occupy 4 of the 12 subcolumns within the main column (a three-across grid), while on moderate widths it may drop to two-across. Each tile has 12–16px internal padding and is separated from its neighbors by 16–24px of horizontal and vertical gutters, respecting the 8px base unit. Icons or small illustrations plus short labels and brief descriptions give it a lively, poster-like feel that echoes the hero cards, but the grid is clearly contained within a rectangular content block that ends with 32–40px of whitespace before any following section elements. This pattern keeps the page feeling energetic yet controlled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully contained within the scrolling flow of the about-section-display. It does not float or stick; it simply occupies vertical space beneath the section’s text content. On smaller screens, it becomes a single-column list, maintaining the same vertical rhythm but collapsing the grid to conserve width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hours-location-panel resides in the right-hand supporting column of the About content band. It aligns horizontally with the top of the about-section-display: their top edges are nearly level, reinforcing the F-pattern where the user reads the left column then glances right for key info. This panel spans the full width of the right column, which is approximately 4 out of 12 grid columns (about one-third of the container). There is a 24px gutter between this panel’s left edge and the right edge of the main content column. The panel itself is card-like: it has a subtle background tint or border, 16–24px internal padding on all sides, and slightly rounded corners, making it feel like a friendly, important information box. Inside, hours and location details are organized into small, stacked blocks with compact labels and content (e.g., bold day headings with lighter detail lines), separated by 8–16px. The panel’s height is modest compared to the main content column, so it typically sits within the upper half of the viewport on initial scroll, making critical visiting information quickly scannable without overshadowing the narrative content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed in the right column of the grid, it scrolls with the page but may use a sticky behavior on larger screens: it can remain fixed within the right column’s visible area until the bottom of the About content band is reached, ensuring hours and location stay accessible as the user scrolls the left-column content. On smaller screens, it reflows beneath the about-section-display, becoming a full-width card that appears before the quick-links-panel in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-links-panel is positioned directly below the hours-location-panel within the same right-hand column. It shares the same width—about 4 of the 12 grid columns—and aligns with the container’s right edge. There is a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px between the bottom of the hours-location-panel and the top of the quick-links-panel, ensuring each card feels distinct. This panel looks like a compact navigation card, with 16–24px internal padding and a subtle background or border similar to the hours card, but the content inside is structured as a short vertical list of prominent links or button-like items (e.g., Memberships, Pool Schedule, Programs). Each link has 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and the items are stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing. The styling is slightly more vibrant—using the brand’s bright color accents—to visually promote these actions without stealing attention from the main hero or the left-column story. The overall panel height is moderate, making it feel like a quick action block rather than a full section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, scrolling with the page. It sits below the hours-location-panel and does not overlap or stick; both panels form a vertical stack in the right column. On smaller screens, after the layout collapses to a single column, the quick-links-panel appears below the hours-location-panel as a full-width block beneath the main left-column content, preserving their order and maintaining clear vertical separation.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Provides dynamic contact details for the aquatic center and a general inquiry form that routes questions about programs, rentals, memberships, passes, and lap lanes.

core_features: Load and display primary contact details (phone, email addresses, physical address, directions summary) using getContactInfo.
Provide a general contact form that submits questions via submitContactForm with topic, name, email, phone, and message.
Preselect the contact form topic based on the URL parameter `topic` when users arrive from other pages (e.g., programs, rentals, memberships, passes, lap lanes).
Offer self-service navigation links to related pages (e.g., Pool Schedule, Parties & Rentals, Passes, Memberships) and a dedicated link to policies.html for policies and FAQs.
Provide submission feedback (success or error) using the response from submitContactForm and handle basic validation states on the client.
user_workflows:
View contact details:
1. User navigates to contact.html (from header, footer, or another page).
2. On page initialization, the page calls getContactInfo with no parameters.
3. While data is loading, the contact-info area can show a loading state (non-functional placeholder, not hardcoded content).
4. Once getContactInfo returns, the page displays the mainPhone, a list of emailAddresses (label + email), the physicalAddress, and directionsSummary.
5. User can click on any email address (mailto link) or initiate a phone call on supported devices using mainPhone.

Submit an inquiry about a specific topic (with preselected topic):
1. User clicks a "Contact us about this" link from another page such as programs.html, rentals.html, memberships.html, passes.html, or lap_lanes.html.
2. That link targets contact.html with a `topic` URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?topic=programs).
3. On load, the Contact page reads the `topic` value from the URL query string.
4. If the value matches an allowed topic (programs, parties, memberships, passes, lap_lanes), the contact form topic field is initialized with that value; otherwise, a neutral default is used.
5. User reviews or changes the preselected topic as needed.
6. User fills in name, email, optional phone, and message fields in the form.
7. On form submission, the page validates required fields (at least topic, name, email, and message) on the client.
8. If validation passes, the page calls submitContactForm with the current topic, name, email, phone, and message.
9. While awaiting the response, the form is temporarily disabled or shows a submitting state.
10. When submitContactForm returns, if success is true, a success confirmation message is shown using the API’s message property; the form may be reset except for the topic selection. If success is false, an error message from the API’s message is shown and the form is re-enabled.
11. If the browser-level request fails (e.g., network error), the page shows a generic error state without hardcoding any backend content.

Self-service before contacting:
1. User arrives on contact.html (with or without a topic parameter).
2. Above or near the form, the page displays a set of text links to relevant self-service pages such as pool schedule, rentals/party information, passes, and memberships.
3. User may click a link if they suspect their question can be answered without submitting the form.
4. For additional policy-related questions, the user can click a "View policies & FAQs" link.
5. Clicking that link navigates to policies.html according to the outgoing_connections definition.
6. If the user returns from those pages or still needs help, they can use the contact form as described in the form workflow.
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- Trigger: Browser loads contact.html.
- Actions: Parse URL parameters (specifically `topic`), store the resolved topic value in page state, call getContactInfo, and initialize the contact form topic field with the resolved value.

Contact information display:
- Trigger: getContactInfo resolves successfully.
- Actions: Bind mainPhone, emailAddresses array, physicalAddress, and directionsSummary to the contact-info component fields. Enable any interactive links (e.g., click-to-call or mailto) based on these values.

Topic preselection via URL parameter:
- Trigger: Page reads URL parameter `topic` on load.
- Actions: If `topic` equals one of the known topics (programs, parties, memberships, passes, lap_lanes), set the contact form’s topic field to that value; otherwise, fall back to a neutral default topic selection. No additional API calls are made based on this parameter.

Manual topic change in the form:
- Trigger: User changes the topic field in the contact form (e.g., selects a different topic from a dropdown).
- Actions: Update the internal form state with the new topic. Optionally update the URL query string (using history API) to reflect the newly chosen topic (e.g., contact.html?topic=memberships) without reloading the page, ensuring state can be reconstructed if the page is reloaded or shared.

Form submission:
- Trigger: User clicks the submit button on the contact form or submits via keyboard.
- Actions: Perform client-side validation; if validation fails, show inline error states and do not call submitContactForm. If validation passes, call submitContactForm with the current topic, name, email, phone, and message values. Disable the submit button or mark the form as submitting to prevent duplicate submissions.

Form submission response handling:
- Trigger: submitContactForm returns a response object.
- Actions: If response.success is true, display response.message in a success feedback area, optionally clear the name, email, phone, and message fields while leaving topic unchanged. If response.success is false, display response.message in an error feedback area and re-enable the form so the user can correct and resubmit.

Policies & FAQs navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the "View policies & FAQs" link.
- Actions: Navigate to policies.html with no additional parameters, as defined in the outgoing_connections. No API call is made as part of this interaction.

Other self-service links:
- Trigger: User clicks a self-service link such as to pool schedules, rentals/parties, passes, or memberships.
- Actions: Navigate to the corresponding page (e.g., programs.html, rentals.html, memberships.html, passes.html, lap_lanes.html). Those pages, when offering a "Contact us" link back, can include the `topic` parameter to re-open contact.html with a preselected topic.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters and in-memory component state, without using localStorage.

URL parameter handling:
- The page reads the query parameter `topic` from the contact.html URL on every initial load.
- Accepted values are limited to the known topics used by upstream pages: 'programs', 'parties', 'memberships', 'passes', 'lap_lanes'.
- If `topic` matches one of these, the contact form's topic field is initialized with that value. If the parameter is missing or not recognized, the form uses a neutral default topic.
- This initialization happens once on page load; the value is stored in the contact-form component’s internal state.

Runtime state and URL synchronization:
- The current topic selection is maintained in the contact-form component's state.
- When the user changes the topic manually, the component can optionally update the URL's `topic` parameter via history.pushState or history.replaceState, so the current topic is reflected in the URL without reloading the page. This ensures that if the user refreshes the page or shares the URL, the selected topic is preserved.
- Submission state (e.g., 'idle', 'submitting', 'success', 'error') is held in the form component's state only and is not written to the URL.

API-driven state:
- Contact info state: On page initialization, the page calls getContactInfo once. The result is stored in a dedicated contact-info component state and used to render phone, email, address, and directions. If getContactInfo fails, the component switches to an error state for contact info (e.g., showing an error message region without any hardcoded contact details).
- Form submission state: When the user submits the form, the page calls submitContactForm with the current state values (topic, name, email, phone, message). The response (success flag and message text) is used to update the feedback area state. No persistent storage is used beyond the current page session.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact page by reading URL parameters, setting the initial topic state, and loading contact information from getContactInfo.
Displays dynamic contact details for the aquatic center, including main phone, labeled email addresses, physical address, and a directions summary, based solely on data returned from getContactInfo.

Provides a general inquiry form that allows users to select a topic, enter their name, email, optional phone, and message, and submit the inquiry through submitContactForm. Handles client-side validation, submission state, and feedback display.

Provides navigation links to related self-service pages such as pool schedules, rentals/party information, passes, memberships, and a dedicated 'View policies & FAQs' link to policies.html, enabling users to find answers without submitting the contact form.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom banded structure. Above everything, the existing site shell (utility bar + logo/nav) remains shallow. Immediately below it, the Contact page uses the contact-page-init as a hero-like introductory band spanning full width. Beneath that, a single main content band holds both the contact-info-panel and contact-form side by side within a centered, max-width container, using a 12-column grid (info on the left, form on the right). Below this main band, the self-service-links appear as a distinct full-width but center-constrained section, visually separated like a footer-adjacent resources strip. All components follow a straightforward vertical scroll with clearly separated sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom banded structure. Above everything, the existing site shell (utility bar + logo/nav) remains shallow. Immediately below it, the Contact page uses the contact-page-init as a hero-like introductory band spanning full width. Beneath that, a single main content band holds both the contact-info-panel and contact-form side by side within a centered, max-width container, using a 12-column grid (info on the left, form on the right). Below this main band, the self-service-links appear as a distinct full-width but center-constrained section, visually separated like a footer-adjacent resources strip. All components follow a straightforward vertical scroll with clearly separated sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (utility + main nav) remains shallow (~10–15% of the initial viewport height). The contact-page-init hero band occupies roughly 30–40% of the initial viewport height, giving the page title and intro copy room to breathe. The combined contact-info-panel + contact-form band fills most of the remaining above-the-fold area on desktop (~35–45% of viewport height), with more vertical space as the form grows. The self-service-links sit below this with a shorter vertical height, about the size of a compact content block (~20–30% of viewport height), acting as a lower-density, supporting band. Vertical separation between these bands follows the 32–48px spacing system, and horizontal padding inside the main content container aligns with the 12-column grid and 8–16px component padding patterns.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium, in line with the mockup. The contact-page-init hero text is large and well spaced, prioritizing scannability and welcome tone over raw information density. The contact-info-panel is denser, like a compact info card: multiple data points (phone, address, hours) grouped but clearly segmented with headings and internal spacing. The contact-form is medium density: many fields but broken into logical groups with clear vertical spacing and relaxed line-height for labels. The self-service-links section is relatively light, presenting a small set of clearly separated links or chips with generous horizontal and vertical spacing to avoid clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left logo in the site header, tracks across the horizontal nav (F-pattern), then drops into the bright contact-page-init hero band. The hero’s left-aligned page title and intro copy anchor attention first, then any supporting visual on the right. From there, the gaze naturally moves down into the main contact band, landing first on the left-aligned contact-info-panel (quick access info), then flowing right into the larger contact-form (primary task). After scanning the form, the eye continues downward to the self-service-links, perceived as secondary but helpful options. A floating chat button (from the broader design system) would sit unobtrusively near the bottom-left of the viewport, noticed peripherally after the main content is understood.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing header and nav, the Contact page opens with a bright, full-width hero band that clearly announces “Contact” and sets a friendly, family-oriented tone. This band is center-aligned within a max-width container, with the main heading and a short, readable description occupying the left two-thirds of the content area and optional supportive visuals (such as an illustration of swimmers or a badge-style graphic) on the right third. The band is tall enough to feel substantial but not overpowering, with ample white space above and below the text. Scrolling down, the hero gives way to a main contact band where the content is constrained to a wide central column that follows a 12-column grid. In this band, a compact contact-info-panel card sits on the left third of the grid, listing address, phone, hours, and directions, while on the right two-thirds, a larger contact-form panel invites users to submit inquiries. Both panels share a common horizontal baseline and are separated by a consistent gutter, making the two-column layout feel cohesive and balanced. Further down, after a comfortable vertical gap, a self-service-links section spans the width of the page with a subtle visual distinction (such as a tinted background or top border) and a centered content column. Here, key self-service options—like program schedules, membership info, rental details, and lap lane reservations—are arranged in a simple grid or horizontal list, offering alternate paths before or after using the form. Across all sections, vertical spacing (32–48px between bands, 16–24px within components) and generous line-height maintain readability and a calm, welcoming feel, even as color and graphics keep the page energetic and family-friendly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-init component is positioned as the first content band immediately below the global header and navigation. It spans the full browser width but constrains its inner content to a centered max-width container of approximately 1,120–1,280px. Vertically, this band occupies about 35–40% of the initial viewport height on desktop (with a minimum height of roughly 320–360px), creating a shallow hero feel. Inside the container, content follows a two-part layout aligned to the 12-column grid: the left 8 of 12 columns (about two-thirds of the width) host the page title (e.g., “Contact Us”) and a brief introductory paragraph, both left-aligned with generous line-height and 24–32px spacing between heading and text. The remaining right 4 columns (one-third) are reserved for an optional supporting graphic or icon cluster that ties into the aquatic theme; if no image is present, this space remains as open breathing room, further emphasizing the text. Top and bottom padding within the band follows the spacing system, roughly 48px–64px, keeping the section airy. The bottom edge of this component is followed by about 32–40px of vertical white space before the next band starts, clearly separating the hero from the main contact content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section with no special scrolling behavior; it sits beneath the fixed or static site header and scrolls away with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel sits in the main contact band directly below the contact-page-init, sharing the space with the contact-form. The entire band’s inner content is constrained to the same centered max-width container as the hero (around 1,120–1,280px), preserving alignment. Within this container, the contact-info-panel occupies the leftmost 4 columns of the 12-column grid—roughly one-third of the content width. It is rendered as a vertically oriented card starting near the left gutter, top-aligned with the contact-form on the right. The panel has internal padding of approximately 24px on all sides, and its content (section title like “Call or Visit Us”, phone number, email, physical address, hours, map/directions link) is stacked linearly from top to bottom, with 8–16px vertical spacing between items. The card’s height is shorter than or equal to the visible portion of the form; it may not stretch to match the form’s full height, which gives a visually anchored but compact feel. There is a consistent horizontal gap of about 32px–40px (equivalent to 1 column/gutter) between this panel and the contact-form to its right. The top margin between the bottom of the contact-page-init band and the top of this card is about 32–40px, maintaining comfortable separation while still reading as a continuation of the hero’s narrative. On smaller screens, this panel stacks above the contact-form, occupying 100% of the width with increased bottom margin (24–32px) before the form to keep sections distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, top-aligned with the contact-form in a shared two-column band; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form shares the main contact band with the contact-info-panel and is positioned to the right of it within the same centered max-width container. Using the 12-column grid, the form spans the remaining 8 columns—approximately two-thirds of the available width—starting just to the right of the info panel’s gutter. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the contact-info-panel, making the two components feel like a coordinated, single unit. The form appears as a large card or panel with 24–32px of internal padding, containing the form title, a brief helper line, and the input fields. Content within the form is arranged in a vertical stack: title and helper text at the top, followed by form fields grouped logically (for example: contact details, topic selection, message). Each field group is separated by about 16–24px vertical spacing, and individual fields have 8–16px spacing between them, echoing the spacing system. The submit button is placed near the bottom-right of the form panel, with at least 24px padding between the last field and the button. On desktop, the form typically extends further down than the info panel, so its bottom edge sits lower, visually reinforcing it as the primary action area. The entire main band (info panel + form) has 48–64px of top and bottom padding inside the band, creating generous white space above the form fields and below the submit button. On narrower viewports, the form shifts to full-width (100%), stacking below the contact-info-panel, but still maintains the same internal padding and vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that grows vertically with content; scrolls naturally with the page and does not overlap or float on other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The self-service-links component forms its own distinct band below the main contact band that contains the info panel and form. There is a vertical gap of roughly 40–48px between the bottom of the contact-form band and the top of this section, ensuring clear separation. The band stretches full width across the viewport, with its inner content once again constrained to the central max-width container for alignment with elements above. Vertically, this section is shorter and more compact—approximately 200–320px in height depending on the number of links—but with generous top and bottom padding (32–48px) to maintain breathing room. Within the container, a short title or prompt (e.g., “Need something specific?” or “Try these quick links”) sits at the top, centered or left-aligned depending on overall site conventions, followed by the links themselves. The link items are arranged either as a two-column list or as a horizontal row of 3–4 pill-like buttons, each occupying about 2–3 columns in the grid with consistent gaps of 16–24px between them. This layout keeps the section visually light and scannable, emphasizing that these are secondary, self-guided options rather than the primary contact route. The section bottom aligns visually with the beginning of the site’s footer or next page section, with an additional 32–48px of whitespace below if needed for separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky, full-width band that sits at the bottom of the page’s main content area and scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

name: Policies & FAQs
title: Policies & FAQs
description: Displays pool policies, safety guidelines, membership terms, and structured FAQs, with deep links into related functional pages and support for URL-based section navigation.

core_features: Fetch and display policy content (facility rules, safety guidelines, membership terms, cancellation and refund policy, waiver information) using getPoliciesContent.
Fetch and render structured FAQ sections (with questions and answers) using getFaqSections.
Support URL-based deep-linking to a specific FAQ section via the sectionId query parameter on initial page load and when navigating within the page.
Provide in-page navigation between FAQ sections, synchronized with the sectionId URL parameter.
Allow expanding and collapsing individual FAQ questions to reveal answers.
From FAQ answers, provide context-aware navigation links to related functional pages (programs.html, lap_lanes.html, passes.html, memberships.html, rentals.html) based on the relatedPage field.
Handle loading and error states for both policy content and FAQ sections without hardcoded fallback text.
user_workflows:
Workflow: User opens Policies & FAQs from footer without a sectionId parameter
1. User clicks the 'Pool Policies & FAQs' link in the site footer.
2. Browser navigates to policies.html with no sectionId in the URL.
3. Page controller parses URL (no sectionId found) and initializes with no pre-selected FAQ section.
4. Page controller calls getPoliciesContent and, in parallel, getFaqSections.
5. While data is loading, the page shows non-content loading indicators for the policy blocks and FAQ area.
6. When getPoliciesContent resolves, the policies-content component renders each policy block using the returned strings.
7. When getFaqSections resolves, the faq-section-nav renders a list of sections, and the faq-section-list renders each section with its questions collapsed by default.
8. User clicks FAQ section titles in the navigation or scrolls the page; clicking a section in the nav scrolls to that section and updates the URL to policies.html?sectionId=<sectionId>.

Workflow: User opens Policies & FAQs from a confirmation page with a specific sectionId
1. User completes an action on another page (for example, a class registration on programs.html) and clicks a link such as 'View policies and FAQs', which navigates to policies.html?sectionId=registrations.
2. Policies & FAQs page loads and reads sectionId=registrations from the query string.
3. Page controller calls getPoliciesContent and getFaqSections.
4. When getFaqSections resolves, the controller finds the FAQ section whose sectionId matches 'registrations' and sets it as the selectedSectionId in state.
5. faq-section-nav highlights the 'registrations' section as active.
6. faq-section-list automatically scrolls to that section and may expand that section’s first question, depending on configuration.
7. If the user clicks another section in the nav, selectedSectionId updates, the URL is updated to the new sectionId, and the view scrolls accordingly.

Workflow: User reads an FAQ and navigates to a related functional page
1. User expands a question inside a FAQ section.
2. faq-question-item checks the question object for a relatedPage value.
3. If relatedPage is present (e.g., 'programs', 'lap_lanes', 'passes', 'memberships', 'rentals'), faq-question-item renders a contextual link such as 'Go to related page'.
4. User clicks the contextual link.
5. click handler maps the relatedPage string to the corresponding HTML file and performs a client-side navigation (e.g., programs.html).
6. The browser loads the target page, where additional functionality specific to that page is available.

Workflow: User switches between FAQ sections using in-page navigation
1. User sees the list of FAQ sections provided by getFaqSections in the faq-section-nav component.
2. User clicks a section title.
3. faq-section-nav emits a sectionSelected event with the selected sectionId.
4. Page controller receives the event, updates selectedSectionId in state, and updates the URL to policies.html?sectionId=<selectedId> using history.replaceState or history.pushState.
5. faq-section-list scrolls to the corresponding section and ensures it is visible; it can optionally expand that section or keep the current expansion state per question.
6. If the user reloads the page or shares the URL, the current sectionId persists in the URL and will be used to restore the same section as selected on load.

Workflow: Handling data load errors
1. On page load, the controller calls getPoliciesContent and getFaqSections.
2. If getPoliciesContent fails, the controller flags a policyLoadError state and the policies-content component renders a non-hardcoded, generic error indicator (e.g., an icon or placeholder) without substituting any specific policy text.
3. If getFaqSections fails, faq-section-nav and faq-section-list render error indicators rather than any static FAQ content.
4. If one interface succeeds and the other fails, the page still shows the section that succeeded and an error placeholder for the failing part.
interactions: On page load: parse the sectionId query parameter from the URL and store it as initialSelectedSectionId; trigger parallel calls to getPoliciesContent and getFaqSections.

On policies content load success: bind the returned strings (poolAndFacilityRules, safetyGuidelines, membershipTerms, cancellationAndRefundPolicy, waiverInformation) to their corresponding policy blocks in the policies-content component.

On FAQ sections load success: bind the returned array to faq-section-nav and faq-section-list; if initialSelectedSectionId exists and matches a sectionId in the array, mark that section as selected and scroll it into view.

On FAQ section navigation click: user clicks a section name in faq-section-nav; event handler updates selectedSectionId state, updates the URL query parameter (sectionId), and requests faq-section-list to scroll to that section anchor.
On FAQ question click: user clicks a question row; faq-question-item toggles expanded/collapsed state for that question’s answer, without affecting other questions unless configured otherwise.

On related page link click from a question: user clicks the dynamic link rendered when question.relatedPage is present; event handler maps relatedPage to a target file (programs.html, lap_lanes.html, passes.html, memberships.html, rentals.html) and triggers a navigation to that page.

On browser back/forward navigation: browser changes the URL; page controller listens to popstate events, re-parses sectionId from the URL, updates selectedSectionId, and instructs faq-section-list to scroll/highlight the appropriate section without re-calling the APIs unless needed.

On manual URL editing: if the user manually changes the URL to include a different sectionId and reloads, the controller uses this new sectionId as the initialSelectedSectionId and behaves as in the deep-link workflow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is the currently selected FAQ section, tied to the sectionId query parameter in the URL. On initial load, the page controller reads window.location.search, extracts the sectionId value (if present), and stores it as initialSelectedSectionId. After getFaqSections resolves, if a section with a matching sectionId exists, selectedSectionId is set to that value, the corresponding section is marked active in faq-section-nav, and faq-section-list scrolls to that section's anchor. If no sectionId is present or no matching section exists, selectedSectionId remains null or defaults to the first section without modifying the URL. When the user clicks on a FAQ section in faq-section-nav, selectedSectionId is updated and the URL is updated to policies.html?sectionId=<selectedSectionId> using the History API, ensuring future reloads or shared links restore the same context. The sectionId parameter is never stored in localStorage; it is always derived from the URL. The load state for each interface (policies vs FAQs) is tracked separately (e.g., policiesLoading, faqsLoading, policiesError, faqsError) but does not affect the URL. Navigating away to related functional pages does not require additional URL parameters from this page; the navigation is performed solely based on the relatedPage mapping to the respective HTML files.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching, URL parameter parsing, global state (selected FAQ section, loading and error state), and communication between all child components on the Policies & FAQs page.
event_handlers: onLoad: Parse URL query for sectionId, store as initialSelectedSectionId, then call getPoliciesContent and getFaqSections in parallel.
onPoliciesContentSuccess: Update policiesData state and notify policies-content component to render content.
onPoliciesContentError: Set policiesError state and notify policies-content component to display an error indicator.

onFaqSectionsSuccess: Update faqSections state; if initialSelectedSectionId matches any sectionId, set selectedSectionId to that section; otherwise leave null or assign a default; notify faq-section-nav and faq-section-list to render.
onFaqSectionsError: Set faqsError state and notify faq-section-nav and faq-section-list to display error indicators.

onSectionSelected(sectionId): Update selectedSectionId state, update URL query parameter sectionId via history.pushState or history.replaceState, and instruct faq-section-list to scroll to the corresponding section anchor.

onPopState: When browser back/forward is used, re-parse sectionId from URL, update selectedSectionId, and notify faq-section-nav and faq-section-list to update active state/scroll position without refetching data.

components.functionality: Renders the main policy content blocks (facility rules, safety guidelines, membership terms, cancellation and refund policy, waiver information) using the dynamic strings from getPoliciesContent. May present each area as a separate, optionally collapsible section.
event_handlers: onToggleSection(sectionKey): Toggle expanded/collapsed state for a specific policy block (e.g., facility rules vs membership terms) while keeping the bound content unchanged.
onPoliciesContentError: Display a generic, non-textual error placeholder when policy content cannot be loaded, without substituting any hardcoded policy text.

components.functionality: Displays a list of FAQ sections for quick navigation, highlights the currently selected section, and emits events when the user chooses a section.
event_handlers: onSectionClick(sectionId): Emit sectionSelected event to the page controller so it can update selectedSectionId and the URL query parameter, then request faq-section-list to scroll to the section.
onFaqSectionsError: Show a non-hardcoded error indicator if FAQ sections cannot be loaded.
onSelectedSectionIdChange: Update visual highlighting of the active section to match the selectedSectionId coming from the controller.

components.functionality: Renders all FAQ sections returned by getFaqSections, each with its own anchor (based on sectionId) and a list of FAQ questions. Handles scrolling to and focusing a section when selectedSectionId changes.
event_handlers: onRender: For each section, create a DOM anchor using its sectionId (e.g., id attribute) to support scroll-to-section behavior.
onSelectedSectionIdChange: Scroll to the section whose sectionId matches selectedSectionId and optionally bring it into focus for accessibility.
onFaqSectionsError: If faqSectionsError is true, render an error placeholder instead of FAQ sections.

components.functionality: Represents a single FAQ section within the list, with a title and a collection of FAQ questions. Provides optional section-level expand/collapse control.
data_binding: selectedSectionId (to determine whether this panel is 'active')
event_handlers: onSectionHeaderClick: Optionally toggle the entire section’s collapsed/expanded state; may also emit a sectionSelected event via the parent that delegates to the page controller.
onQuestionToggle(questionIndex): Propagate the toggle event to the child faq-question-item component or manage open/closed state for each question within this section.

components.functionality: Renders a single FAQ question and its answer from the getFaqSections data, with an expandable answer area and an optional link to a related functional page based on the relatedPage field.
event_handlers: onQuestionClick: Toggle the expanded/collapsed state of this question’s answer area.

onRelatedPageLinkClick: When question.relatedPage is defined, map its value to a target page and navigate. Mapping rules:
  - If relatedPage === 'programs', navigate to programs.html.
  - If relatedPage === 'lap_lanes', navigate to lap_lanes.html.
  - If relatedPage === 'passes', navigate to passes.html.
  - If relatedPage === 'memberships', navigate to memberships.html.
  - If relatedPage === 'parties' or 'rentals', navigate to rentals.html.
  If relatedPage is undefined or does not match a known value, no navigation link is rendered.

components.functionality: Abstracts reading and writing of the sectionId query parameter so components can keep selectedSectionId in sync with the URL without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse window.location.search to extract sectionId and return it to the page controller as initialSelectedSectionId.
onSelectedSectionIdChange: Update the URL’s sectionId query parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState without reloading the page.
onPopState: Listen for browser navigation events, re-parse sectionId, and notify the page controller of the new value.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid. The hero section shows four equal-width columns (each effectively spanning 3 of 12 columns). Content is centered within a max‑width container while some elements (hero images) extend nearly full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom banded structure: global utility bar + header (with logo/nav) at the very top, followed by the sitewide full-width hero band (likely promoting programs or seasonal info). Immediately beneath the hero, this Policies & FAQs page begins with a centered page-intro band (title and short explanatory copy). Below that, the main content is organized into two major stacked bands: a policies band (rich text, guidelines, membership terms) and then an FAQs band. Within the FAQs band, a horizontal in-page FAQ nav runs across the top, and the different FAQ sections stack vertically below it. All of this sits inside a center-constrained content container aligned to the implicit 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom banded structure: global utility bar + header (with logo/nav) at the very top, followed by the sitewide full-width hero band (likely promoting programs or seasonal info). Immediately beneath the hero, this Policies & FAQs page begins with a centered page-intro band (title and short explanatory copy). Below that, the main content is organized into two major stacked bands: a policies band (rich text, guidelines, membership terms) and then an FAQs band. Within the FAQs band, a horizontal in-page FAQ nav runs across the top, and the different FAQ sections stack vertically below it. All of this sits inside a center-constrained content container aligned to the implicit 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (utility bar + logo/nav) remains shallow (~10–15% of initial viewport height), and the full-width hero still claims the main above-the-fold emphasis (~40–50% of initial viewport), consistent with the mockup. This page’s own content starts just below the hero: the intro band is moderately tall but mostly text. The policies band gets a generous vertical allocation for dense text while maintaining comfortable margins (top/bottom spacing ~32–48px). The FAQ band is similar in vertical size but broken into collapsible chunks (questions) to keep perceived density low. Horizontally, all page-specific content sits inside a centered max-width container (~1120–1200px), generally spanning about 8–10 of 12 columns, with white margins on left and right. The in-page FAQ nav occupies the full width of this container but only a shallow vertical slice, leaving most of the space to the FAQ content beneath.
layout_strategies.content_density: Honor the mockup’s low-to-medium density: text sections (policies and answers) use generous line-height, comfortable paragraph spacing, and avoid edge-to-edge lines. The policies section is the densest area but is broken into clearly labeled subsections with ample breathing room between headings and paragraphs. The FAQ section distributes density by using collapsible question items: only a subset of answers is visible at once. Interactive elements (nav pills, question headers) use at least 8–16px padding per side, and vertical separation between major sections (intro, policies, FAQ nav, FAQ content) follows the 32–48px section margins described in the spacing system.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows the same F-pattern as the mockup: logo at top-left, across the main nav, then down into the colorful hero band. After scanning the hero, the gaze drops into the Policies & FAQs page area, starting with a clear page title and intro text centered in the content column. From there, the eye moves downward into the policies text, reading left-aligned, editorial-style content. As the user scrolls further, the colorful, horizontally arranged FAQ section nav catches attention, encouraging a lateral scan across FAQ categories.
